title: Cold outreach that doesn't sound like cold outreach
use_when: Reaching out to a stranger whose work you actually respect and you want them to read past the first sentence.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft a 100-word cold outreach email to [RECIPIENT] that gets read past the first sentence by sounding like a real person who has actually engaged with their work, not a sender who scraped their LinkedIn. Success means a reply, not a polite ignore.

## AGENT
You are a writer who has sent hundreds of cold emails and learned what gets replied to. You know that flattery reads as sales, that "I came across your work" is a tell, and that the opening line either earns the next sentence or doesn't.

## SITUATION
The recipient: [RECIPIENT - name, role, what they do publicly]
What I actually engaged with: [SPECIFIC PIECE OF THEIR WORK - article, talk, project, with a real detail]
Why I'm reaching out: [REAL REASON - not "to connect"]
What I'm asking for: [SPECIFIC ASK - 15 minutes, an introduction, an opinion]
What I bring: [WHY THEY MIGHT CARE]

## TASK
1. Open with a specific reference to their work that proves I actually engaged.
2. State why I'm writing in one sentence.
3. State the specific ask in one sentence with a low-cost option.
4. Close with one line that respects their time.

## THINK
Before writing: (a) name what the recipient most likely cares about that the user did not say explicitly, (b) name the line in the draft most likely to be misread, (c) name the response the recipient is most likely to send back. Use this to shape the draft.

## RULES
- Do NOT use "I came across your work."
- Do NOT use "I hope this finds you well."
- Do NOT ask for a "quick chat" without a specific topic.
- Maximum 100 words.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Would the recipient be able to respond with a decision in one reply, or would they have to ask a clarifying question? (2) Did I cut every sentence that softens without changing meaning? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
The email only. No subject line unless asked.
notes: Run a sanity check: would a stranger in this person's inbox at 11pm read past your second sentence? If no, recut.

title: Cold outreach subject line A/B generator
use_when: You have the body of a cold email and need 5 subject-line options that don't trigger spam-folder reflexes.
prompt:
I have this cold email body: [PASTE BODY]

Generate 5 subject lines optimized for open rate from a sender the recipient does not know. For each, predict in 5-7 words why it works.

Constraints:
- Maximum 7 words each.
- No exclamation points.
- No "quick question."
- No "circling back."
- One must include the recipient's specific work.
- One must be a clean direct ask.
- One must be intentionally low-key.

After the 5, rank them by likely open rate and explain the top pick.
notes: The 'intentionally low-key' option often wins. Hype subject lines burn trust before the open.

title: Internal email that doesn't waste a senior person's time
use_when: You need to email someone two levels up who reads 200 emails a day.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft an email to [SENIOR PERSON] that gets a decision or reaction in under 60 seconds of their time. Success means they reply with an answer, not "let's discuss."

## AGENT
You are a senior individual contributor who has learned how the calendar of an executive works. You front-load the ask, structure for skim, and never make them scroll.

## SITUATION
The recipient: [SENIOR PERSON - role, what they care about, their inbox volume]
What I'm asking: [SPECIFIC ASK]
The context they need to make the call: [MINIMUM CONTEXT]
What I've already done: [PRIOR WORK]
My preferred answer: [WHAT I HOPE FOR]

## TASK
1. Subject line: action requested + topic.
2. Line 1: the ask in one sentence, with the decision they need to make.
3. Lines 2-4: the minimum context, in bullets.
4. Line 5: my recommendation and why.
5. Line 6: what happens if they don't reply by [DATE].

## THINK
Before writing: (a) name what the recipient most likely cares about that the user did not say explicitly, (b) name the line in the draft most likely to be misread, (c) name the response the recipient is most likely to send back. Use this to shape the draft.

## RULES
- Do NOT open with "I hope you're well" or backstory.
- Do NOT use more than 100 words total.
- Do NOT bury the ask.
- Do NOT use bold/italic for emphasis - structure does the work.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Would the recipient be able to respond with a decision in one reply, or would they have to ask a clarifying question? (2) Did I cut every sentence that softens without changing meaning? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Subject line / body. Under 100 words.
notes: The 'what happens if they don't reply' line forces useful default-setting and often unsticks decisions.

title: Status update email people will actually read
use_when: Sending a weekly status update to a stakeholder who skims.
prompt:
Draft a weekly status update email to [STAKEHOLDER] covering [PROJECT/AREA].

Structure exactly this way:
- TL;DR: one sentence stating whether we're on track, ahead, or behind.
- This week: max 3 bullets. Each starts with a verb. Each names a concrete deliverable, not "worked on."
- Next week: max 3 bullets.
- Risks: 1-2 items with severity (low/medium/high). Skip if nothing material.
- Needs from you: explicit ask or "none."

Rules:
- No "we" without naming who.
- No "ongoing." Replace with date the thing ships.
- Maximum 200 words.

What I did this week: [LIST]
Coming up next: [LIST]
Risks: [LIST]
Asks: [LIST OR NONE]
notes: The 'no ongoing' rule alone eliminates 30% of waste. Date everything or omit it.

title: Follow-up nudge that doesn't read as nagging
use_when: It's been 5+ days since you sent something, you need a reply, and you want to nudge without being annoying.
prompt:
I sent [ORIGINAL THING] to [RECIPIENT] on [DATE]. I need a reply on [SPECIFIC ASK].

Draft a follow-up that is brief, respectful, and gives them an out without dropping the ask.

Structure:
- Subject: "Following up on [ORIGINAL THING]"
- Line 1: reference the original send with a one-line summary so they don't have to scroll.
- Line 2: the specific question I need answered, restated in one sentence.
- Line 3: an explicit out ("If this isn't a priority right now, just say so and I'll plan around it.").
- Line 4: a soft deadline ("Hoping to lock this by [DATE] either way.").
- Sign-off.

Maximum 80 words. No "just checking in." No "wanted to bump this." No "in case it got buried".
notes: The 'explicit out' line at line 3 is what makes the nudge land. People reply when given permission to say no.

title: Apology email that does not over-apologize
use_when: You messed up something material at work and need to send an email that acknowledges it without spiraling.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft an apology email to [RECIPIENT] about [WHAT WENT WRONG] that takes ownership, names the fix, and reestablishes trust without theatrical self-flagellation.

## AGENT
You are a professional who has made and recovered from real mistakes. You know that excessive apology shifts the emotional labor to the recipient and that the fastest path back to trust is "I broke it / here's why / here's the fix / here's how I'll prevent it."

## SITUATION
The recipient: [RECIPIENT]
What went wrong: [WHAT]
Why it went wrong: [REAL CAUSE]
What I'm doing to fix it: [FIX]
What I'm doing to prevent recurrence: [PREVENTION]
What I am NOT going to do: [BOUNDARIES]

## TASK
1. Sentence 1: state what went wrong, in active voice. ("I sent the wrong figures in Tuesday's report.")
2. Sentence 2: the real cause, briefly. No excuses, no defensiveness.
3. Sentence 3: what I'm doing about it, with a timeline.
4. Sentence 4: what I'm changing to prevent recurrence.
5. Sentence 5: short, sincere closing. No "again, so sorry."

## THINK
Before writing: (a) name what the recipient most likely cares about that the user did not say explicitly, (b) name the line in the draft most likely to be misread, (c) name the response the recipient is most likely to send back. Use this to shape the draft.

## RULES
- Do NOT use "I sincerely apologize."
- Do NOT explain at length. The recipient cares about the fix, not the why.
- Do NOT promise it will "never happen again."
- Do NOT use passive voice ("mistakes were made").

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Would the recipient be able to respond with a decision in one reply, or would they have to ask a clarifying question? (2) Did I cut every sentence that softens without changing meaning? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
The email. Under 100 words.
notes: The hardest constraint is brevity. Long apology emails make recipients more uncomfortable, not less.

title: Reply to a hostile email without escalating
use_when: Someone sent you a sharp or hostile message and you need to respond without dropping to their level or rolling over.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft a reply to [HOSTILE EMAIL] that addresses the substance, names the tone professionally, and either resolves the issue or invites a real conversation - without escalating and without capitulating.

## AGENT
You are someone who has been in this situation before. You distinguish between hostile tone and substantive disagreement and respond to each separately. You do not pretend the tone didn't happen, and you do not match it.

## SITUATION
The original email: [PASTE]
What about it was hostile: [TONE ELEMENTS]
What's substantively legitimate in it: [LEGITIMATE POINTS]
What's not legitimate: [UNFAIR POINTS]
My relationship to this person: [RELATIONSHIP]
What outcome I want: [OUTCOME]

## TASK
1. Acknowledge the substantive points that are fair, briefly.
2. Address the points that are unfair with facts, not feelings.
3. Name the tone once, neutrally, without making it the main subject.
4. Propose the concrete next step (meeting, deadline, decision).

## THINK
Before writing: (a) name what the recipient most likely cares about that the user did not say explicitly, (b) name the line in the draft most likely to be misread, (c) name the response the recipient is most likely to send back. Use this to shape the draft.

## RULES
- Do NOT lead with the tone complaint.
- Do NOT use "I think" or "I feel" when stating facts.
- Do NOT match their tone.
- Do NOT apologize for things that aren't your fault.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Would the recipient be able to respond with a decision in one reply, or would they have to ask a clarifying question? (2) Did I cut every sentence that softens without changing meaning? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
The email. Under 200 words.
notes: The 'name the tone once' constraint is what differentiates this from either capitulation or escalation. Once is enough.

title: Email asking your manager for a raise
use_when: You're requesting a compensation conversation and want to set it up well, not blow it before the meeting.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft a 100-word email to [MANAGER] requesting a compensation conversation, framed in a way that makes saying yes to the meeting easy and the conversation itself productive. Success means a calendar invite, not "let's talk soon."

## AGENT
You are a professional asking for what you deserve, calmly and from evidence. You know the email's job is to land the meeting, not win the case. You do not over-explain.

## SITUATION
My role: [ROLE]
How long I've been here: [TENURE]
Last comp adjustment: [WHEN]
What's changed since: [SCOPE / IMPACT / RESPONSIBILITIES]
What I'll bring to the meeting: [EVIDENCE - 2-3 concrete items]
Relationship with this manager: [RELATIONSHIP]

## TASK
1. State the ask: a conversation about compensation.
2. State what's changed since last comp adjustment.
3. Offer a 30-minute slot range, not "when is good."
4. Note you're prepared to discuss specifics in the meeting.

## THINK
Before writing: (a) name what the recipient most likely cares about that the user did not say explicitly, (b) name the line in the draft most likely to be misread, (c) name the response the recipient is most likely to send back. Use this to shape the draft.

## RULES
- Do NOT make the case in the email - the email is just for the meeting.
- Do NOT use "I think I deserve."
- Do NOT use "I've been doing a lot lately."
- Do NOT hedge.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Would the recipient be able to respond with a decision in one reply, or would they have to ask a clarifying question? (2) Did I cut every sentence that softens without changing meaning? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
The email. Under 100 words.
notes: The 'don't make the case in the email' rule is the lesson. Long pre-meeting emails make managers defensive before the conversation starts.

title: Thank-you note that is actually about them, not you
use_when: Writing a thank-you to someone who helped you in a real way (interview, mentorship, recommendation).
prompt:
Draft a thank-you note to [PERSON] for [SPECIFIC HELP THEY GAVE].

The note should be:
- 4-6 sentences.
- Specific about what they did and why it mattered (not "thanks for your time").
- About one specific thing they said or did that changed something for me.
- Closed with a concrete next step (not "let's stay in touch").

Rules:
- Do NOT start with "I just wanted to say thank you."
- Do NOT use "took the time."
- Do NOT use "appreciate."
- Reference one specific moment from our interaction.

Context I'll give you: [PASTE NOTES ON WHAT HAPPENED]
notes: The 'one specific moment' rule is what makes the note memorable. Generic thanks reads like a template.

title: Decline an invitation without burning the relationship
use_when: You've been invited to something - speaking gig, advisory role, board, dinner - and the right answer is no.
prompt:
Draft a decline to [INVITATION] from [INVITER] that protects the relationship and is final.

Structure:
- Sentence 1: thank them specifically for the consideration, name the invitation.
- Sentence 2: decline cleanly. No "unfortunately." No "I wish I could."
- Sentence 3: one-line real reason, not "scheduling conflict." If you can't share the real reason, say "I can't commit to it well right now."
- Sentence 4: an alternative if you want to offer one - an intro, a future window, or a person they should ask instead.
- Sentence 5: close.

Rules:
- Maximum 80 words.
- No "Best wishes for your event/initiative."
- No "Maybe in the future" unless you mean it specifically.
- Do not open a renegotiation.
notes: The 'don't open renegotiation' rule is the discipline. Soft declines invite re-asks.

title: Inbox triage protocol applied to a flooded morning
use_when: You return to 80+ unread messages and need a fast triage protocol so you respond to what matters and ignore what doesn't.
prompt:
## MISSION
Process my inbox of [N] unread messages and produce a triage report that tells me which 3-5 emails to respond to in the next 30 minutes, which to schedule for later, which to delegate, and which to ignore. Success means I leave inbox-triage mode in 10 minutes with a clear action list.

## AGENT
You are an executive assistant with a strong sense of what actually requires the user's attention vs. what is performative copying-in.

## SITUATION
Email snapshot: [PASTE SUBJECT LINES + SENDERS, OR DESCRIBE]
My priorities today: [TOP 3 PRIORITIES]
People whose messages always require fast response: [LIST]
People I am happy to defer: [LIST]
Topics I am happy to defer or ignore: [LIST]

## TASK
1. Categorize each email into: RESPOND-NOW / SCHEDULE / DELEGATE / IGNORE / DELETE.
2. For RESPOND-NOW (max 5), give me the one-sentence response I should send, ready to copy.
3. For SCHEDULE, name the time block (today/this week/next week).
4. For DELEGATE, name who and what to send them.
5. Flag the one email I'm likely to misclassify - the one that looks low-priority but isn't, or looks urgent but isn't.

## THINK
Before writing: (a) name what the recipient most likely cares about that the user did not say explicitly, (b) name the line in the draft most likely to be misread, (c) name the response the recipient is most likely to send back. Use this to shape the draft.

## RULES
- Do NOT default to RESPOND-NOW. The bar is high.
- Do NOT make the IGNORE pile timid. Ignoring is a real option.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Would the recipient be able to respond with a decision in one reply, or would they have to ask a clarifying question? (2) Did I cut every sentence that softens without changing meaning? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Triage table / draft replies / misclassification flag.
notes: The 'flag the one I'd misclassify' rule catches the buried-lede email that ruins a Thursday afternoon.

title: Drafting a Slack message that doesn't need 3 follow-ups
use_when: You're about to send a Slack message and want it to land cleanly so the conversation goes one round, not five.
prompt:
I am about to send a Slack message to [RECIPIENT/CHANNEL] about [TOPIC].

Help me draft it so the recipient can respond in one reply, not three.

Structure:
- Open with the ask or update in one line.
- Provide the context they need, no more.
- State the response I'm hoping for (decision / acknowledgment / action / FYI only).
- End with a deadline or "no rush."

Rules:
- Maximum 4 lines including spacing.
- No "Hey! quick question" preamble.
- No "lmk" - replace with the specific ask.
- If this needs >4 lines, it's not a Slack message - it's an email or a meeting.

What I want to send: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE]
notes: The 'if this needs >4 lines' constraint is the channel-choice forcing function. Slack is for things that fit in 4 lines.

title: Diplomatic 'no' to a peer asking for unscoped help
use_when: A peer asks for help with vague scope ('Can you take a look at this?') and you need to decline or scope-narrow without offending.
prompt:
A peer has asked: "[PASTE THEIR ASK]"

Draft a response that either declines or sharply scopes their ask.

Options to consider:
Option A (decline): Acknowledge the ask, decline cleanly, offer one alternative (a person, a resource, a future window).
Option B (scope): Acknowledge the ask, ask 2 specific scoping questions, and propose a small first commitment that's bounded.

Pick the better option for this situation and write it. Maximum 80 words.

Rules:
- Do NOT agree to "take a look" without scope.
- Do NOT promise more than I will deliver.
- Do not over-apologize.

Context I want you to know: [RELATIONSHIP / WHY I CAN'T DO THIS / WHAT I WOULD DO IF I COULD]
notes: The Option A vs Option B forking is the value here. Most replies to vague asks default to vague yes; this forces a real choice.

title: Reaching out to someone you ghosted
use_when: You owe someone a reply that's now weeks late and you want to re-engage without the cringe.
prompt:
I owe [PERSON] a reply on [THREAD] that I let drop [TIME AGO]. The reason I dropped it: [REAL REASON - busy, lost interest, didn't know how to respond, embarrassed].

Draft a message that gets the relationship un-stuck.

Structure:
- One sentence acknowledgment of the lag. Brief. Direct. Not over-apologetic.
- One sentence stating where I am with the original ask now.
- One sentence proposing a concrete next step or final answer.
- Close.

Rules:
- Maximum 70 words.
- No "Apologies for the late reply" as the opener.
- No "Life got busy."
- No long explanation. The recipient does not need the story.
notes: The 'no long explanation' rule is the discipline. The recipient mostly wants the un-stuck, not the why.

title: Group thread cleanup message
use_when: A group thread (email, Slack) has spiraled across many topics and needs a clean reset.
prompt:
This group thread with [PEOPLE] has covered [TOPIC]. The thread has spiraled. I need to send a clean reset.

Draft a single message that:
1. Summarizes where we ended up in 2 lines.
2. Lists the open decisions still needed, with who needs to make each.
3. Proposes the next medium (sync meeting, doc, async by [DATE]).
4. Closes the side-thread topics that are pulling attention.

Rules:
- No "Just to summarize" - just summarize.
- Maximum 150 words.
- Side topics get one line each, with a recommendation (spin off / drop / defer).

What's been discussed: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE THE MESS]
notes: This template alone saves more time than any other in this category. Group threads compound disorder; one well-timed reset clears it.

title: Introductory email connecting two people
use_when: You're double opt-in introducing two people and want the intro itself to be useful, not just a hand-off.
prompt:
Draft a double opt-in email introducing [PERSON A] to [PERSON B].

Structure:
- Subject: "[A NAME] <> [B NAME] - [TOPIC]"
- Line 1: name the topic and why I'm making the intro.
- Lines 2-3: 1-2 sentences on Person A - what they do, what's relevant about them for B.
- Lines 4-5: 1-2 sentences on Person B - what they do, what's relevant for A.
- Line 6: name the specific reason to talk - not "you two should know each other."
- Line 7: hand off ("Taking myself off this thread - happy connecting!").

Context:
Person A: [A - role, interest, what they need]
Person B: [B - role, interest, what they can offer]
Specific reason for intro: [SHARED INTEREST OR ASK]

Rules:
- Maximum 120 words.
- Make the specific reason for the intro the load-bearing line.
notes: Most intros fail at line 6. A specific shared interest beats 'you'll get along.'

title: Out-of-office that does not waste senders' time
use_when: Setting an OOO and you want it to actually deflect non-urgent asks instead of just signaling 'I'm away.'
prompt:
Draft an out-of-office reply for [DATES] given:
- Why I'm out: [REASON - vacation, conference, parental leave, none of your business]
- Whether I'll check email: [CHECKING / NOT CHECKING / EMERGENCIES ONLY]
- Who covers for me: [COVER - name + topic they can help with]
- What kind of issue should escalate: [WHAT REACHES ME]

Structure:
- Line 1: when I'm back.
- Line 2: what to do for [SPECIFIC TOPIC] - name the coverer.
- Line 3: what counts as urgent enough to flag - and how to do it.
- Line 4: brief closing.

Rules:
- Maximum 60 words.
- Do not say "I'll respond when I return" - that's the default; don't waste a line.
- Do not over-apologize for being out.
- Make the "what to do instead" the load-bearing line.
notes: The 'what counts as urgent' line is what makes this useful. Without it, senders escalate everything or nothing.

title: Email re-asking after a vague yes
use_when: Someone responded to your ask with 'happy to help' or similar but never followed through, and you need to convert the soft yes to action.
prompt:
[PERSON] said [SOFT YES - e.g., "Happy to help when you have something specific!"] on [DATE]. They have not followed through. I need to convert this to action without losing the relationship.

Draft a follow-up that:
1. References their offer in one line.
2. Makes the ask SPECIFIC, BOUNDED, and EASY (a 15-minute call on X; review of a 1-page doc; intro to one specific person).
3. Proposes specific times or a specific deliverable.
4. Offers an out ("If your bandwidth is now different than when you offered, no worries - just say so").

Rules:
- Maximum 100 words.
- Do not over-thank them for the original offer.
- The ask must be smaller than what they generally said yes to.
notes: The 'ask smaller' rule is the conversion trick. People honor soft yeses when the actual ask is concrete and 15 minutes.

title: Drafting a difficult message to a family member
use_when: Family communication that's not casual - you need to say something hard without making it worse, but it's not a work setting.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft a message to [FAMILY MEMBER] about [DIFFICULT TOPIC] that says what I actually need to say, holds my position, and leaves room for them to respond without forcing them into a corner.

## AGENT
You are a careful, warm communicator who has been through hard family conversations. You understand that family communication is not professional communication - emotional reality matters more than efficiency. You also understand that vagueness is not kindness.

## SITUATION
Family member: [WHO - relationship, communication style]
The difficult thing: [WHAT]
History: [PRIOR ATTEMPTS, PRIOR CONFLICTS]
What I want: [SPECIFIC OUTCOME]
What I'll accept: [BACKSTOP]
What I will not accept: [LINE]
My emotional reality right now: [HOW I'M FEELING - this informs tone]

## TASK
1. Open with one line of acknowledgment - of them, the relationship, or the difficulty.
2. State what I need to say, directly, in 2-3 sentences. No softening that changes the meaning.
3. Name what I want from them. Specific. Not "I just want you to understand."
4. State what I will do if my ask isn't met. Calm. Not a threat. Just an honest description of the next chapter.
5. Close with one line that leaves the door open if I want it open, closed if I don't.

## THINK
Before writing: (a) name what the recipient most likely cares about that the user did not say explicitly, (b) name the line in the draft most likely to be misread, (c) name the response the recipient is most likely to send back. Use this to shape the draft.

## RULES
- Do NOT use "I feel like" when you mean "I feel."
- Do NOT use "I'm not trying to..." - just don't do the thing.
- Do NOT include lines whose only purpose is to soften.
- Do NOT use "with all due respect" or "no offense."

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Would the recipient be able to respond with a decision in one reply, or would they have to ask a clarifying question? (2) Did I cut every sentence that softens without changing meaning? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
The message. Length to fit the situation, but every sentence must earn its place.
notes: This template requires more pre-thinking than most. The SITUATION block is where the work happens.

title: Voicemail or audio-message script under 30 seconds
use_when: You're leaving a voicemail or sending a voice note and need to be brief and clear because nobody plays back voicemails twice.
prompt:
Draft a voicemail/voice-message script under 30 seconds.

Recipient: [WHO]
Purpose: [WHY YOU'RE CALLING]
The ask or message: [WHAT]
Your callback info (if applicable): [PHONE/CHANNEL]

Structure (max 30 seconds = ~60-75 words read aloud):
- Sentence 1: who I am + why I'm calling.
- Sentence 2: the ask or message.
- Sentence 3: what you want them to do.
- Sentence 4: callback info.

Rules:
- Conversational, not formal.
- No "Hope you're well."
- No rambling preamble.
- If it can't fit in 30 seconds, it should be a text or email - tell me.
notes: The fallback at the end ('tell me if this should be text') is the discipline. Many voicemails should have been texts.

title: Birthday or condolence message that doesn't sound generic
use_when: Sending a personal message at a milestone (birthday, illness, loss) and you want it to actually land, not read as Hallmark.
prompt:
I need to send a [BIRTHDAY / CONDOLENCE / OTHER MILESTONE] message to [PERSON].

About them: [SPECIFIC THINGS - what they care about, what I know about them, our shared history]
The occasion: [WHAT]
My relationship to them: [CLOSE / MEDIUM / DISTANT]
Length: [DESIRED LENGTH - short text vs longer message]

Produce 3 versions:
1. The version that's specific to them - references something real.
2. The version that's warm but light - works if I want to keep it brief.
3. The version that's longer, deeper - for if our relationship warrants it.

For each, predict in one sentence what it conveys.

Rules:
- No "thinking of you in this difficult time" boilerplate.
- No "happy birthday wishes" - just "happy birthday."
- Reference one specific thing about them.
- For condolence: never say "they're in a better place" unless I asked you to.
notes: The 'specific thing about them' is the load-bearing element. Without it, any of these reads like a template.

title: Professional intro line for a profile, bio, or signature
use_when: Writing a 1-2 sentence professional intro for a bio, signature, or short profile that actually says what you do.
prompt:
Write 3 versions of a 1-2 sentence professional intro for me.

About me: [WHAT I DO + RELEVANT BACKGROUND + ANYTHING DISTINCT]
Where this will appear: [LINKEDIN / EMAIL SIG / WEBSITE BIO / CONFERENCE BIO]
Audience: [WHO WILL READ THIS]

Produce:
1. The straightforward version - what I do and for whom.
2. The version that names what's distinct about my approach.
3. The version that's specific enough to feel like a person, not a role.

Rules:
- No "passionate about."
- No "results-driven."
- No "seasoned professional."
- Maximum 30 words each.
- The third version should sound like something a friend would say about me, not a recruiter.
notes: Version 3 is the goal. Most professional bios read like job postings; the best read like a friend describing you in a sentence.

title: Pre-meeting message setting the agenda
use_when: You're about to lead a meeting and want to send a short pre-read so people show up prepared.
prompt:
Draft a pre-meeting message for [MEETING TOPIC] with [ATTENDEES] on [DATE].

Structure:
- Goal of the meeting in 1 sentence.
- 3 agenda items, each with one-line context.
- What each attendee should think about beforehand (max 2 lines).
- What I will NOT cover in this meeting.
- Decision(s) I need from this meeting.

Rules:
- Maximum 150 words.
- No "Looking forward to chatting!"
- The "decisions needed" is the load-bearing element.
- If there are no decisions, this should be an email, not a meeting - tell me.
notes: The 'should this be a meeting' kicker at the end alone saves hours per month.

title: Post-meeting recap with actions and owners
use_when: You ran a meeting and want a recap that turns conversation into commitments.
prompt:
Produce a post-meeting recap from these notes: [PASTE NOTES OR DESCRIBE]

Meeting: [NAME, DATE, ATTENDEES]

Structure:
**Decisions made:**
- [DECISION]: [BRIEF CONTEXT]

**Action items:**
- [WHO] will [WHAT] by [WHEN].

**Open questions / parking lot:**
- [QUESTION]: [WHO IS OWNING IT]

**Next sync:**
- [DATE if set, otherwise "TBD"]

Rules:
- Every action item must have a name and a date. "TBD" is acceptable only with a date for when TBD becomes a date.
- Do NOT include conversation summary that isn't a decision or action.
- Maximum 250 words total.
notes: Recaps without dates are aspirations, not commitments. The 'TBD-by-date' rule is the discipline.

title: One-line message escalating an issue to leadership
use_when: You need to flag a real problem to a leader, in one message, and have them take it seriously.
prompt:
Draft a one-message escalation to [LEADER] about [ISSUE].

Structure:
- Sentence 1: what's happening, in active voice. ("The release scheduled for Friday is going to slip.")
- Sentence 2: why it matters. ("We committed this to [CUSTOMER/STAKEHOLDER] and the slip is two weeks, not days.")
- Sentence 3: what I'm doing about it. ("I'm pulling [TEAM] together at 3 today and have a recovery plan by EOD.")
- Sentence 4: what I need from them. ("Need your decision by tomorrow morning on whether to renegotiate the deadline or push to recover - I'll come with a recommendation.")
- Sentence 5: closing, calm.

Rules:
- Maximum 80 words.
- No "wanted to flag" or "heads up" - state the thing.
- The "what I need from them" must be a specific decision or action, not "thoughts".
notes: Sentence 4 is the discipline. Escalation without an ask is just news.

title: Recruiter outreach reply when you're not looking but might be later
use_when: A recruiter contacted you about a role and you want to reply in a way that keeps the door open without misleading.
prompt:
A recruiter has reached out about [ROLE] at [COMPANY]. I'm not actively looking but I might be later.

Draft a reply that:
1. Thanks them, briefly.
2. Names that I'm not actively looking right now.
3. Mentions what kinds of roles WOULD get my attention.
4. Offers to stay in touch if they have something matching that later.
5. Does NOT lead them on.

Rules:
- Maximum 80 words.
- No "I'll definitely keep this in mind."
- Do not list specific companies, salary, etc. - keep criteria abstract.
- Be honest: if you'd consider this exact role for the right number, say that instead.

My current role and what would interest me: [CONTEXT]
notes: The 'be honest' kicker is the calibration. Recruiters keep notes; vagueness ages badly.

title: Reaching back out to a contact after long silence
use_when: You haven't talked to [CONTACT] in 1+ years and want to reconnect without it feeling transactional.
prompt:
I haven't been in touch with [CONTACT] in [TIME PERIOD]. I want to reconnect.

Why I'm reaching out now: [REAL REASON - genuine reconnection / specific ask / shared event triggered it]

Draft a message that:
1. Acknowledges the silence without over-apologizing for it (one line).
2. References something specific - a memory, something they did recently I noticed, a shared connection.
3. States why I'm reaching out NOW (not "just thought I'd say hi" unless that's true).
4. Proposes a concrete reconnect option (call, coffee, async update exchange).

Rules:
- Maximum 100 words.
- No "Long time no talk!"
- No "Wanted to circle back."
- If you have an ask, name it - don't bury it in pleasantries.
notes: The 'state why NOW' rule prevents the most common failure: reconnects that read as transactional ambushes.

title: Polite but firm reminder about a missed deadline
use_when: Someone owes you a deliverable that's past due and you need to remind them without being passive-aggressive.
prompt:
[PERSON] was supposed to deliver [WHAT] by [DEADLINE]. We are now [HOW LATE]. I need to send a reminder.

Context: [RELATIONSHIP / WHETHER THIS IS A PATTERN / WHAT'S BLOCKED BY THIS]

Draft a reminder that is direct, professional, and gives them one out:

Structure:
- Sentence 1: name the missed deliverable with the original deadline and how late we are.
- Sentence 2: name the downstream impact - what's blocked.
- Sentence 3: ask: when can I expect it, or what changed?
- Sentence 4: offer help if they're stuck.
- Sentence 5: state the new deadline I'll work to.

Rules:
- Maximum 90 words.
- No "I hate to bug you."
- No "Just following up."
- The "downstream impact" line is the load-bearing element - it converts the ask from a personal nag to a project consequence.
notes: Sentence 2 is the lift. People respond to consequence, not to personal frustration.

title: Op-ed draft with single contested claim
use_when: You want to write a 700-900 word op-ed defending one specific position that reasonable people disagree with.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft an op-ed of 700-900 words arguing that [CONTESTED_CLAIM] for submission to [PUBLICATION_NAME].

## AGENT
You write op-eds that the opposition reads to the end because the strongest counter-argument is treated honestly in the third paragraph, not strawmanned.

## SITUATION
The claim: [CONTESTED_CLAIM]
Who disagrees and their best argument: [STEELMAN_OF_OPPOSITION]
My credential or standing to make this claim: [WHY_ME]
The recent news hook making this timely: [NEWS_HOOK_FROM_LAST_14_DAYS]
The one piece of evidence I want to anchor on: [LOAD_BEARING_EVIDENCE]

## TASK
1. Lede: open with a concrete scene, statistic, or single sentence that states the claim sharply.
2. Stakes paragraph: why this matters now, tied to [NEWS_HOOK_FROM_LAST_14_DAYS].
3. Steelman the opposition in one paragraph using "The strongest case against this is..."
4. Three paragraphs of argument, each built around one piece of evidence or example.
5. Anticipate one secondary objection and address it.
6. Close with a specific action or shift the reader should make. No "we must all..." rhetoric.

## RULES
- Do NOT use the phrase "now more than ever."
- Do NOT begin with a dictionary definition or a rhetorical question.
- Do NOT use "I believe" or "I think" - assert the claim directly.
- The steelman paragraph must be one a thoughtful opponent would sign their name to.

## THINK
Before drafting, write three sentences identifying the single strongest objection. The op-ed must survive that objection.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: would someone who disagrees finish reading? If the answer is no, the steelman is too weak.

## OUTPUT
The op-ed, 700-900 words, with a working headline and a one-line deck.
notes: Highest leverage variable is STEELMAN_OF_OPPOSITION. Weak steelman produces a weak op-ed every time.

title: Technical blog post that earns the developer's trust
use_when: You're writing a 1500-2500 word technical post explaining how you solved a real engineering problem.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft a technical blog post about how [TEAM_OR_AUTHOR] solved [SPECIFIC_PROBLEM] using [APPROACH], targeting engineers at [AUDIENCE_SENIORITY_LEVEL].

## AGENT
You write the kind of engineering post that ends up on Hacker News' front page because it shows the failed approaches before the successful one and includes the actual numbers.

## SITUATION
The problem: [SPECIFIC_PROBLEM_WITH_METRICS]
The constraint that made it hard: [REAL_CONSTRAINT]
What we tried first that did not work: [FAILED_APPROACH_1]
What we tried second that did not work: [FAILED_APPROACH_2]
The approach that worked: [WORKING_APPROACH]
The measurable result: [BEFORE_NUMBER] to [AFTER_NUMBER]
The thing we are still unhappy about: [REMAINING_TRADE_OFF]

## TASK
1. Open with the problem stated as a specific scenario, not abstractly. Include the metric that was bad.
2. Explain the constraint that ruled out the obvious solution.
3. Walk through [FAILED_APPROACH_1] and [FAILED_APPROACH_2] briefly - what was tried, why it failed, what we learned.
4. Introduce [WORKING_APPROACH] with the architectural sketch or code excerpt.
5. Show the before/after numbers with a chart or table description.
6. Honest section: [REMAINING_TRADE_OFF] - what we would do differently with more time.
7. Close with what this generalizes to for the reader's situation.

## RULES
- Do NOT skip the failed approaches. They are why readers trust you.
- Do NOT use the word "simply" or "just" when describing code.
- Code blocks must be runnable or annotated to show what is elided.
- No "stay tuned for part 2" - finish the argument.

## THINK
Identify the single moment of insight that flipped the problem. That sentence should appear in the post verbatim.

## EVAL
A senior engineer in [AUDIENCE_SENIORITY_LEVEL] should be able to apply this to a different problem after reading.

## OUTPUT
The post in Markdown, 1500-2500 words, with section headers, code blocks, and a one-paragraph TL;DR at the top.
notes: If the team did not actually fail at the first two approaches, do not invent them. Use a single 'why the naive solution fails' section instead.

title: Personal narrative blog post built around one scene
use_when: You want to write a personal essay or narrative blog post that uses one specific scene as its spine.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft a 1000-1500 word personal narrative post centered on [ONE_SPECIFIC_SCENE] that opens out to a larger reflection about [THEME].

## AGENT
You write essays in the lineage of Joan Didion and Jia Tolentino - the small concrete scene carries the weight of the larger idea, never the reverse.

## SITUATION
The scene (date, place, who was there, one sensory detail): [SCENE_DETAILS]
What I noticed in the scene that I have not stopped thinking about: [THE_NOTICING]
The larger theme this scene illuminates: [THEME]
What I want the reader to feel by the last line: [TARGET_FEELING]
What I am NOT trying to do: [BOUNDARY - e.g., "give advice", "convince anyone", "be uplifting"]

## TASK
1. Drop the reader into [ONE_SPECIFIC_SCENE] in the first 80 words. No setup.
2. Hold the scene for two paragraphs. Specific sensory detail. No abstraction yet.
3. Pivot to [THE_NOTICING] - what about this scene refuses to leave you alone.
4. Move outward to [THEME] but keep returning to the scene's details as anchors.
5. Return to the scene at the end, slightly recontextualized by what you have written.
6. End on an image or a concrete sentence. No moral. No "and so I learned..."

## RULES
- Do NOT explain the theme. Trust the scene to carry it.
- Do NOT start with "I have always..." or "Growing up..."
- Do NOT use the word "journey."
- The final sentence must be ten words or fewer.

## THINK
What is the smallest, most specific detail from the scene? That detail is the title.

## EVAL
Read the draft aloud. Any sentence that sounds like it could appear in any essay gets cut.

## OUTPUT
The essay, 1000-1500 words, with a title pulled from a phrase inside the piece.
notes: Do not use this prompt if the scene is not actually specific. 'A time I felt scared as a kid' is not specific. 'The day in April 2009 my dad sold the truck' is specific.

title: LinkedIn post that reads like a person wrote it
use_when: You want to post on LinkedIn about something real without sounding like a LinkedIn post.
prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post about [SPECIFIC_THING_THAT_HAPPENED] that does not read as a LinkedIn post.

Context:
- What happened: [ONE_SENTENCE_INCIDENT]
- Why I am posting about it: [REAL_REASON]
- The audience I actually care about reaching: [SPECIFIC_AUDIENCE - e.g., "early-career data engineers"]
- The action or thought I want them to take: [SPECIFIC_ASK]

Constraints:
- Open with a sentence that would make a friend text back, not a stranger like the post.
- No "I am humbled / honored / excited to share."
- No one-sentence paragraphs stacked like a poem unless the actual content demands it.
- Maximum 180 words.
- Do not end with "What do you think? Drop a comment below."
- If there is a lesson, it should be specific to the situation, not a universal truth.
- The post must not work as a generic post if you swap the topic out.

Output: the post, ready to paste.
notes: If you can replace the specifics with any other story and the post still works, it is not specific enough yet.

title: Twitter/X thread defending one argument
use_when: You want to write a 6-10 tweet thread that defends a single argument without thread-bro mannerisms.
prompt:
Write a 6-10 tweet thread arguing that [ONE_ARGUMENT].

Context:
- The argument: [ONE_ARGUMENT]
- The reader I am writing for: [AUDIENCE]
- The evidence I have to support it: [EVIDENCE_LIST]
- The counter-argument I will address: [STRONGEST_COUNTER]

Constraints:
- Tweet 1 must state the argument plainly. No "a thread:" and no "I have been thinking about this for a while..."
- No numbered emoji bullets. No "🧵" emoji. No rocket ships.
- Each tweet must stand alone as readable - someone landing on tweet 4 should still get value.
- One tweet must engage [STRONGEST_COUNTER] honestly.
- Final tweet is the sharpest restatement of the argument, not a CTA to follow or like.
- 280 characters max per tweet. Count.

Output: numbered tweets 1/N through N/N.
notes: Avoid the format if the argument fits in one tweet. Threads are for arguments that require sequencing.

title: Newsletter intro that earns the click-through
use_when: You're writing the opening section of a newsletter issue and need the reader to keep reading past the first paragraph.
prompt:
Write the opening 120-180 words of a newsletter issue about [TOPIC_OF_THIS_ISSUE].

Context:
- The newsletter is called [NEWSLETTER_NAME] and goes to [READER_DESCRIPTION].
- This issue's central piece is about [TOPIC_OF_THIS_ISSUE].
- The hook I noticed this week that connects to the topic: [SPECIFIC_OBSERVATION]
- The one thing the reader should take away: [TAKEAWAY]

Constraints:
- Open with the observation, not with "Hi everyone" or "Welcome back."
- The observation must be small and specific (a sentence overheard, a chart, a one-line news item) - not "lately I've been thinking about..."
- The intro must end on a sentence that makes the reader want to scroll to the main piece. No "let's dive in."
- Do not summarize the rest of the newsletter. Tease one specific question or claim.
- No em-dash on every other line. Use one max.

Output: the intro paragraph, plus a single transition line into the main piece.
notes: The SPECIFIC_OBSERVATION variable is everything. If you cannot point to one concrete thing from the last week, the intro will read as filler.

title: Press release for a small-company milestone
use_when: Your small company hit a milestone (funding, launch, customer count) and you need a press release that actually gets picked up.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft a press release announcing [MILESTONE] for [COMPANY_NAME], formatted for distribution to [TARGET_OUTLETS].

## AGENT
You have written press releases that got picked up by trade press because they answered the journalist's "so what" in the first paragraph and gave them a quotable line by paragraph two.

## SITUATION
Company: [COMPANY_NAME], founded [YEAR_OF_FOUNDING], headquartered [LOCATION]
The milestone: [MILESTONE_WITH_NUMBER]
What this means for customers: [CUSTOMER_IMPLICATION]
What this means for the market: [MARKET_IMPLICATION]
Quotable executive: [EXEC_NAME], [TITLE]
The trade outlet most likely to cover this: [TARGET_OUTLET]
The angle that outlet cares about: [OUTLET_ANGLE]

## TASK
1. Headline: under 12 words, leads with the news, not the company name.
2. Subhead: one line of context. Quantify if possible.
3. Dateline paragraph: city, date, then the news in one sentence with the number.
4. Paragraph 2: why this matters to [TARGET_OUTLET]'s audience, framed around [OUTLET_ANGLE].
5. Paragraph 3: quote from [EXEC_NAME] that sounds like a person, not a press release. Should make a specific claim.
6. Paragraph 4: one supporting detail or customer voice.
7. Boilerplate: 3-sentence "About [COMPANY_NAME]" and a contact line.

## RULES
- Do NOT use "leading", "innovative", "best-in-class", "world-class", "industry-leading", or "revolutionary."
- Do NOT use a CEO quote that begins with "We are thrilled to..."
- The headline must work if a journalist copies it verbatim into a tweet.
- Numbers must be specific. "Hundreds of customers" is not specific. "412 customers" is.

## THINK
What is the single sentence a journalist would underline? Make sure that sentence is in paragraph 2 or 3.

## EVAL
Send to a friend in the target audience. Ask: "Would you read past the headline?" If no, the headline is wrong.

## OUTPUT
Full press release, around 350-450 words, in standard format with FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE at the top.
notes: Skip this if the milestone is not actually newsworthy. A blog post is better than a press release that gets ignored.

title: Conference talk abstract for a competitive CFP
use_when: You're submitting a talk proposal to a conference with a competitive CFP and need an abstract that gets selected.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft a 150-250 word conference talk abstract for [CONFERENCE_NAME], track [TRACK], on the topic of [TOPIC].

## AGENT
You have served on CFP review committees and know the abstract is read in under 90 seconds by tired reviewers comparing it against 300 others. Specificity and a clear takeaway beat polish.

## SITUATION
The conference: [CONFERENCE_NAME], audience is [TYPICAL_ATTENDEE]
The track: [TRACK], with stated theme [TRACK_THEME]
The talk topic: [TOPIC]
The novel claim or framework I bring: [NOVEL_CONTRIBUTION]
The concrete evidence backing the claim: [EVIDENCE]
What attendees will be able to do after the talk: [TAKEAWAY_ACTION]
Length of the talk: [TALK_LENGTH]

## TASK
1. Sentence 1: the specific problem the audience faces. Not "many companies struggle with..." - name the problem precisely.
2. Sentence 2-3: why the standard approach fails or falls short.
3. Sentence 4-5: the speaker's contribution - [NOVEL_CONTRIBUTION] - stated as a claim, not a tease.
4. Sentence 6-7: the evidence - [EVIDENCE] - with numbers if applicable.
5. Final 1-2 sentences: what attendees will leave able to do.

## RULES
- Do NOT write "In this talk, we will explore..." - state what you will argue.
- Do NOT promise to "share insights." Share the actual insight in the abstract.
- Do NOT use a question as the closing sentence.
- The abstract must work as a standalone argument even if no one attends the talk.

## THINK
What is the one sentence that distinguishes this talk from the other 50 on the same general topic? That sentence belongs in the first three.

## EVAL
A reviewer who reads 100 abstracts in a row should remember yours. If yours is "yet another talk on [TOPIC]", rework the novel contribution.

## OUTPUT
The abstract (150-250 words), plus a 50-word speaker bio in third person.
notes: NOVEL_CONTRIBUTION is the load-bearing variable. If you cannot finish the sentence 'No one else is saying X', the talk is not ready.

title: Book pitch query letter to a literary agent
use_when: You're sending a query letter to a literary agent about a specific book project.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft a query letter to [AGENT_NAME] at [AGENCY] about [BOOK_TITLE], a [GENRE] book of approximately [WORD_COUNT] words.

## AGENT
You write queries that get full requests because the hook is genuinely intriguing, the comp titles are recent and accurate, and the bio answers why this writer can write this book.

## SITUATION
Agent's name: [AGENT_NAME]
Agency: [AGENCY]
Why this agent specifically (recent deal, client, MSWL post): [WHY_THIS_AGENT]
Book title: [BOOK_TITLE]
Genre / category: [GENRE]
Word count (rounded to nearest thousand): [WORD_COUNT]
Two comp titles from the last 3 years: [COMP_1] and [COMP_2]
The hook (the one-sentence pitch): [HOOK]
The protagonist + their concrete want + the obstacle: [CHARACTER_LINE]
What is at stake if they fail: [STAKES]
My relevant credential or platform: [BIO_DETAIL]

## TASK
1. Salutation: "Dear [AGENT_FIRST_NAME]," - never "Dear Agent" or "To Whom It May Concern."
2. Personalization paragraph: one or two sentences referencing [WHY_THIS_AGENT]. Specific, not flattery.
3. Hook paragraph: open with [HOOK] reframed as a one-line teaser. Then 3-4 sentences on the protagonist, want, obstacle, stakes.
4. Comp + housekeeping paragraph: "[BOOK_TITLE] is a [WORD_COUNT]-word [GENRE] that will appeal to readers of [COMP_1] and [COMP_2]."
5. Bio paragraph: 2-3 sentences. Lead with the most relevant credential.
6. Closing: "Thank you for your consideration." Sign off. Nothing more.

## RULES
- Do NOT use "fiction novel."
- Do NOT compare your book to anything older than 4 years or to a megabestseller like Harry Potter or Gone Girl.
- Do NOT pitch more than one book.
- Do NOT include the full synopsis in the query.
- Maximum 350 words for the body.

## THINK
What is the single most surprising element of this story? Make sure it appears in the hook paragraph.

## EVAL
Read the query without the title. Could it be any book in the genre? If yes, the hook is too generic.

## OUTPUT
The complete query letter, ready to paste into an agent's submission form.
notes: Do not skip the WHY_THIS_AGENT variable. A query without personalization signals 'mass blast' and gets passed.

title: Cover letter that earns the interview slot
use_when: You're applying to a specific role at a specific company and the cover letter has to do real work.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft a cover letter for [ROLE_TITLE] at [COMPANY_NAME] that gets [APPLICANT_NAME] into the interview round.

## AGENT
You have read 5000 cover letters as a hiring manager. The ones that worked named a specific problem the company is solving and connected it to specific work the applicant has done - not generic enthusiasm.

## SITUATION
Applicant: [APPLICANT_NAME]
Role: [ROLE_TITLE]
Company: [COMPANY_NAME]
The hiring manager's name (if known): [HIRING_MANAGER_NAME]
A specific thing the company is working on right now (from their blog, podcast, hiring page): [COMPANY_SIGNAL]
The applicant's most relevant prior project: [PROJECT_DESCRIPTION]
The measurable result of that project: [PROJECT_OUTCOME_WITH_NUMBER]
The skill the job description emphasizes most: [TOP_REQUIRED_SKILL]
The gap on the applicant's resume that this letter should address: [REAL_GAP]

## TASK
1. Salutation: "Dear [HIRING_MANAGER_NAME]," or "Dear [TEAM_NAME] team," - never "To Whom It May Concern."
2. Opening paragraph: reference [COMPANY_SIGNAL] and connect it in one sentence to your interest. No "I am writing to apply."
3. Body paragraph 1: [PROJECT_DESCRIPTION] with [PROJECT_OUTCOME_WITH_NUMBER]. Show the work, not the adjectives.
4. Body paragraph 2: connect [TOP_REQUIRED_SKILL] to a specific moment in your background where you used it.
5. Address [REAL_GAP] directly in one sentence - what it is, what you have done to close it.
6. Closing: one sentence on what you want next (a conversation about X). No "thank you for your time and consideration."

## RULES
- Do NOT use "passionate about", "results-driven", "team player", or "self-starter."
- Do NOT restate the resume. The cover letter is for what the resume cannot say.
- Do NOT exceed 350 words.
- Do NOT begin a paragraph with "I."
- Every claim must be backed by a specific example, not an adjective.

## THINK
The one sentence that would make the hiring manager say "wait, who wrote this?" - that sentence should be the second sentence of the opening paragraph.

## EVAL
Strip the company name out. Could this letter be sent to a competitor? If yes, [COMPANY_SIGNAL] is not specific enough.

## OUTPUT
The cover letter, 250-350 words, in standard format.
notes: COMPANY_SIGNAL is non-negotiable. If you cannot point to something specific the company has done, do not write the letter yet - research more first.

title: Resume bullets: from task description to impact
use_when: Your resume bullets currently describe what you did, not what changed because you did it.
prompt:
Rewrite the following resume bullets so each one shows impact, not activity.

Original bullets:
[PASTE_BULLETS_HERE - one per line]

Context for each bullet (fill in what you can):
- Role: [ROLE_TITLE] at [COMPANY]
- The metric that changed because of this work: [METRIC]
- The before/after numbers: [BEFORE] to [AFTER]
- The scope (team size, budget, user count): [SCOPE]
- Tools / methods used (only if differentiating): [TOOLS]

Constraints:
- Each rewritten bullet must follow the structure: [STRONG_VERB] + [WHAT_CHANGED] + [BY_HOW_MUCH] + [HOW or FOR_WHOM].
- No "responsible for", "duties included", "worked on", "helped with."
- No verbs like "utilized" - use "used."
- Each bullet must be one line on a standard resume (roughly 18 words max).
- If a bullet has no measurable result, mark it (NO METRIC) and propose one question I can answer to find the number.
- Do not invent numbers. If I did not give you a metric, do not make one up.

Output: a table with two columns - Original and Rewritten - and a third row at the bottom listing any (NO METRIC) bullets with the question to answer for each.
notes: The honest 'I do not know the number' move is more useful than a fake number. Mark the bullets, ask the question, do not fabricate.

title: Personal statement built around one inflection
use_when: You're writing a college or graduate school personal statement and need it to be about a real moment, not a polished narrative arc.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft a 500-650 word personal statement for [PROGRAM_NAME] at [INSTITUTION] built around [ONE_INFLECTION_MOMENT].

## AGENT
You have read admissions essays for ten years. The ones that worked were not about the biggest moment of the applicant's life - they were about the most specific one, told with enough particularity that no other applicant could have written it.

## SITUATION
Applicant: [APPLICANT_NAME], applying to [PROGRAM_NAME] at [INSTITUTION]
The one inflection moment (with date, place, who was there): [INFLECTION_DETAILS]
What changed in the applicant's thinking because of it: [CHANGE_IN_THINKING]
How this connects to the field of study: [CONNECTION_TO_FIELD]
What specifically about [INSTITUTION]'s [PROGRAM_NAME] matches: [PROGRAM_FIT_DETAIL - a specific lab, faculty, course, or initiative]
What I am NOT going to do: claim this experience defines me, claim it was harder than it was, claim a tidy lesson learned.

## TASK
1. Open inside [INFLECTION_DETAILS] - in the moment, sensory detail, no setup.
2. Hold the scene for 100-150 words. What was said, what was done, what was felt.
3. Pivot to [CHANGE_IN_THINKING] in one sentence. Not "and I realized..." - show the shift in how you described the next thing you did.
4. Trace 1-2 concrete things you did because of the shift. Project, role, course, conversation.
5. Connect to [CONNECTION_TO_FIELD] without using the word "passion."
6. Last paragraph: [PROGRAM_FIT_DETAIL]. One specific thing at [INSTITUTION] you want to do.
7. Close on a sentence that returns to a detail from the opening scene.

## RULES
- Do NOT begin with a quote.
- Do NOT use "ever since I was young", "from a young age", or "as long as I can remember."
- Do NOT name three different unrelated experiences. One inflection. Deep.
- Do NOT use the word "passion" or "passionate."
- The essay must not work if you swap the program name out at the end. The fit must be earned.

## THINK
What is the single image from the inflection moment that you can describe in five words that no one else could? That image is the first sentence.

## EVAL
Read aloud. Any sentence that could appear in another applicant's essay gets cut or made more specific.

## OUTPUT
The essay, 500-650 words.
notes: INFLECTION_DETAILS must be a real moment with concrete particulars. 'When I volunteered at the hospital' is not specific. 'The Tuesday in February when Mr. Reyes asked me to read the discharge form to him' is specific.

title: Wedding toast for a specific person, not the role
use_when: You're giving a wedding toast and want it to be about this person, not about marriage in general.
prompt:
Draft a 2-3 minute wedding toast (roughly 300-450 spoken words) for [COUPLE - e.g., 'Maya and Joel'].

Context:
- My relationship to [PERSON_I_KNOW_BEST]: [RELATIONSHIP - e.g., 'her older brother', 'his college roommate of 8 years']
- One specific story about [PERSON_I_KNOW_BEST] that shows who they are: [STORY_WITH_CONCRETE_DETAILS]
- What changed in [PERSON_I_KNOW_BEST] after they met [PARTNER]: [SPECIFIC_OBSERVED_CHANGE]
- The single quality [PARTNER] brings out in [PERSON_I_KNOW_BEST]: [QUALITY]
- The audience: family-friendly but the couple is in their 30s and would prefer not boring.

Constraints:
- Open with the story, not with "For those of you who don't know me..."
- The story must have one specific detail (a day, a quote, a place) - generic memories do not land.
- Land on [SPECIFIC_OBSERVED_CHANGE] as the bridge from the story to the toast itself.
- One line of warm humor maximum. Do not roast.
- No advice about marriage. You are not qualified, and neither am I.
- End with a toast line that names both people, in 12 words or fewer.
- Spoken word count, not written - say it out loud, time it, trim.

Output: the toast, with one-line stage directions in brackets where useful (e.g., [pause], [look at couple]).
notes: If you cannot fill in SPECIFIC_OBSERVED_CHANGE honestly, do not give the toast - ask someone closer to do it.

title: Eulogy outline that holds the room
use_when: You've been asked to give a eulogy and need to organize what to say without flattening the person into a list of virtues.
prompt:
Help me build an outline for a 5-7 minute eulogy for [PERSON_NAME].

Context:
- My relationship to [PERSON_NAME]: [RELATIONSHIP]
- The audience: [AUDIENCE - e.g., 'mostly family, some colleagues, a few neighbors']
- Three specific memories I want to include (with one concrete detail each): [MEMORY_1], [MEMORY_2], [MEMORY_3]
- One quality of theirs that other people may not have seen: [PRIVATE_QUALITY]
- One thing they said often that I want to quote: [RECURRING_PHRASE]
- The hardest thing about losing them: [HONEST_HARD_PART]
- What I do NOT want to do: list their accomplishments, pretend they were perfect, make it about me.

Constraints:
- The outline must be structured so the speaker can deviate without losing place. Use roman numerals for sections, bullets underneath.
- Open with a specific moment, not a summary of their life.
- One of the three memories must show a flaw or contradiction in [PERSON_NAME] that the room would recognize - this is what makes the love feel real.
- [RECURRING_PHRASE] should appear once, late in the eulogy.
- [HONEST_HARD_PART] should be named, not euphemized. Do not use "passed away" - use "died" if appropriate to the family's culture.
- End on an image of [PERSON_NAME] doing something specific, not a benediction.
- Include one [PAUSE] mark and one [BREATH] mark where the speaker will likely need them.

Output: the outline, plus a 60-word opening paragraph drafted in full so the speaker has the first words ready when they walk to the lectern.
notes: The flaw memory is what separates a real eulogy from a sympathy card. If the family is not ready for that, skip it - but the eulogy will be smaller.

title: Speech opener for a tough or distracted crowd
use_when: You're giving a talk to an audience that does not yet care, and the first 60 seconds have to earn the room.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft the opening 90 seconds (roughly 200 spoken words) of a talk on [TOPIC] for [AUDIENCE_DESCRIPTION] who are currently [AUDIENCE_STATE - e.g., 'tired after lunch', 'skeptical of consultants', 'forced to attend'].

## AGENT
You have opened keynotes for hostile and indifferent rooms. The first sentence either wakes the audience up or confirms they were right to check their phones. You do not waste it on thank-yous.

## SITUATION
Topic: [TOPIC]
Audience: [AUDIENCE_DESCRIPTION]
Their current state: [AUDIENCE_STATE]
What they expect this talk to be (so we can subvert it): [AUDIENCE_EXPECTATION]
The single claim of the full talk: [TALK_THESIS]
A specific story, statistic, or scene I can open on: [OPENER_RAW_MATERIAL]
The room: [VENUE_DETAILS - virtual? auditorium? boardroom? after lunch?]

## TASK
1. Sentence 1: a hook. A specific scene, a counter-intuitive fact, or a direct question that names the audience's actual skepticism. No greeting first.
2. Sentences 2-4: develop the hook. Sensory detail or a number that lands.
3. Sentence 5-6: pivot to [TALK_THESIS] in a way that uses the hook as evidence.
4. Sentence 7-8: name the one thing the audience will be able to do or believe by the end. Specific.
5. Land on a sentence that sets up the next section. No "let's get started."

## RULES
- Do NOT open with "Good morning / afternoon / evening, everyone."
- Do NOT introduce yourself first. Earn the room first, introduce yourself once they're listening.
- Do NOT open with a joke unless the joke is structurally part of the argument.
- Do NOT ask a question the audience cannot answer (e.g., "How many of you have ever...") unless you are prepared to use the show of hands.
- The opener must subvert [AUDIENCE_EXPECTATION] within the first three sentences.

## THINK
The audience's first thought when you start should be one of: "wait, what?", "is that true?", or "that's me." If it is "okay, here we go", the opener is wrong.

## EVAL
Read it out loud, standing up. If it does not feel like something a person says, rewrite it as something a person says.

## OUTPUT
The opening 90 seconds in spoken-word format, with [PAUSE] and [SLIDE CHANGE] cues if relevant.
notes: OPENER_RAW_MATERIAL is everything. Without a specific scene, statistic, or counter-intuitive claim, the opener defaults to filler.

title: B2B white paper that does not waste the buyer's time
use_when: You're writing a B2B white paper that has to do real work in a sales cycle without reading like a brochure.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft a 2500-3500 word B2B white paper titled around [WORKING_TITLE] for [TARGET_BUYER_PERSONA] evaluating [PROBLEM_SPACE].

## AGENT
You write white papers that buyers actually finish because the framework is non-obvious, the data is sourced, and the vendor pitch is confined to the last 15% - and even there, it earns its space.

## SITUATION
Working title: [WORKING_TITLE]
Buyer persona: [TARGET_BUYER_PERSONA - title, company size, current pain]
Problem space: [PROBLEM_SPACE]
The non-obvious framework the paper introduces: [FRAMEWORK_NAME_AND_ONE_LINE]
The 2-3 data sources I will cite (with publication dates): [SOURCE_1], [SOURCE_2], [SOURCE_3]
The customer story or case I can reference (anonymized if needed): [CASE_DETAILS]
The vendor's solution: [SOLUTION] - but only mentioned in the final section.

## TASK
1. Executive summary (200-300 words): the problem, the framework, the takeaway. Written so a CFO who reads only this knows the argument.
2. Section 1: The problem framed in the buyer's language, with one cited statistic.
3. Section 2: Why the standard approaches fall short - name two or three by category, not by competitor.
4. Section 3: Introduce [FRAMEWORK_NAME_AND_ONE_LINE]. Walk through it with a diagram description.
5. Section 4: Apply the framework to [CASE_DETAILS] with the before/after.
6. Section 5: An honest section on when the framework does NOT apply. This builds trust.
7. Section 6 (last, max 15% of word count): how [SOLUTION] operationalizes the framework. Concrete, not promotional.
8. Closing: a single next step the reader can take that does not require buying anything.

## RULES
- Do NOT use "in today's rapidly evolving landscape", "digital transformation", or "unlock value."
- Do NOT cite "studies show" without a footnote.
- Do NOT mention the vendor by name until section 6.
- Every chart or table must have a one-sentence caption stating what the reader should conclude.
- The "when this framework does not apply" section is required.

## THINK
What is the one chart or framework diagram a reader would screenshot and share? Make sure the paper builds toward that single image.

## EVAL
Could a competitor read this and learn something useful? If not, the framework is too vendor-shaped.

## OUTPUT
Full draft in Markdown with section headers, callout boxes marked [CALLOUT: ...], and figure captions marked [FIGURE 1: ...]. 2500-3500 words.
notes: The 'when this does not apply' section is the highest-trust move in the paper. Do not skip it to save space.

title: One-pager for an executive sponsor who has 90 seconds
use_when: You need to brief an executive who will read this in the elevator and decide whether to fund or block the work.
prompt:
Draft a one-pager (single page, roughly 350-450 words) for [EXEC_NAME], [EXEC_TITLE], on [INITIATIVE_NAME].

Context:
- The decision I am asking [EXEC_NAME] to make: [SPECIFIC_DECISION - fund / approve / block / sponsor]
- The amount or scope at stake: [DOLLARS_HEADCOUNT_OR_TIMELINE]
- The single most important fact the exec needs: [TOP_FACT]
- The one risk I want to surface honestly: [RISK]
- The alternative I considered and rejected: [REJECTED_ALTERNATIVE_AND_WHY]
- The exec's known preferences or pet peeves: [EXEC_CONTEXT - e.g., 'hates slides, loves prose', 'always asks about customer impact first']

Constraints:
- Top of page: a single bolded "Ask:" line stating [SPECIFIC_DECISION] and [DOLLARS_HEADCOUNT_OR_TIMELINE].
- Three sections only: Why now, What we will do, What could go wrong.
- "What could go wrong" must include [RISK] and your mitigation. Do not bury risk.
- One small table or three bullets max per section. No prose paragraphs over 4 sentences.
- No background section. The exec already knows the background or will ask.
- No "next steps" section. The next step is [SPECIFIC_DECISION].
- Fit on one page at 11pt font.

Output: the one-pager in Markdown, formatted to print on a single page.
notes: If your decision ask is fuzzy ('explore', 'consider'), the exec will not act. Sharpen SPECIFIC_DECISION before writing.

title: Quarterly investor update that builds trust with bad news
use_when: You're sending a quarterly update to investors and this quarter has at least one piece of bad news that needs honest treatment.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft a quarterly investor update for [COMPANY_NAME] covering [QUARTER_AND_YEAR] that handles [PIECE_OF_BAD_NEWS] with the honesty that builds long-term investor trust.

## AGENT
You write investor updates that VCs forward to their partners as examples of "this is how to communicate." You know that hiding bad news destroys more value than the bad news itself.

## SITUATION
Company: [COMPANY_NAME]
Quarter: [QUARTER_AND_YEAR]
Last update's headline number / target: [PRIOR_TARGET]
This quarter's actual: [ACTUAL_NUMBER]
Variance and why: [VARIANCE_EXPLANATION]
The bad news: [PIECE_OF_BAD_NEWS]
What we are doing about it: [REMEDIATION]
The two wins from this quarter: [WIN_1], [WIN_2]
The specific ask of investors this quarter: [ASK - intros, hires, advice]
Cash position: [RUNWAY_IN_MONTHS] months

## TASK
1. Top of update: TL;DR in 4 lines - headline metric, runway, biggest win, biggest concern.
2. Section: The Number. State [PRIOR_TARGET] vs [ACTUAL_NUMBER]. Explain [VARIANCE_EXPLANATION] without hedging.
3. Section: What Worked. [WIN_1] and [WIN_2] with one specific detail each.
4. Section: What Did Not. [PIECE_OF_BAD_NEWS] stated directly. What we know. What we do not yet know. What we are doing about it: [REMEDIATION].
5. Section: Team and Hiring. Departures (named, briefly) and key hires.
6. Section: Asks. Specific [ASK] with names if you have them.
7. Sign-off: brief, sincere, no boilerplate.

## RULES
- Do NOT bury bad news in the middle of a positive paragraph.
- Do NOT use "headwinds", "challenging quarter", or "soft."
- Do NOT round numbers to hide the precision.
- Do NOT skip the runway number even if it is uncomfortable.
- The bad news section must come BEFORE the asks section.

## THINK
What is the question the smartest investor on the cap table will ask after reading this? Address it in the update.

## EVAL
If this update leaks publicly, would you stand behind every sentence? If not, rewrite.

## OUTPUT
The investor update as it would appear in an email - subject line, body, and a "reply if you can help with X" closing.
notes: The variance explanation is where founders lose investor trust. Be specific. 'Sales cycle lengthened' is not specific. 'Two enterprise deals slipped from Q2 to Q3 because of procurement freezes at [INDUSTRY]' is specific.

title: Grant proposal narrative section that scores well
use_when: You're writing the narrative section of a grant application and need to score well against a published rubric.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft the [SECTION_NAME] narrative section (target length [WORD_LIMIT] words) of a grant application to [FUNDER_NAME] for [PROJECT_NAME], scored against [RUBRIC_CRITERIA].

## AGENT
You have served as a grant reviewer. You read narratives with the rubric open in front of you and assign points sentence by sentence. You know unfunded proposals usually fail not on idea but on failing to map clearly onto the rubric.

## SITUATION
Funder: [FUNDER_NAME], program: [PROGRAM_NAME]
Section being drafted: [SECTION_NAME]
Word limit: [WORD_LIMIT]
The funder's stated priorities (quote verbatim from RFP): [FUNDER_PRIORITY_QUOTES]
Scoring rubric criteria (with point weights): [RUBRIC_CRITERIA_WITH_POINTS]
Project name: [PROJECT_NAME]
The problem in numbers: [PROBLEM_STATISTIC_WITH_CITATION]
Our intervention: [INTERVENTION]
The evidence base supporting our approach: [EVIDENCE_BASE]
Our measurable outcomes (with baseline and target): [OUTCOMES]
Why our org specifically can do this: [ORG_DIFFERENTIATOR]

## TASK
1. Open with the problem stated in [FUNDER_NAME]'s own framing language, citing [PROBLEM_STATISTIC_WITH_CITATION].
2. Map our intervention to each of [RUBRIC_CRITERIA_WITH_POINTS]. For each criterion, write 1-3 sentences that directly answer the rubric question.
3. Cite [EVIDENCE_BASE] in the section the rubric weights highest.
4. State [OUTCOMES] as specific numbers: baseline → target by date.
5. Close the section by tying back to [FUNDER_PRIORITY_QUOTES] without repeating the phrase verbatim.

## RULES
- Do NOT use jargon the funder's program officers do not use. Match their vocabulary.
- Do NOT use "innovative" - every applicant says this. Show novelty by specifics.
- Do NOT promise outcomes you cannot measure or back out of.
- Every claim must be either cited or backed by org track record.
- Stay under [WORD_LIMIT]. Reviewers stop reading at the limit even if you keep writing.

## THINK
For each rubric criterion, ask: which exact sentence in my draft earns those points? If you cannot point to one, the section will lose points.

## EVAL
Hand the draft to someone who has never seen the RFP. Can they reverse-engineer the rubric from your text? If not, the mapping is too loose.

## OUTPUT
The section as it will appear in the application, with [CITATION] markers where references go, plus a brief scoring map: which sentences earn which rubric points.
notes: FUNDER_PRIORITY_QUOTES verbatim from the RFP is the single most underused move in grant writing. Reviewers reward applicants who mirror the program's language.

title: Product launch announcement that is not a feature list
use_when: You're announcing a product launch and need an announcement that makes the reader want the thing, not skim the bullets.
prompt:
Draft a product launch announcement (800-1200 words) for [PRODUCT_NAME] from [COMPANY_NAME], publishing on our blog.

Context:
- What [PRODUCT_NAME] does in one sentence: [ONE_LINER]
- The specific user it is for: [USER_PERSONA]
- The specific moment in their workflow where they would reach for it: [TRIGGER_MOMENT]
- What they did before [PRODUCT_NAME] existed: [PRIOR_WORKAROUND]
- The one thing it does that no competitor does: [DIFFERENTIATOR]
- The thing it deliberately does NOT do (and we are proud of that): [NON_FEATURE]
- A real beta user quote with their permission: [BETA_QUOTE_WITH_NAME_TITLE]
- The price or pricing model: [PRICING]

Constraints:
- Open with [TRIGGER_MOMENT] as a scene. The reader should recognize themselves in the first paragraph.
- Do not list features in a bullet list before paragraph 4. Earn the features.
- [DIFFERENTIATOR] must be shown through a use case, not stated as a claim.
- The [NON_FEATURE] section is required - it builds trust and signals product taste.
- [BETA_QUOTE_WITH_NAME_TITLE] goes near the end, not as opening social proof. It should land like a confirmation, not a hook.
- No "We are excited to announce..." opening. No "Today, we are thrilled to launch..." either.
- Close with one specific next action and the pricing line.

Output: the announcement, formatted for a blog post with H2 section breaks.
notes: The NON_FEATURE move is the load-bearing thing in the announcement. It signals you know what your product is for, which is the rarest signal a launch post can send.

title: Internal memo announcing an org change that lands without panic
use_when: Leadership is announcing a reorg, restructure, or role change and the memo has to inform without setting off a rumor cycle.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft an internal memo from [LEADER_NAME] announcing [ORG_CHANGE] effective [EFFECTIVE_DATE], for the [SCOPE - team / division / company].

## AGENT
You have written reorg memos that did not blow up because they named the change directly, addressed what employees actually want to know (am I safe, who is my boss, what happens to my work), and did not over-explain the why.

## SITUATION
Leader: [LEADER_NAME], [LEADER_TITLE]
The change: [ORG_CHANGE - be specific: who reports to whom, what is moving where]
Effective date: [EFFECTIVE_DATE]
Scope: [SCOPE]
The honest reason for the change: [REAL_REASON]
What is NOT changing (compensation, role, location): [WHAT_IS_NOT_CHANGING]
Whether there are layoffs involved (yes/no, count): [LAYOFF_STATUS]
The person each affected employee should talk to next: [POINT_OF_CONTACT_LOGIC]
Open question we have not resolved: [HONEST_OPEN_QUESTION]
When the next update is coming: [NEXT_UPDATE_DATE]

## TASK
1. Subject line: state the change directly. Not "An update from leadership."
2. Sentence 1: what is changing, in one sentence with [EFFECTIVE_DATE].
3. Paragraph 2: who reports to whom now, in a clear list.
4. Paragraph 3: [REAL_REASON] in 2-4 sentences. No corporate abstraction.
5. Paragraph 4: [WHAT_IS_NOT_CHANGING]. This is what most employees want to know.
6. Paragraph 5: address [LAYOFF_STATUS] directly, even if the answer is "no layoffs."
7. Paragraph 6: [POINT_OF_CONTACT_LOGIC] - who each affected person should talk to and when.
8. Paragraph 7: [HONEST_OPEN_QUESTION] stated plainly with [NEXT_UPDATE_DATE].
9. Close: one sentence. No "we are excited about this next chapter."

## RULES
- Do NOT use "synergies", "right-sizing", "strategic realignment", or "operational excellence."
- Do NOT bury the layoff question. Address it in the top half of the memo.
- Do NOT pretend the change is purely positive if it is not.
- Do NOT promise things you cannot keep ("no further changes are planned" - only say this if it is true).
- The memo must answer the three questions every employee has: Am I safe? Who is my boss? What happens to my project?

## THINK
What is the rumor you are trying to prevent? Make sure the memo addresses it head-on by paragraph 4.

## EVAL
Read the memo as if you just learned you might be affected. Does it answer your questions or leave you needing to forward it to your manager? If the latter, rewrite.

## OUTPUT
The memo as it will appear in employees' inboxes - subject line, salutation, body, sign-off.
notes: The HONEST_OPEN_QUESTION variable is the trust move. Pretending all questions are answered when they are not is what makes reorg memos fail.

title: Vision document for a team or product that survives a year
use_when: You're writing a vision document that will be read by new hires, executives, and partners for the next 12+ months and needs to hold up.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft a 2-3 page vision document for [TEAM_OR_PRODUCT_NAME] that will guide decisions over [TIME_HORIZON - e.g., '12 months', '3 years'].

## AGENT
You write vision documents that survive contact with reality because they make hard trade-offs explicit and name what we will NOT do as clearly as what we will do.

## SITUATION
Team or product: [TEAM_OR_PRODUCT_NAME]
Time horizon: [TIME_HORIZON]
The user or customer we serve: [SPECIFIC_CUSTOMER]
The single most important outcome we are driving toward: [NORTH_STAR_METRIC]
The current state of that metric: [BASELINE]
Where it will be at the end of [TIME_HORIZON] if we succeed: [TARGET]
The two or three principles that will guide our decisions: [PRINCIPLES]
The things we will deliberately NOT do (and why): [NON_GOALS]
The biggest assumption we are making that could be wrong: [LOAD_BEARING_ASSUMPTION]
What will tell us we are off track within 90 days: [LEADING_INDICATOR]

## TASK
1. Section: Who we serve. [SPECIFIC_CUSTOMER] described as a real person with a real day, not a persona.
2. Section: What we are driving toward. [NORTH_STAR_METRIC] from [BASELINE] to [TARGET] by [TIME_HORIZON].
3. Section: How we will operate. [PRINCIPLES] with one example of a recent decision that followed each.
4. Section: What we will NOT do. [NON_GOALS] explained with the rationale. This is the hardest section to write and the most valuable.
5. Section: What could make this wrong. [LOAD_BEARING_ASSUMPTION] named directly. What we will watch.
6. Section: How we will know if we are off track. [LEADING_INDICATOR] with the threshold and the response.

## RULES
- Do NOT use "world-class", "category-defining", or "best-in-class."
- Do NOT write a vision the competitor could write with their name swapped in.
- Do NOT skip the non-goals section. A vision without non-goals is a wish list.
- Do NOT promise the future is certain. Name [LOAD_BEARING_ASSUMPTION] honestly.
- Every principle must have a recent decision that followed it. If you cannot name one, the principle is aspirational, not real.

## THINK
What is the decision someone might make in 6 months that this document should prevent? Make sure the principles or non-goals would prevent it.

## EVAL
