4. For each cluster, name one tripwire metric I will commit to monitoring monthly.

## THINK
Before producing the output: (a) name the obvious version that almost any writer would produce, (b) name what would make this output uniquely the user's instead of generic, (c) name the move you are most tempted to make that would be too safe. Use this to shape the output.

## RULES
- Do NOT include "an act of God" or "the market changed" as a scenario
- Each scenario must trace to a decision I personally made or could have made
- Tripwires must be observable in 30 days or less, not annual

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Does this output have at least one specific detail or move that another writer with the same prompt would not have produced? (2) Did I avoid the obvious version of this? If either is no, sharpen.

## OUTPUT
8 narrated scenarios, then the cluster table with tripwires.
notes: Disasters that trace to your own decisions are the only ones you can actually prevent.

title: Naming brainstorm - product, project, team
use_when: You need a name for [THING] and want options that span tone registers, not 15 variations of the same idea.
prompt:
Generate 25 name candidates for [THING_BEING_NAMED]. Description: [WHAT_IT_DOES_AND_WHO_IT_IS_FOR].

Spread the 25 across these registers, 5 each:
- Latin/Greek roots (serious, institutional)
- Plain English nouns (Stripe, Square, Notion style)
- Made-up words (Spotify, Roblox style)
- Mythological/literary references
- Action verbs as names (Zoom, Slack)

After the list, flag any that have obvious .com or trademark problems and pick the top 3 with one-line justifications. Do NOT pick a "safe middle" name - pick three with distinct positioning trade-offs.
notes: The five registers force you out of the rut your taste keeps pulling you into.

title: Tagline brainstorm with deliberate tone variations
use_when: You need a tagline and want to feel the difference between witty, serious, and cryptic before picking.
prompt:
Write 15 taglines for [PRODUCT_OR_BRAND], 5 in each register:

WITTY (wordplay, surprise, light): under 8 words, must land in one beat.
SERIOUS (mission, gravity, confidence): can run longer, must promise something concrete.
CRYPTIC (intrigue, deliberately incomplete): under 6 words, must make the reader want to ask "what does that mean?"

Brand context: [POSITIONING], [AUDIENCE], [CATEGORY].

Then pick the strongest from each register and explain what kind of company would pick that one. Do NOT pick the middle option to play it safe.
notes: Putting the three registers side by side reveals what you're actually trying to say.

title: Headline brainstorm A/B/C/D
use_when: You have content ready to ship and need 4 headline variants that test genuinely different psychological angles.
prompt:
Generate 4 headlines for [ARTICLE_OR_POST_OR_EMAIL], each pulling a different lever:

A - CURIOSITY GAP: tease an answer without giving it.
B - SPECIFIC NUMBER: anchor with a counterintuitive figure.
C - DIRECT BENEFIT: promise the reader's outcome in plain language.
D - CONTRARIAN TAKE: state the opposite of conventional wisdom.

Content summary: [SUMMARY_IN_3_SENTENCES]. Target audience: [AUDIENCE].

For each, name the type of reader who clicks it and the type who scrolls past. Do NOT write four versions of the same lever in different words.
notes: If two of your four headlines pull the same lever, you have two headlines, not four.

title: Pivot brainstorm - 5 directions if current path fails
use_when: You suspect your current approach to [PROJECT] is wrong but you're too committed to see alternatives clearly.
prompt:
## MISSION
Assume my current approach to [PROJECT] has failed. Generate 5 distinct pivot directions, each preserving a different asset I've built so far.

## AGENT
You are an advisor who has watched founders and operators pivot 30+ times. You know good pivots preserve one valuable asset (audience, technology, insight, relationship, distribution) and discard everything else.

## SITUATION
Current approach: [WHAT_I_AM_DOING]
What I've built that has real value: [ASSETS - list at least 4]
What's not working: [SPECIFIC_EVIDENCE_OF_FAILURE]
What I refuse to give up emotionally: [SACRED_COW]

## TASK
1. List 5 pivots, each named after which asset it keeps: audience-pivot, technology-pivot, insight-pivot, relationship-pivot, distribution-pivot.
2. For each, write the new offer in one sentence and the customer it serves.
3. Identify which pivot requires me to give up my sacred cow.
4. Recommend which one I should actually try and why.

## THINK
Before producing the output: (a) name the obvious version that almost any writer would produce, (b) name what would make this output uniquely the user's instead of generic, (c) name the move you are most tempted to make that would be too safe. Use this to shape the output.

## RULES
- Do NOT propose a pivot that requires raising money or hiring before any revenue
- Each pivot must be testable with a single customer conversation this week
- The sacred-cow pivot must be on the list even if I'll hate it

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Does this output have at least one specific detail or move that another writer with the same prompt would not have produced? (2) Did I avoid the obvious version of this? If either is no, sharpen.

## OUTPUT
5 pivots with their preserved asset, then the recommendation.
notes: The pivot you most resist is usually the one you most need to consider.

title: Feature brainstorm for an existing product
use_when: You're planning the next quarter's roadmap for [PRODUCT] and want feature candidates beyond what's already on the backlog.
prompt:
Generate 12 feature ideas for [PRODUCT], 3 from each source:

1. FROM CHURN DATA: features that address why people leave.
2. FROM POWER USERS: features that let your top 5% do something they're already hacking together.
3. FROM ADJACENT TOOLS: things users already do in another tab that could happen in yours.
4. FROM REGULATORY/TRUST GAPS: features that reduce the buyer's perceived risk.

For each, state: the user job, the smallest version that could ship in 2 weeks, and what data would prove it worked.

Do NOT include features that are already in the public backlog. Do NOT propose "AI-powered X" without naming the specific user action it replaces.
notes: Four different sources prevent the bias toward whichever team shouts loudest.

title: Content calendar brainstorm
use_when: You need [N] pieces of content over [TIMEFRAME] and want variety in topic, format, and angle - not 10 versions of the same post.
prompt:
Generate a content matrix of 12 ideas for [CHANNEL - e.g., LinkedIn, newsletter, YouTube]. Topic area: [TOPIC]. Audience: [AUDIENCE].

Spread across:
- 4 topic types: how-to, opinion, case study, contrarian take
- 3 formats: short post, long-form, visual/carousel/video
- 3 angles: personal story, data-driven, interview/quote-led

Output as a table. Each row: title, topic type, format, angle, the single hook in one sentence.

Do NOT repeat the same hook in different words. Do NOT include "X tips for Y" as more than one of the 12.
notes: A real content calendar has variety across all three axes - a fake one varies the title and repeats the substance.

title: Gift idea brainstorm for a specific person
use_when: You're shopping for someone specific, have a real budget, and want options that feel chosen, not algorithmic.
prompt:
Generate 10 gift ideas for [PERSON - name, relationship, age, what I know they care about] with a budget of [BUDGET] and an occasion of [OCCASION].

For each idea, include:
- The item or experience
- Where to actually get it
- Why it fits THIS person (one sentence tying to something specific I told you)
- One thoughtful add-on under $15 that elevates it

Spread across categories: 2 experiences, 2 books or media, 2 useful objects, 2 indulgences they wouldn't buy themselves, 2 personalized/handmade options.

Do NOT suggest gift cards. Do NOT suggest "a nice candle" or other category-only ideas. Do NOT recommend anything that requires me to know their size.
notes: The 'why it fits this person' line is what separates a gift from a transaction.

title: Side project brainstorm matched to my skills
use_when: You want to start something on the side and want options that build on what you already know, not start from zero.
prompt:
## MISSION
Generate 6 side project ideas matched specifically to my existing skills and circumstances. Each must be launchable in under 30 days of evening/weekend work.

## AGENT
You are an advisor to people with full-time jobs who want side projects that compound. You know most side projects fail because they're someone else's dream applied to the wrong life.

## SITUATION
My day job and skills: [JOB_AND_HARD_SKILLS]
What I'm secretly good at that isn't in my job description: [HIDDEN_SKILLS]
Hours per week I can realistically commit: [HOURS]
What I want from this: [MONEY / AUDIENCE / LEARNING / CRAFT / OPTIONALITY]
Constraints (non-compete, family, health, location): [CONSTRAINTS]

## TASK
1. Generate 6 ideas, each anchored to a specific existing skill.
2. For each: what it is in one sentence, the first $1 of revenue or first 10 users path, the failure mode most likely in week 6.
3. Identify which one is closest to what I want, and which one would teach me the most about something I don't yet know.

## THINK
Before producing the output: (a) name the obvious version that almost any writer would produce, (b) name what would make this output uniquely the user's instead of generic, (c) name the move you are most tempted to make that would be too safe. Use this to shape the output.

## RULES
- Do NOT propose a SaaS unless I have shipped software before
- Do NOT propose "build an audience first" as the project - that's a tactic, not a project
- Each idea must produce something a stranger could buy or use within 30 days
- Do NOT recommend more than 1 project that competes with my day job

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Does this output have at least one specific detail or move that another writer with the same prompt would not have produced? (2) Did I avoid the obvious version of this? If either is no, sharpen.

## OUTPUT
6 ideas with the three required fields, then the two recommendations.
notes: The 'closest to what I want' and 'teaches me most' are usually different ideas - pick deliberately.

title: Conversation starter brainstorm for a specific event
use_when: You have an event coming up where you'll meet new people and want openers that aren't 'what do you do.'
prompt:
Generate 10 conversation starters for [EVENT_TYPE_AND_CONTEXT - e.g., industry conference dinner, friend's wedding where I know nobody, neighborhood block party].

Spread across:
- 3 observational (about the room, the food, the venue)
- 3 curious (asking about something the person obviously chose - book, tattoo, jacket)
- 2 light-stakes opinion (asking them to pick between two things)
- 2 situational (specific to this event - the speaker, the host, the occasion)

For each, include the line itself and the natural follow-up question if they answer briefly.

Do NOT include "so what do you do?" or any variant. Do NOT include questions that require them to perform expertise.
notes: The follow-up question matters more than the opener - that's where conversations actually start.

title: Solution brainstorm for a stuck personal problem
use_when: You've been stuck on a personal-life problem and your usual thinking has run out - you want fresh angles.
prompt:
## MISSION
Generate 8 distinct approaches to [STUCK_PROBLEM], each from a different problem-solving stance. Then identify which one I've avoided and why.

## AGENT
You are a thinking partner with the discretion of a therapist and the bluntness of a friend who has known me 20 years. You don't moralize and you don't optimize.

## SITUATION
The problem in 2 sentences: [PROBLEM]
What I've tried and how it went: [ATTEMPTS]
What I'm afraid the answer is: [FEAR]
What changes if I do nothing for another year: [STATUS_QUO_PROJECTION]

## TASK
Generate 8 approaches, one from each stance:
1. Acceptance: stop trying to solve and live with it
2. Subtraction: remove something currently in the picture
3. Addition: bring in a new resource, person, or skill
4. Time: change the timeline (slower, faster, defer, force)
5. Reframe: define the problem differently
6. Constraint: impose a rule that forces a decision
7. Help: ask someone specific for something specific
8. Exit: leave the situation entirely

For each, write the move in one sentence. Then name the one I've been avoiding and write one sentence on why.

## THINK
Before producing the output: (a) name the obvious version that almost any writer would produce, (b) name what would make this output uniquely the user's instead of generic, (c) name the move you are most tempted to make that would be too safe. Use this to shape the output.

## RULES
- Do NOT moralize about which is "healthiest"
- Do NOT recommend therapy as a substitute for an answer
- The avoidance call must be honest, not flattering
- Do NOT skip stance 8 even if it seems extreme

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Does this output have at least one specific detail or move that another writer with the same prompt would not have produced? (2) Did I avoid the obvious version of this? If either is no, sharpen.

## OUTPUT
8 stances with their moves, then the avoidance call.
notes: The stance you avoided is usually the one carrying the answer.

title: Counter-position brainstorm - 5 reasons someone disagrees
use_when: You hold a position on [TOPIC] and want to genuinely understand the strongest opposing views, not strawmen.
prompt:
I believe [MY_POSITION] about [TOPIC]. Generate the 5 strongest reasons a thoughtful person disagrees with me. Each must:
- Be the kind of argument I would have to take seriously, not a fringe view
- Come from a different vantage point (different values, different data, different experience, different time horizon, different stakeholder)
- Be stated in the disagreer's own framing, not mine
- Include the one piece of evidence they'd cite

After the 5, identify which one is hardest for me to dismiss and why. Then name the weakest of the 5 and explain why my position handles it well.

Do NOT include any "they just don't understand" arguments. If I could win the disagreement by explaining harder, it doesn't go on the list.
notes: The argument you can't dismiss is the one worth updating on - or worth answering publicly.

title: What would [specific person or role] do here?
use_when: You're facing a decision and want to borrow the mental model of someone whose judgment you trust on this kind of thing.
prompt:
I'm facing [SPECIFIC_DECISION_OR_SITUATION]. Walk me through how [SPECIFIC_PERSON_OR_ROLE - e.g., Charlie Munger, a 30-year ER nurse, my grandmother, a defense lawyer, a touring drummer] would think about this.

Cover:
- What they would notice first that I'm missing
- The question they'd ask me before giving advice
- The decision criteria they'd apply (in their actual vocabulary)
- The move they'd make
- What they'd warn me about that I haven't considered

Stay in their voice and worldview throughout. Do NOT genericize them into "a wise advisor." If I named a real person, use what's publicly known about how they actually think.

End by naming one thing this person would do that I won't do, and why that gap exists.
notes: The 'what won't I do' line is where you learn what your real constraint is.

title: Feynman-technique explainer (smart 12-year-old)
use_when: You want to test whether you actually understand [CONCEPT] by forcing an explanation that bans jargon.
prompt:
## MISSION
Explain [CONCEPT_I_WANT_TO_UNDERSTAND] to a smart 12-year-old in under 250 words, then expose the 3 places the simplification leaks.

## AGENT
You are a teacher in the Feynman tradition. You believe that if you can't explain it without the field's vocabulary, you don't yet understand it. You also believe that hiding the leaks is dishonest pedagogy.

## SITUATION
Concept: [CONCEPT]
Why I care about understanding it: [PURPOSE - e.g., to use it, to teach it, to make a decision]
What field this comes from: [FIELD]
My current level: [WHAT_I_ALREADY_KNOW]

## TASK
1. Write the 250-word explanation. Use everyday objects and situations. Use no field-specific terms unless you define them in the same sentence.
2. After the explanation, list 3 places where the simplification is technically wrong or misleading.
3. For each leak, write the one-sentence correction the 12-year-old gets later if they keep studying.

## THINK
Before answering: (a) distinguish what you actually know vs. what you are pattern-completing, (b) identify the claim in your draft most likely to be wrong, (c) flag where you would need primary-source verification. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Do NOT use words like "essentially," "fundamentally," or "basically" - they signal a missing explanation
- Do NOT use analogies you don't unpack (no bare "it's like a wave")
- The leaks section must name real conceptual gaps, not "the math is more complex"
- Do NOT exceed 250 words in the main explanation

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I mark any claim I am not certain about with [UNCERTAIN] or [UNVERIFIED]? (2) For any cited source, can the user actually find it in 30 seconds with the description I gave? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
The explanation, then the leaks table.
notes: The leaks section is where the real learning happens - it tells you what you still don't know.

title: Concept map / mental model builder
use_when: You're learning a new field and need to see how the major concepts connect, not just memorize them in a list.
prompt:
## MISSION
Build a concept map of [FIELD_OR_SUBFIELD] showing 10-15 core concepts and the relationships between them.

## AGENT
You are a textbook author who has taught the field for 20 years. You know that learners struggle because textbooks present concepts sequentially, but the field is a graph, not a list.

## SITUATION
Field: [FIELD]
My current level: [LEVEL - beginner / has read a book or two / professional adjacent]
What I want to do with this knowledge: [GOAL]
Concepts I already half-know: [PRIOR_EXPOSURE]

## TASK
1. List the 10-15 core concepts of the field. For each, give a one-sentence definition.
2. For each concept, name what it depends on (which other concepts you must understand first).
3. Identify the 3 "keystone" concepts - the ones that, once understood, unlock everything else.
4. Draw the map as an indented text outline showing the dependency structure.
5. Recommend the order I should learn them in, with the keystone first.

## THINK
Before answering: (a) distinguish what you actually know vs. what you are pattern-completing, (b) identify the claim in your draft most likely to be wrong, (c) flag where you would need primary-source verification. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Do NOT list more than 15 concepts - the constraint forces real prioritization
- Do NOT define a concept using a term that appears later in the list without flagging the forward reference
- The keystone concepts must include at least one that isn't typically taught first

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I mark any claim I am not certain about with [UNCERTAIN] or [UNVERIFIED]? (2) For any cited source, can the user actually find it in 30 seconds with the description I gave? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Numbered concept list with definitions, the dependency outline, the keystones, the recommended learning order.
notes: The non-obvious keystone is usually what separates good teachers from textbook ones.

title: Analogy generator - 3 candidates with best pick
use_when: You're teaching or writing about [CONCEPT] and want analogies strong enough to carry weight, not just decorate.
prompt:
Generate 3 candidate analogies for [CONCEPT]. For each, fully unpack:
- Source domain (the familiar thing)
- The specific mapping (what corresponds to what - at least 4 points)
- Where the analogy holds tightly
- Where it breaks down (and what misconception that risks creating)

Then pick the best analogy for [SPECIFIC_AUDIENCE] and explain why it beats the others for THAT audience.

Do NOT propose an analogy you can't map in at least 4 points. If you can only say "it's like a tree," it's a metaphor, not an analogy.
notes: The 'where it breaks down' part is what makes the analogy safe to teach with.

title: Prerequisites checklist for learning [X]
use_when: You want to learn [SUBJECT] and need to know what you actually need to know first - not the bloated 'recommended background' list.
prompt:
I want to learn [SUBJECT_OR_SKILL] well enough to [SPECIFIC_OUTCOME - e.g., read research papers, ship a project, pass a certification, hold a conversation with an expert]. My current background: [WHAT_I_KNOW_AND_DON'T].

Produce a true prerequisites list, separating:
- MUST KNOW FIRST: the 3-5 things without which the subject is incomprehensible
- HELPFUL: things that make learning faster but aren't blockers
- OFTEN LISTED BUT ACTUALLY OPTIONAL: prerequisites the field claims but you can skip given my goal
- WILL PICK UP NATURALLY: don't pre-study these, they emerge from doing the main work

For each MUST KNOW, name the single best resource (book, course, video) that gets me there in under 10 hours.

Do NOT recommend "math" or "programming" as a prerequisite - name the specific topics (linear algebra through eigenvectors; Python control flow and dictionaries).
notes: Most prerequisite lists are written by people who want you to take their other courses - separate the real from the inherited.

title: Why is this hard? - common misconception map
use_when: You're starting to learn [TOPIC] and want to know where most learners get stuck and why, so you can avoid the same traps.
prompt:
For [TOPIC_I_AM_LEARNING], list the 5 most common misconceptions that trip up learners at my level ([CURRENT_LEVEL]).

For each:
- The misconception stated in the learner's own words (what they'd actually say)
- Why it's intuitive (where it comes from - usually a leaky analogy from earlier learning)
- The specific moment in study when this misconception shows up
- The corrective frame that replaces it
- A test question whose answer is right only if you've shed the misconception

Do NOT list "it's complicated" or "it takes practice" as a reason something is hard. Name the actual conceptual hurdle.
notes: Knowing the misconception in advance lets you catch yourself making it.

title: Steel-thread learning plan (minimum viable understanding)
use_when: You need functional understanding of [TOPIC] fast for a real situation - not mastery, just enough to act.
prompt:
## MISSION
Build a steel-thread learning plan that gets me from current ignorance to functional competence in [TOPIC] for [SPECIFIC_SITUATION] in [TIMEFRAME].

## AGENT
You are an instructor who specializes in adult learners under deadline pressure. You know that mastery is the wrong target - situational competence is. You also know that learners default to consuming, not practicing, and you push back on that.

## SITUATION
The situation forcing the learning: [WHY_I_NEED_THIS - e.g., job interview, meeting with experts, building one specific thing]
Timeframe: [TIME_AVAILABLE]
Hours per day I can commit: [HOURS]
What "competent enough" looks like in the situation: [SUCCESS_CRITERIA]

## TASK
1. Identify the 20% of [TOPIC] that handles 80% of the situation. Be specific - name the concepts, not the field.
2. Build a day-by-day plan that splits time 30% reading/watching, 70% doing or speaking back.
3. For each day, name the one artifact I produce (a written explanation, a working prototype, a list of questions, a teach-back).
4. Identify what I can safely ignore even though it's "important" - explain why it's not on the critical path for THIS situation.
5. Include a checkpoint on day 3 (or one-third of the way) for honest reassessment.

## THINK
Before answering: (a) distinguish what you actually know vs. what you are pattern-completing, (b) identify the claim in your draft most likely to be wrong, (c) flag where you would need primary-source verification. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Do NOT recommend a textbook as the primary resource - too slow for the timeframe
- Do NOT include any day that is purely passive consumption
- The "safely ignore" list must contain at least 3 items real teachers of the field would push back on
- Day-by-day plan, not week-by-week

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I mark any claim I am not certain about with [UNCERTAIN] or [UNVERIFIED]? (2) For any cited source, can the user actually find it in 30 seconds with the description I gave? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
The 20% list, the day-by-day plan, the ignore list, the checkpoint criteria.
notes: The 'safely ignore' list is the most valuable part - it gives you permission to be functionally competent without being a fraud.

title: Active-recall flashcards from a topic
use_when: You've studied [TOPIC] and want flashcards built around recall and synthesis, not trivia or definitions.
prompt:
Generate 20 active-recall flashcards for [TOPIC_AND_SCOPE]. Mix of card types:
- 6 "explain why" cards (not "what" - "why does X happen?")
- 5 "given this scenario, predict what happens" cards
- 4 "compare and contrast" cards
- 3 "common mistake" cards (the answer is the trap that catches most learners)
- 2 "synthesis" cards (connect this topic to a different topic I know)

Format each as:
FRONT: [the question]
BACK: [the answer in 1-3 sentences, including the reasoning]

Do NOT generate definition-only cards (no "what is X?"). Definitions are the lowest tier of learning and waste flashcard time.
notes: Definitions belong in a glossary - flashcards should make you reason, not recite.

title: Spaced-repetition schedule generator
use_when: You have a finite set of material to learn by a date and want a review schedule that uses spacing instead of cramming.
prompt:
Build a spaced-repetition schedule for [MATERIAL - what it is and rough volume] with a target date of [DATE] and review sessions available [WHICH_DAYS_AND_HOW_LONG].

Output a calendar showing:
- When new material is introduced
- When each batch is reviewed (1 day later, 3 days, 7 days, 14 days, ~30 days)
- Cumulative review load per session (cap at 60% of session time so 40% stays available for new material)
- The 3 days before the target date - what to do (and not do) in those days

Do NOT schedule new material in the final 5 days. Do NOT exceed the session cap - if the schedule overflows, push the target date or cut scope.
notes: The cap forces honesty - if it overflows, you don't have a study plan, you have a wish.

title: Compare-and-contrast two confusable concepts
use_when: You keep mixing up [CONCEPT_A] and [CONCEPT_B] and want a precise discrimination, not a fuzzy 'they're related.'
prompt:
Compare and contrast [CONCEPT_A] and [CONCEPT_B], both from [FIELD], which I keep confusing.

Structure:
1. The one-sentence definition of each in plain language
2. A table of 5 dimensions on which they differ, with each concept's value on that dimension
3. The single distinguishing test - the question I can ask of any new example to tell which one I'm looking at
4. Two short example scenarios - one of A, one of B - that I'd misclassify if I confused them
5. The historical or linguistic reason they get confused (where did the confusion come from?)

Do NOT say "they're similar but different." Specify the difference precisely enough that someone using your test can't go wrong.
notes: The single distinguishing test is the heart of the prompt - without it you've described both concepts but not separated them.

title: Glossary builder with examples
use_when: You're entering a new field and the jargon is a wall - you want a glossary that includes how the terms are actually used, not dictionary definitions.
prompt:
Build a glossary of the 15 most-used terms in [FIELD_OR_SUBFIELD] that a newcomer hears on day one.

For each term:
- Definition in plain language (one sentence)
- A real sentence using the term the way a practitioner would say it
- The most common adjacent term it gets confused with
- Whether the term has different meanings in different sub-communities of the field (and which)

Order the glossary by frequency of use in real practitioner conversation, not alphabetically. Do NOT include archaic or textbook-only terms. Do NOT define a term using two other terms from the same list without cross-referencing.
notes: The 'used in a real sentence' line is what makes a glossary functional instead of decorative.

title: Teach-this-back - Socratic test of my understanding
use_when: You think you understand [CONCEPT] and want a Socratic interrogator to find the holes by asking, not telling.
prompt:
## MISSION
Test my understanding of [CONCEPT] by asking me questions, one at a time, that probe for genuine comprehension versus pattern-matching.

## AGENT
You are a Socratic examiner who has caught thousands of students confusing fluency with understanding. You ask questions, you do not lecture. You praise specifically and rarely. You don't move on until the answer is solid.

## SITUATION
Concept: [CONCEPT]
Why I'm learning it: [PURPOSE]
My claimed level of understanding: [SELF_ASSESSMENT]
A specific scenario where I want to apply it: [APPLICATION]

## TASK
Conduct the exam by:
1. Starting with a definition question in my own words
2. Following up with a "why is it that way" question
3. Probing with a scenario where the concept applies in a non-obvious way
4. Asking for a case where the concept does NOT apply (limits of the model)
5. Asking me to predict what happens when one variable changes
6. Closing with: "what's a question about this you can't yet answer?"

Wait for my answer between each question. If my answer is weak, ask a clarifying question - do not give me the answer. If my answer is strong, say "good" in 5 words or less and move on.

## THINK
Before answering: (a) distinguish what you actually know vs. what you are pattern-completing, (b) identify the claim in your draft most likely to be wrong, (c) flag where you would need primary-source verification. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Do NOT give me the answer when I'm wrong - ask a smaller question that helps me find it
- Do NOT ask more than one question per turn
- Do NOT skip step 6 - admitting an unanswerable question is the proof of real understanding
- Be sparing with praise

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I mark any claim I am not certain about with [UNCERTAIN] or [UNVERIFIED]? (2) For any cited source, can the user actually find it in 30 seconds with the description I gave? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Start with question 1. Wait for my answer.
notes: The last question - 'what can't you yet answer' - is the real assessment.

title: Hardest-question generator (the stumper)
use_when: You're preparing to be tested or interviewed on [TOPIC] and want to face the questions you'd most fear, not the easy ones.
prompt:
For [TOPIC_AND_CONTEXT - e.g., a job interview for X role, a PhD oral exam in Y, a debate with an expert], generate 7 questions an examiner could ask that would expose shallow understanding.

Mix:
- 2 questions about the limits or failure modes of standard models
- 2 questions about historical or contingent reasons the field looks the way it does
- 1 question about a recent development that updates traditional thinking
- 1 question requiring synthesis with an adjacent field
- 1 question with no clean answer, asked to see how I handle uncertainty

For each, include a one-line note on what kind of answer signals real understanding versus rehearsal.

Do NOT include trivia or memorizable facts. Stumpers test reasoning, not recall.
notes: The 'no clean answer' question is the most revealing - it tests judgment, not knowledge.

title: Reading list curator - 3 books, 5 articles, 2 talks
use_when: You want to go deep on [TOPIC] and need a curated reading list, not a 50-item dump.
prompt:
Curate a reading list for [TOPIC] at [LEVEL - beginner / intermediate / serious]:

3 BOOKS: one foundational (the book the field built on), one accessible (the best entry point today), one contrarian (challenges the consensus).

5 ARTICLES OR PAPERS: spread across decades - at least one pre-2000 if the field is that old. Prefer pieces with lasting citation over recent buzz.

2 TALKS OR LECTURES: prefer ones where you can see the speaker think, not polished keynote performances.

For each item: title, author, one-sentence reason it's on the list, the order to consume it in across the full 10, and the one item to start with TODAY.

Do NOT include any book you'd recommend just because it's famous. Do NOT include "[Topic] for Dummies" or trade-paperback summaries.
notes: The contrarian book matters more than the foundational one - it's what keeps you from groupthink.

title: Course outline / syllabus draft
use_when: You want to teach [SUBJECT] (to yourself, a colleague, a class) and need a syllabus that sequences for understanding, not coverage.
prompt:
## MISSION
Draft a [N]-week course outline for [SUBJECT] taught to [AUDIENCE]. The course must build genuine capability, not coverage.

## MISSION
Produce a course outline or syllabus draft that gets a learner from current state to target competency without padding - each module load-bearing on a specific learning outcome.

## AGENT
You are a curriculum designer who has built courses for both universities and bootcamps. You know university syllabi optimize for coverage and bootcamps optimize for skill - pick the right bias for the audience.

## SITUATION
Subject: [SUBJECT_AND_SCOPE]
Audience: [WHO_THEY_ARE_AND_WHY_THEY'RE_TAKING_IT]
Duration: [WEEKS_AND_HOURS_PER_WEEK]
What students should be able to DO at the end: [TERMINAL_CAPABILITY]
Resources available: [BUDGET, TOOLS, GUEST_ACCESS]

## TASK
1. State the terminal capability in one sentence using a verb a student would perform.
2. Work backward: name the 4-6 sub-capabilities required.
3. Sequence them across the weeks, with dependencies respected.
4. For each week, list: the sub-capability built, the core idea taught, the one assignment that proves the capability, and the assessment criterion.
5. Identify the week where most students will hit the wall, and what's in place to catch them.
6. Name one topic you'd be expected to cover that you're cutting, and why.

## THINK
Before answering: (a) distinguish what you actually know vs. what you are pattern-completing, (b) identify the claim in your draft most likely to be wrong, (c) flag where you would need primary-source verification. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Do NOT structure by topic - structure by capability built
- Do NOT have a week without a graded artifact (even self-graded)
- The "wall week" must be identified explicitly, not glossed
- The "cut topic" must be defensible - it costs you something

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I mark any claim I am not certain about with [UNCERTAIN] or [UNVERIFIED]? (2) For any cited source, can the user actually find it in 30 seconds with the description I gave? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
The terminal capability, the sub-capability map, the week-by-week table, the wall-week plan, the cut-topic note.
notes: The 'wall week' identification is what separates a real curriculum from a list of lectures.

title: Show-your-work worked-example walkthrough
use_when: You learn best from seeing a worked example with reasoning, not just the formula - walk me through [PROBLEM_TYPE].
prompt:
Walk me through a worked example of [PROBLEM_TYPE - e.g., setting up a Bayesian inference problem, structuring a cap table, diagnosing a slow SQL query] at [DIFFICULTY - introductory / realistic / advanced].

Show:
1. The problem statement, with one ambiguity you flag and resolve
2. Your reading of the problem - what type of problem this is and what tool that suggests
3. The setup step, with reasoning for each choice
4. The execution, narrating what you'd think (not just what you'd write)
5. The sanity check - how you know the answer isn't crazy
6. Two common student mistakes at the equivalent step

Do NOT skip any step. Do NOT present the clean version - present the version where you'd notice and correct an off-by-one or unit error.
notes: The sanity check is what separates someone who can solve the problem from someone who can be trusted with the answer.

title: Common pitfalls list when learning [X]
use_when: You're at month 2-3 of learning [SKILL_OR_SUBJECT] and want to know which traps people fall into at exactly this stage.
prompt:
I'm at month [N] of learning [SKILL_OR_SUBJECT]. List the 6 most common pitfalls people hit at exactly this stage - not beginner mistakes, not advanced ones, but the ones specific to where I am.

For each:
- The pitfall described as the learner's actual behavior
- Why this stage triggers it (what made beginner-me protected from this trap)
- The early symptom I can catch in myself this week
- The corrective action - what I do INSTEAD

Do NOT include generic "study harder" or "find a mentor" pitfalls. Name behaviors specific to month [N] of this subject.
notes: Stage-specific pitfalls are different from beginner ones - month-2 traps come from premature confidence.

title: Explain a complex paper / chapter / book to me
use_when: You're trying to understand a dense academic paper or technical chapter and want a structured unpacking, not a summary.
prompt:
## MISSION
Help me understand [PAPER_OR_CHAPTER_TITLE_AND_AUTHOR]. I will paste the text or describe it. Produce a structured unpacking that lets me engage with the source, not replace reading it.

## AGENT
You are a graduate-level reading partner. You know the difference between a summary (which substitutes for the source) and an unpacking (which prepares me to read it well).

## SITUATION
Source: [WHAT_I'M_READING]
My background in the field: [LEVEL]
Why I'm reading this: [PURPOSE - coursework, research, decision, curiosity]
What I've understood so far: [CURRENT_UNDERSTANDING]
The part that's confusing me most: [SPECIFIC_CONFUSION]

## TASK
1. State the paper's central claim in one sentence using the author's vocabulary.
2. Identify the prior work it's responding to - the position the author is arguing against.
3. Map the argument structure: premise → premise → conclusion, with one sentence per node.
4. Explain the methodology in terms of what it can and cannot show.
5. Name the 2 strongest objections a reviewer would raise.
6. Tell me what to read BEFORE this if I'm missing context, and what to read AFTER if I'm convinced.
7. End with the question the paper does NOT answer.

## THINK
Before answering: (a) distinguish what you actually know vs. what you are pattern-completing, (b) identify the claim in your draft most likely to be wrong, (c) flag where you would need primary-source verification. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Do NOT produce a stand-alone summary that lets me skip reading
- Do NOT smooth over confusion - if a section is genuinely hard, name the hard part
- The objections must be ones a hostile reviewer would actually raise, not soft critiques
- Do NOT skip step 7

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I mark any claim I am not certain about with [UNCERTAIN] or [UNVERIFIED]? (2) For any cited source, can the user actually find it in 30 seconds with the description I gave? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
The 7 sections in order.
notes: The 'what the paper doesn't answer' question is what turns reading into research.

title: Translate jargon-heavy text to plain language
use_when: You have a piece of text loaded with field-specific jargon and want a plain-language version that doesn't lose precision.
prompt:
Translate the following text from [FIELD]-speak into plain language for [AUDIENCE]:

[PASTE_TEXT]

Rules for the translation:
- Replace every jargon term with a plain-language equivalent, but flag the original term in parentheses on first use so I can map back
- Preserve any technical precision - if the original distinguishes two things, the translation must too
- Note the 2-3 places where plain language genuinely loses something the jargon carried, and what gets lost
- Note any places where the jargon was actually hiding vagueness, not adding precision

Do NOT smooth over the lossy spots. Honest translation acknowledges them.
notes: The 'jargon hiding vagueness' callout is where you learn whether the original was rigorous or pretentious.

title: Generate practice problems with answers
use_when: You've learned [TOPIC] and want practice problems that test understanding, not just procedure, with answers I can check.
prompt:
Generate 8 practice problems for [TOPIC_AND_SCOPE] at [LEVEL].

Mix:
- 3 procedure problems (apply a known method)
- 3 understanding problems (the method is obvious but you must reason about which to use and why)
- 2 transfer problems (the topic shows up in an unfamiliar context)

For each problem:
- The problem statement (clear, no trick wording)
- The full answer with reasoning shown step by step
- The single most common wrong answer and what mistaken thinking produces it

Do NOT generate problems where the answer is just plugging into a formula - those don't test understanding. Do NOT include problems whose "common wrong answer" is just arithmetic error.
notes: The 'common wrong answer' diagnosis is where these become learning problems instead of just exercise problems.

title: Concept-to-real-world mapping
use_when: You learned [CONCEPT] in the abstract and want to see where it actually shows up in life or work, not in textbook examples.
prompt:
For [CONCEPT_I_LEARNED], give me 6 real-world examples where this concept is actively at work, beyond textbook cases:

- 2 from everyday life (things I encounter without noticing)
- 2 from a specific industry: [INDUSTRY_OF_INTEREST]
- 1 from a current event in the last 6 months
- 1 from a domain that seems unrelated to where the concept was originally developed

For each, name: what's happening, where the concept shows up specifically (not vaguely), and what changes about the example if you understand the concept versus don't.

Do NOT use textbook examples (no apples-and-oranges for set theory, no falling balls for gravity). Do NOT use examples where the concept is just labeled on top - show it doing work.
notes: The 'unrelated domain' example is the one that proves the concept is real and not just a field-specific convention.

title: What experts disagree on within a field
use_when: You're learning [FIELD] and the textbooks present consensus - you want to know where actual practitioners genuinely disagree.
prompt:
For [FIELD_OR_SUBFIELD], identify 5 questions where serious experts genuinely disagree right now (not historical debates, not fringe vs. mainstream - actual current disagreement among credentialed practitioners).

For each disagreement:
- The question stated neutrally
- Camp A's position and the lead figure or school associated with it
- Camp B's position and the lead figure or school
- The empirical evidence each side cites
- Why the disagreement persists (different values, different data, different time horizons, methodological differences)
- What a resolution would require

Do NOT present settled questions as if they were open. Do NOT include disagreements where one side is fringe - both camps must be respectable.
notes: Knowing the live debates is what turns a student into a participant in the field.

title: History-of-the-idea explainer
use_when: You want to understand [CONCEPT] by tracing how it was developed, not just what its current form is.
prompt:
Tell the history of [CONCEPT_OR_THEORY] in 5 chapters:

1. THE PROBLEM that existed before the concept - what was unsolved or poorly handled
2. THE FIRST FORMULATION - who proposed it, in what context, and what they got right and wrong
3. THE KEY REFINEMENT - the major correction or generalization that made it usable
4. THE MODERN FORM - what the concept looks like today, and what's still debated
5. THE NEXT FRONTIER - where the concept is currently being pushed and likely to evolve

For each chapter, name the key figure(s) and one accessible source to read more.

Do NOT present the history as a clean march of progress. Include at least one dead-end or reversal where the field thought it had the answer and didn't.
notes: The dead-end inclusion is what makes intellectual history honest instead of triumphalist.

title: Decision tree - should I learn this?
use_when: You're considering whether to invest serious time learning [SKILL_OR_TOPIC] and want a structured way to decide, not just enthusiasm.
prompt:
Help me decide whether to commit serious time (50+ hours) to learning [SKILL_OR_TOPIC].

My context: [CURRENT_ROLE, GOALS, COMPETING_PRIORITIES, OPPORTUNITY_COST].

Walk me through this decision tree:

1. What specific outcome would learning this unlock that I don't already have access to?
2. Is the half-life of this knowledge long (>5 years) or short (<2 years)? If short, is the speed of acquisition worth the decay?
3. Is the easier substitute (hire someone, use a tool, partner with someone) genuinely cheaper than learning?
4. What's the minimum useful proficiency, and how long to reach it (not mastery - minimum useful)?
5. What's the highest-impact thing I'd give up time on to learn this?
6. If I imagine myself 12 months in and I never finished, what's the likeliest reason?

End with a clear LEARN / DON'T LEARN / LEARN LATER recommendation and the one condition that would flip your answer.
notes: Question 6 (the imagined failure) is the most predictive - if you can already write the failure story, listen to it.

title: Personal study plan from current level to goal
use_when: You know where you are with [SKILL] and where you want to be, and need a concrete plan to get from here to there.
prompt:
## MISSION
Build a personal study plan to take me from my current level in [SKILL] to my target level in [TIMEFRAME], using only the time and resources I actually have.

## AGENT
You are a coach who builds plans for working adults. You know plans fail because they're aspirational, not because they're insufficiently ambitious. You build for the average week, not the best week.

## SITUATION
Skill: [SKILL_AND_SPECIFIC_DOMAIN]
Current level (with evidence, not self-rating): [CURRENT_LEVEL]
Target level (with concrete capability): [TARGET_LEVEL]
Timeframe: [TARGET_DATE]
Hours per week in an average week (not best week): [REALISTIC_HOURS]
Resources I have access to: [TOOLS, MENTORS, COURSES, COMMUNITIES]
Past attempts and what derailed them: [HISTORY]

## TASK
1. Diagnose the gap: name the 3-4 specific sub-skills between current and target.
2. Sequence the sub-skills with dependencies.
3. Build a weekly plan template: which sub-skill is the focus, what's the practice block, what's the artifact produced that week.
4. Specify the monthly checkpoint and what evidence would make me believe progress is real.
5. Pre-mortem: based on my past-attempt history, the most likely derailment is X. Build the specific countermeasure into the plan.
6. Name one thing I should stop doing to make room for this.

## THINK
Before answering: (a) distinguish what you actually know vs. what you are pattern-completing, (b) identify the claim in your draft most likely to be wrong, (c) flag where you would need primary-source verification. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Do NOT plan for more than 80% of stated weekly hours - leave slack for life
- Do NOT recommend resources I haven't said I have access to
- The countermeasure must be specific to my past pattern, not generic willpower advice
- The "stop doing" must be real - a genuine subtraction, not a polite suggestion

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I mark any claim I am not certain about with [UNCERTAIN] or [UNVERIFIED]? (2) For any cited source, can the user actually find it in 30 seconds with the description I gave? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Gap diagnosis, sub-skill sequence, weekly template, monthly checkpoint, derailment countermeasure, stop-doing item.
notes: The 'what to stop doing' is the load-bearing part - plans without subtraction collapse under the weight of existing commitments.

title: Code review for security, performance, correctness
use_when: Reviewing a PR or diff before merge and you want a structured triage of risks, not generic 'looks good.'
prompt:
## MISSION
Review [DIFF / PR] and produce a structured triage that names the specific bugs, security issues, and performance risks, ranked by severity. Success means I can prioritize the next 30 minutes of review.

## AGENT
You are a senior engineer who has caught real production bugs in code review. You distinguish style nits from blocking issues. You name the specific line, the specific failure mode, and the specific fix.

## SITUATION
The diff: [PASTE_DIFF]
What this code does: [FEATURE_CONTEXT]
Language/framework: [STACK e.g. Python 3.11 + FastAPI]
Highest-risk paths: [SENSITIVE_PATHS e.g. auth, billing, PII handling]

## TASK
1. Blocking issues: file:line + failure mode + concrete fix.
2. Should-fix-before-merge: file:line + reason.
3. Nice-to-have: defer-able items.
4. One thing I should test before merging that the diff doesn't cover.

## THINK
Before producing the code or analysis: (a) identify the failure modes that would matter most in production for this stack, (b) identify what the simplest correct version looks like, (c) note where your output is pattern-matching from training vs. reasoning from the specific code in front of you. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Do NOT include "consider renaming for clarity" unless it changes meaning.
- Do NOT flag style if no linter would catch it.
- Do NOT use "best practice" as a justification - name the specific failure.
- Each issue must name the exact file:line.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I name specific file:line locations for every issue (not vague 'consider')? (2) Would this code pass the actual failure mode I described - or does it just look right? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Three labeled sections + one explicit test recommendation. No preamble.
notes: The 'name the exact line' rule is the lift - generic code reviews are useless.

title: Explain this code (legacy understanding)
use_when: Inheriting a function you didn't write and need to understand what it actually does, not what comments claim it does.
prompt:
## MISSION
Explain what [FUNCTION_NAME] in [FILE_PATH] actually does, including side effects and assumptions that aren't obvious from the signature. Success means I could rewrite it from your explanation.

## AGENT
You are a reverse-engineer who reads code by tracing data flow, not by trusting variable names or comments. You flag where the code disagrees with what it appears to do.

## SITUATION
The code: [PASTE]
Where it's called from: [CALL_SITES]
What I think it does: [MY_GUESS]

## TASK
1. One-sentence summary of actual behavior.
2. Inputs: types + any silent assumptions (non-null, sorted, etc.).
3. Outputs: type + edge cases (empty, error states).
4. Side effects: I/O, mutations, global state, exceptions thrown.
5. Where my guess is wrong, if it is.
6. One trap a new caller would fall into.

## THINK
Before producing the code or analysis: (a) identify the failure modes that would matter most in production for this stack, (b) identify what the simplest correct version looks like, (c) note where your output is pattern-matching from training vs. reasoning from the specific code in front of you. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Do NOT paraphrase variable names back as explanation.
- Do NOT trust the docstring - read the code.
- If behavior depends on a config value, say which one.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I name specific file:line locations for every issue (not vague 'consider')? (2) Would this code pass the actual failure mode I described - or does it just look right? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Six labeled sections. Be specific about types and exceptions.
notes: The 'trap a new caller would fall into' line catches the implicit contract violations.

title: Refactor for readability without changing behavior
use_when: Code works but is hard to read; you want cleanup that won't break tests.
prompt:
Refactor [FUNCTION / FILE] for readability. Behavior must be identical - same inputs produce same outputs, same exceptions, same side effects.

Code: [PASTE]
Language: [LANG]
Existing test coverage: [COVERAGE_STATUS]

Deliver:
1. Refactored code.
2. Diff summary: what changed and why (max 5 bullets).
3. Any behavior I should verify with a test before trusting the refactor.

Constraints:
- No new dependencies.
- No renaming of public API symbols.
- Preserve all error messages exactly (logs may be parsed downstream).
notes: The 'preserve error messages exactly' constraint stops silent log-format breakage.

title: Refactor for performance with measurable goal
use_when: Function is too slow and you have a concrete latency target.
prompt:
## MISSION
Refactor [FUNCTION] so it runs in under [TARGET_MS] ms for input size [N]. Current measured time: [CURRENT_MS] ms. Success is a version that hits the target with a test demonstrating it.

## AGENT
You are a performance engineer who profiles before optimizing. You distinguish algorithmic wins (Big-O) from constant-factor tricks. You name the bottleneck before suggesting changes.

## SITUATION
Code: [PASTE]
Hot loop / suspected bottleneck: [LINE_RANGE]
Profile output if available: [PROFILE_DATA]
Constraints: [MEMORY_BUDGET, READABILITY_BUDGET]

## TASK
1. Diagnose: which operation dominates, and why.
2. Propose change ranked by expected speedup.
3. Show refactored code.
4. Show a benchmark snippet that proves the speedup.
5. Trade-offs (memory, readability, edge cases).

## THINK
Before producing the code or analysis: (a) identify the failure modes that would matter most in production for this stack, (b) identify what the simplest correct version looks like, (c) note where your output is pattern-matching from training vs. reasoning from the specific code in front of you. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Do NOT recommend "use numpy" or "use Cython" without showing the specific operation.
- Do NOT optimize before naming the bottleneck.
- Any algorithmic change must include a correctness argument vs. the original.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I name specific file:line locations for every issue (not vague 'consider')? (2) Would this code pass the actual failure mode I described - or does it just look right? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Diagnosis -> Proposal -> Code -> Benchmark -> Trade-offs.
notes: The benchmark requirement closes the loop - 'should be faster' is not faster.

title: Add unit tests for an untested function
use_when: Function has no tests and you need coverage that catches real bugs, not just hits lines.
prompt:
## MISSION
Write unit tests for [FUNCTION_NAME] that would catch the kinds of bugs that would actually ship. Success means a future regression breaks a named test.

## AGENT
You are a test writer who picks inputs adversarially. You think about boundary values, empty cases, error paths, and silent wrong answers - not just happy path.

## SITUATION
Function: [PASTE]
Test framework: [pytest / jest / etc.]
What the function is supposed to guarantee: [CONTRACT]
Where it's called: [CALLERS]

## TASK
Produce tests in this order:
1. Happy path (one canonical case).
2. Boundary values (empty, single, max).
3. Error paths (invalid input, expected exceptions).
4. Silent-wrong-answer cases (returns something, but wrong).
5. One property-style test if applicable (round-trip, idempotence).

## THINK
Before producing the code or analysis: (a) identify the failure modes that would matter most in production for this stack, (b) identify what the simplest correct version looks like, (c) note where your output is pattern-matching from training vs. reasoning from the specific code in front of you. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Each test must have a name that describes the failure it catches, not the input.
- No assertions on incidental output (timestamps, ordering of dict keys) unless contract requires it.
- Do NOT mock what the function itself does.
- Do NOT write tests whose assertions would still pass if the function body were replaced with a stub returning a constant or the input unchanged.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I name specific file:line locations for every issue (not vague 'consider')? (2) Would this code pass the actual failure mode I described - or does it just look right? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Runnable test file. Each test prefixed with a one-line comment naming the bug it catches.
notes: Naming tests by the bug they catch is what makes them survive future refactors.

title: Add integration tests for an API endpoint
use_when: You have an HTTP endpoint and want tests that exercise the full request/response cycle.
prompt:
Write integration tests for the endpoint [METHOD PATH] in [FRAMEWORK].

Endpoint behavior: [SPEC_OR_CODE]
Auth model: [AUTH e.g. bearer token, session cookie]
Database setup: [FIXTURES_AVAILABLE]
Test framework: [pytest+httpx / supertest / etc.]

Cover, in order:
1. 200 with valid request and assert response shape.
2. 401 / 403 for missing or wrong auth.
3. 400 for each validation rule (one test per rule).
4. 404 for missing resource.
5. One concurrency / idempotency case if the endpoint mutates state.

Constraint: every test must clean up the DB state it created. No order dependence between tests.
notes: The 'no order dependence' constraint kills the flakiest class of integration test bug.

title: Debug a stack trace
use_when: You have a stack trace and want a structured diagnosis, not 'try X, try Y.'
prompt:
## MISSION
Diagnose this stack trace and propose the most likely root cause with a confidence level. Success is a single hypothesis I can test in under 10 minutes.

## AGENT
You are a debugger who reads stack traces top-down for the immediate failure and bottom-up for the trigger. You distinguish symptom from cause.

## SITUATION
Stack trace: [PASTE]
Language/runtime: [LANG_VERSION]
What I was doing when it fired: [REPRO_STEPS]
What changed recently: [RECENT_CHANGES]
Code at the throwing line: [PASTE_IF_AVAILABLE]

## TASK
1. What the exception actually means (not the wording - the underlying state).
2. Most likely root cause + confidence (high/med/low).
3. Two alternative hypotheses if confidence is not high.
4. One command or print I can add to confirm the hypothesis in <10 min.
5. Fix once confirmed.

## THINK
Before producing the code or analysis: (a) identify the failure modes that would matter most in production for this stack, (b) identify what the simplest correct version looks like, (c) note where your output is pattern-matching from training vs. reasoning from the specific code in front of you. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Do NOT list every possible cause - rank them.
- Do NOT say "check your code" - name what to check.
- If the trace points to a library, name the library function and what it expects.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I name specific file:line locations for every issue (not vague 'consider')? (2) Would this code pass the actual failure mode I described - or does it just look right? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Five labeled sections. State confidence explicitly.
notes: Forcing a confidence label stops the 'maybe-it-could-be-anything' answer.

title: Debug 'works locally, fails in prod'
use_when: Code passes on your machine but breaks in production - environment is the suspect.
prompt:
## MISSION
Find the environment difference causing [BEHAVIOR] to work in [LOCAL_ENV] but fail in [PROD_ENV]. Success is a named delta I can verify.

## AGENT
You are an SRE who knows that "works on my machine" is almost always env, data, or concurrency. You ask for diffs in that order before suggesting code changes.

## SITUATION
Local env: [OS, runtime version, deps]
Prod env: [OS, runtime version, deps, container image]
Failure symptom in prod: [LOGS_OR_ERROR]
What's the same: [GIT_SHA, CONFIG_HASH]
What I've already ruled out: [CHECKED]

## TASK
1. Top 3 suspect deltas, ranked.
2. For each: one command to verify whether it's the cause.
3. The minimal local reproduction (Docker / env-var trick) to make local match prod.
4. If none of the above, what to instrument next.

## THINK
Before producing the code or analysis: (a) identify the failure modes that would matter most in production for this stack, (b) identify what the simplest correct version looks like, (c) note where your output is pattern-matching from training vs. reasoning from the specific code in front of you. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Do NOT suggest "add more logging" without saying where.
- Do NOT assume the code is wrong before env is ruled out.
- Each suspect must include the specific difference (version number, env var name, file path).

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I name specific file:line locations for every issue (not vague 'consider')? (2) Would this code pass the actual failure mode I described - or does it just look right? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Three ranked suspects with verification commands + a repro recipe.
notes: Forcing 'name the specific env var' kills vague hand-waving about 'config differences.'

title: Write a regex with safety checks
use_when: You need a regex and want one that handles the edge cases, not a Stack Overflow copy-paste.
prompt:
Write a regex that matches [PATTERN_DESCRIPTION] in [LANGUAGE_FLAVOR e.g. PCRE, Python re, JS].

Must match: [POSITIVE_EXAMPLES - at least 5]
Must NOT match: [NEGATIVE_EXAMPLES - at least 5, including near-misses]

Deliver:
1. The regex.
2. Plain-English explanation of each group.
3. Catastrophic-backtracking check: confirm it's not vulnerable to ReDoS, or flag if it is.
4. A test snippet running it against all examples.

Constraints:
- Prefer readability over cleverness - use named groups and verbose mode if the flavor supports it.
- If a non-regex solution (parser, split) would be more reliable, say so before writing the regex.
notes: The ReDoS check stops you from shipping a regex that hangs on adversarial input.

title: SQL query generation with EXPLAIN review
use_when: You need a query and want to know it'll perform before you run it on prod data.
prompt:
## MISSION
Write SQL that answers [QUESTION] against [SCHEMA], and predict its execution plan. Success is a query that returns the right answer and won't table-scan a billion-row table.

## AGENT
You are a SQL engineer who thinks about indexes before writing JOINs. You know the difference between what a query expresses and what the planner does with it.

## SITUATION
Question: [BUSINESS_QUESTION]
Schema: [TABLES, COLUMNS, TYPES, INDEXES]
Approximate row counts: [ROW_ESTIMATES]
Dialect: [POSTGRES / MYSQL / SNOWFLAKE / etc.]
Performance budget: [TARGET_MS or none]

## TASK
1. The query.
2. Plain-English read: row-by-row what it does.
3. Predicted plan: which indexes it should use, which joins, expected scan vs seek.
4. One EXPLAIN flag to look for that would indicate the plan went wrong.
5. Two ways to rewrite it if the plan is bad.

## THINK
Before producing the code or analysis: (a) identify the failure modes that would matter most in production for this stack, (b) identify what the simplest correct version looks like, (c) note where your output is pattern-matching from training vs. reasoning from the specific code in front of you. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Do NOT use SELECT * in the final query.
- Every JOIN must say which index supports it (or flag the missing one).
- Do NOT wrap indexed columns in functions in WHERE clauses.
- Do NOT use syntax or functions specific to one dialect (window-function syntax that varies, RETURNING, ARRAY types, LATERAL joins, MERGE) without flagging the dialect dependency. If unsure whether a feature exists in the stated dialect, say so.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I name specific file:line locations for every issue (not vague 'consider')? (2) Would this code pass the actual failure mode I described - or does it just look right? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Query -> Read -> Predicted plan -> Watch-for -> Alt rewrites.
notes: Predicting the plan before EXPLAIN runs forces real thinking about indexes.

title: SQL query optimization
use_when: Existing query is slow and you need to make it fast without changing its result.
prompt:
Optimize this SQL query. The result set must be identical to the original.

Current query: [PASTE]
Dialect: [DIALECT]
Schema + indexes: [SCHEMA]
Current EXPLAIN output: [EXPLAIN_PASTE]
Current runtime: [BASELINE_MS]
Target runtime: [TARGET_MS]

Deliver:
1. Diagnosis: which operation in the plan dominates and why.
2. Rewritten query.
3. Any index additions needed (with CREATE INDEX statement).
4. Predicted new plan.
5. A correctness check: a small query that compares row counts and a hash of the old vs new result.

Constraint: if the answer is "add an index," still produce the index DDL and explain trade-offs (write cost, storage).
notes: The result-equality check is what lets you trust the optimization in prod.

title: Convert from one language to another
use_when: You have working code in language A and need a faithful port to language B.
prompt:
Port this code from [SOURCE_LANG] to [TARGET_LANG]. Behavior must match.

Source code: [PASTE]
What it does: [FUNCTIONAL_SUMMARY]
Target language version + standard libs available: [TARGET_DETAILS]

Deliver:
1. Ported code, idiomatic to [TARGET_LANG] (not a transliteration).
2. List of semantic differences I should know (e.g. integer division, default sort stability, exception types).
3. Three test cases that should pass in both versions.

Constraint: if [TARGET_LANG] has a built-in that replaces a 20-line custom loop, use the built-in and note it.
notes: The 'semantic differences' list catches the silent-divergence bugs of porting.

title: Convert callback to async/await
use_when: Old callback-style code needs to become async/await (or the reverse).
prompt:
Convert this code from [callback / async-await / promise chain] style to [target style] in [LANGUAGE].

Code: [PASTE]
Concurrency expectations: [SERIAL or PARALLEL where]
Error-handling expectations: [PROPAGATE / SWALLOW / RETRY]

Deliver:
1. Converted code.
2. Where I had a choice between serial and parallel, what I picked and why.
3. Any error-handling behavior change (a thrown error now propagates where it used to be swallowed, etc.).

Constraint: do not change the function's public signature. Callers must not need to be updated.
notes: The 'parallel where' note catches the perf win or the race condition hidden in the rewrite.

title: Write a Dockerfile for a project
use_when: Need a production-grade Dockerfile, not the toy one from a tutorial.
prompt:
## MISSION
Write a production Dockerfile for [PROJECT] that builds small, builds fast, and runs safely. Success is an image that's under [SIZE_TARGET] and starts in under [START_MS].

## AGENT
You are a container engineer who uses multi-stage builds, pins versions, runs as non-root, and knows that "FROM ubuntu:latest" is a bug. You write Dockerfiles that reproduce a year from now.

## SITUATION
Project: [LANG + FRAMEWORK + VERSION]
Build steps locally: [BUILD_COMMANDS]
Runtime needs: [PORTS, ENV_VARS, VOLUMES]
Size target: [TARGET_MB]
Registry / base image policy: [INTERNAL_BASE or any public]

## TASK
1. Dockerfile with multi-stage build.
2. .dockerignore.
3. One-line build + run commands.
4. Notes: why the base image, what's in each stage, why the layer order.
5. One security check (Trivy / Snyk / etc.) command to run.

## THINK
Before producing the code or analysis: (a) identify the failure modes that would matter most in production for this stack, (b) identify what the simplest correct version looks like, (c) note where your output is pattern-matching from training vs. reasoning from the specific code in front of you. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Pin every base image to a specific digest or version tag - no :latest.
- Run as non-root in the final stage.
- Do NOT copy the whole repo before installing deps (cache-busts the layer).
- No secrets in ENV or build args.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I name specific file:line locations for every issue (not vague 'consider')? (2) Would this code pass the actual failure mode I described - or does it just look right? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Dockerfile + .dockerignore + commands + notes + scan command.
notes: The 'why this base image, why this layer order' notes are what make the Dockerfile maintainable.

title: Write a CI/CD pipeline
use_when: Need a GitHub Actions (or similar) pipeline that does the right things in the right order.
prompt:
## MISSION
Write a CI/CD pipeline for [REPO] in [PLATFORM e.g. GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, CircleCI]. Success is a pipeline that catches bugs before merge and deploys cleanly to [ENV].

## AGENT
You are a build engineer who knows that fast feedback wins. You order jobs by cost (lint -> typecheck -> unit -> integration -> deploy) and fail fast.

## SITUATION
Repo: [LANG/FRAMEWORK]
Test commands: [LINT, TYPECHECK, UNIT, INTEGRATION]
Deploy target: [ENV + DEPLOY_MECHANISM]
Secrets management: [WHERE_SECRETS_LIVE]
Branch strategy: [TRUNK / GITFLOW / etc.]

## TASK
1. The pipeline file (full YAML, runnable).
2. Job ordering rationale.
3. Caching strategy (deps, build artifacts).
4. Required secrets / variables and where to set them.
5. Rollback mechanism if deploy fails.

## THINK
Before producing the code or analysis: (a) identify the failure modes that would matter most in production for this stack, (b) identify what the simplest correct version looks like, (c) note where your output is pattern-matching from training vs. reasoning from the specific code in front of you. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Lint and typecheck must run before tests (they're faster and catch more).
- Integration tests must not run on every push to feature branches if they're slow - gate them.
- Deploy job must require all previous jobs to pass - no orphan deploys.
- Secrets must be referenced by name, never inlined.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I name specific file:line locations for every issue (not vague 'consider')? (2) Would this code pass the actual failure mode I described - or does it just look right? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Pipeline YAML + rationale + caching + secrets list + rollback note.
notes: The 'gate slow tests' rule is the practical lever - it's what keeps PR feedback under 5 min.

title: Generate API documentation from code
use_when: You have endpoints and want docs that match reality, not stale comments.
prompt:
Generate API docs for [SERVICE] in [FORMAT e.g. OpenAPI 3.1 YAML, Markdown].

Source of truth: [CODE_LOCATION e.g. FastAPI routes in app/api/]
Auth scheme: [AUTH_TYPE]
Audience: [INTERNAL / PARTNER / PUBLIC]

For each endpoint, include:
1. Method + path + summary.
2. Request: headers, path params, query params, body schema (with examples).
3. Responses: every status code the code can return, with body schema.
4. Auth requirement.
5. Rate limits or quotas if applicable.

Constraint: every response status must come from reading the code, not guessing. If the code can return 422 from validation, document 422 - not just 200 and 500.
notes: The 'every status from code' rule kills the lie that all endpoints return 200 or 500.

title: Write a README from scratch
use_when: New project needs a README that answers the questions a first-time reader actually has.
prompt:
Write a README for [PROJECT].

What it is: [ONE_SENTENCE]
Audience: [INTERNAL_TEAM / OSS / END_USERS]
Stack: [LANG + KEY_DEPS]
How to run it locally: [COMMANDS]
How to deploy: [DEPLOY_PROCESS]
Where help lives: [DOCS_URL, SLACK_CHANNEL, ISSUE_TRACKER]

Sections, in this order:
1. What and why (3 sentences max).
2. Quick start (paste-and-run, 5 lines or fewer).
3. Requirements (versions).
4. Configuration (env vars table).
5. Common tasks (test, build, deploy).
6. Architecture (one paragraph + link to deeper doc).
7. Troubleshooting (top 3 things that go wrong).
8. Contributing or contact.

Constraint: no marketing language. A reader should know in 30 seconds whether this repo is what they're looking for.
notes: The 'top 3 things that go wrong' section is the one new users will actually read.

title: Generate a CHANGELOG entry
use_when: You shipped a release and need a CHANGELOG entry users will actually understand.
prompt:
Write a CHANGELOG entry for version [VERSION] of [PROJECT], following Keep a Changelog format.

Inputs:
- Merged PRs / commits since [PREV_VERSION]: [LIST_OR_GIT_LOG]
- Audience: [INTERNAL_DEVS / END_USERS / API_CONSUMERS]

Group changes under: Added, Changed, Deprecated, Removed, Fixed, Security.

Constraints:
- Each line describes user-visible impact, not the internal mechanism. "Fixed crash on empty input to upload()" not "refactored upload() to handle null."
- Breaking changes get a BREAKING marker and a migration note.
- Skip internal refactors that have no user impact (don't pad).
notes: The 'user-visible impact' framing is what separates a changelog from a git log.

title: Architecture diagram description
use_when: You need a diagram to explain a system and want Mermaid (or similar) source you can edit.
prompt:
Generate a [Mermaid / PlantUML / Graphviz] diagram for [SYSTEM].

System description: [WHAT_IT_DOES]
Components: [LIST]
Data flow: [WHO_CALLS_WHOM]
External dependencies: [APIS, DBS, QUEUES]
Diagram type: [sequence / component / data-flow / deployment]

Deliver:
1. The diagram source code (runnable in [tool]).
2. A one-paragraph caption naming what the diagram is showing and what it deliberately leaves out.
3. One thing a reader might misread, and a note to prevent that.

Constraint: max 12 nodes. If the system has more, pick the right abstraction level and say what's collapsed.
notes: The 'what it deliberately leaves out' note is what separates a useful diagram from a wall of boxes.

title: Convert pseudocode to working code
use_when: You have an algorithm sketch and need real, runnable code in a specific language.
prompt:
Convert this pseudocode to runnable [LANGUAGE].

Pseudocode: [PASTE]
Intended use case: [CONTEXT]
Input format: [TYPES]
Output format: [TYPES]
Constraints: [PERFORMANCE / MEMORY / DEPS]

Deliver:
1. Working code with idiomatic patterns for [LANGUAGE].
2. A sample invocation with example input/output.
3. Any place I had to make a judgment call the pseudocode didn't specify (off-by-one, error handling, etc.) - list them.

Constraints:
- Don't add features the pseudocode didn't ask for. If logging or retries weren't specified, don't add them.
- Do NOT import third-party libraries unless the pseudocode named them or I listed them as available - prefer the standard library. If you do reference an external library, only call functions whose names and signatures you are certain exist; flag any call where you're guessing.
notes: The 'judgment calls' list is where the bugs hide - surfacing them is the value.

title: Generate edge-case tests
use_when: You have basic tests and want the tests that catch what production will throw at you.
prompt:
## MISSION
Generate edge-case tests for [FUNCTION] that go beyond happy path. Success is at least one test that would have caught a real bug in this code.

## AGENT
You are an adversarial tester. You think about what an attacker, a buggy upstream service, or a sleep-deprived intern would send. You enumerate boundaries, not just types.

## SITUATION
Function under test: [PASTE]
Test framework: [pytest / jest / etc.]
Existing tests: [SUMMARY]
Domain quirks: [DOMAIN_SPECIFICS e.g. timestamps cross DST, names contain emoji]

## TASK
Produce tests in these categories:
1. Boundary: 0, 1, max-int, max-int+1, negative, NaN, Infinity.
2. Empty / null / undefined / missing.
3. Encoding: emoji, RTL text, null bytes, BOM, very long strings.
4. Concurrency / ordering: if applicable.
5. Resource exhaustion: huge input, deeply nested input.
6. Adversarial: malformed but plausible input (e.g. JSON with duplicate keys).

## THINK
Before producing the code or analysis: (a) identify the failure modes that would matter most in production for this stack, (b) identify what the simplest correct version looks like, (c) note where your output is pattern-matching from training vs. reasoning from the specific code in front of you. Show this briefly, then deliver.

## RULES
- Each test must name the bug it catches in the test name.
- Skip categories that genuinely don't apply, but justify the skip in a comment.
- Do NOT duplicate the happy-path tests already in the file.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Did I name specific file:line locations for every issue (not vague 'consider')? (2) Would this code pass the actual failure mode I described - or does it just look right? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Runnable test file. Categories as section comments.
notes: The 'name the bug in the test name' rule turns the test suite into documentation.

title: Explain a confusing compiler/build error
use_when: You're stuck on an obscure error message and want to know what it actually means.
prompt:
Explain this error and propose a fix.

Error: [PASTE_ERROR]
Language/toolchain: [VERSION_INFO]
The code at the error site: [SNIPPET]
What I tried already: [ATTEMPTED_FIXES]

Deliver:
1. Plain-English translation of what the compiler/build is actually complaining about.
2. The most likely cause given my code.
3. The fix, with the corrected snippet.
4. Why my attempted fixes didn't work (if any did anything visible).
5. One thing this error is often confused with.

Constraint: don't say "this means X" without referencing the part of the error message that says it. Quote the relevant tokens.
notes: Forcing the explanation to quote the error message keeps it grounded.

title: Write a small CLI tool
use_when: You want a focused command-line utility that does one thing well.
prompt:
Write a CLI tool in [LANGUAGE] that [SINGLE_PURPOSE e.g. de-duplicates lines in a file while preserving order].

Inputs: [STDIN / FILE / ARGS]
Output: [STDOUT / FILE]
Flags needed: [LIST e.g. --case-insensitive, --keep-last]
Error behavior: [EXIT_CODES]

Deliver:
1. Full runnable code in one file.
2. Argument parsing using [argparse / clap / cobra / etc.] - no hand-rolled.
3. --help output that's actually useful (not just flag names - short examples).
4. Exit codes: 0 success, non-zero with documented meaning.
5. One example invocation pasted at the top as a comment.

Constraint: the tool reads stdin when no file argument is given and writes to stdout by default - it should compose in a pipeline.
notes: The pipe-composability constraint is what separates a Unix tool from a script.

title: Generate type hints / TypeScript types
use_when: You have untyped Python or JS and need types added without changing runtime behavior.
prompt:
Add [Python type hints / TypeScript types] to this code. Runtime behavior must not change.

Code: [PASTE]
Target type-check strictness: [strict / standard]
Existing callers: [HOW_IT'S_USED]

Deliver:
1. Annotated code.
2. Any place the existing code is type-ambiguous (overloaded return types, implicit any) - flag it.
3. Suggested types for any external boundary (function returning JSON, etc.) - name the schema.
4. One narrowing or guard I should add if [strict] flags survive.
