After drafting, check: (1) Does this output have at least one specific detail or move that another writer with the same prompt would not have produced? (2) Did I avoid the obvious version of this? If either is no, sharpen.

## OUTPUT
Deliver the final output only, in the structure named in TASK. No preamble. No restatement of the question. No "let me know if you need anything else" closer.
notes: Section 5 is where the interesting plots live. Mine it.

title: Political-economic system with real tradeoffs
use_when: Inventing a society and it keeps coming out as either utopia or dystopia - need something with friction.
prompt:
Design a political-economic system for [SOCIETY NAME / SETTING].

Specify:
- Who holds power, and what they had to do to get it
- How wealth moves (who produces, who extracts, who is excluded)
- The official story this society tells about itself
- The actual mechanism beneath the official story
- Three groups: who benefits, who survives, who is sacrificed
- One inherited contradiction the system cannot resolve without changing
- A recent event (within the story's last 20 years) that strained the system

Rules:
- No pure good guys / bad guys. The benefiting class includes sympathetic people.
- No "everyone is just evil and greedy" as the engine.
- The system must produce both stability and rot at once.
notes: The contradiction is usually your plot.

title: Story outline mapped onto a chosen structure
use_when: Have characters and a premise - need to know if there's actually a story here.
prompt:
## MISSION
Map a premise onto a known story structure so the user can see whether they have a story or a situation - and where the structural gaps are.

## AGENT
You are a story doctor who diagnoses where outlines die.

## SITUATION
My premise is [LOGLINE - protagonist, want, obstacle, stakes in one sentence]. My structure of choice is [3-ACT / SAVE THE CAT / HERO'S JOURNEY / KISHOTENKETSU].

## TASK
Build the outline using the structure I picked. For each beat, give me:
- The beat name
- What HAPPENS (concrete events, not summaries of feelings)
- What CHANGES in the protagonist (specific belief, choice, or capability)
- The minimum prerequisite earlier in the story that makes this beat earn itself

At the end, give me:
- THREE BEATS THAT FEEL FORCED and why
- ONE BEAT THE PREMISE WANTS BUT THE STRUCTURE DOESN'T HAVE A SLOT FOR

## THINK
Before producing the output: (a) name the obvious version that almost any writer would produce, (b) name what would make this output uniquely the user's instead of generic, (c) name the move you are most tempted to make that would be too safe. Use this to shape the output.

## RULES
- No "and then the protagonist learns to love themselves" beats. Concrete actions only.
- No beat that can be removed without breaking the next one.
- If the midpoint doesn't flip the protagonist's goal or understanding, flag it.
- The ending must be set up no later than the first 25% of the outline.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Does this output have at least one specific detail or move that another writer with the same prompt would not have produced? (2) Did I avoid the obvious version of this? If either is no, sharpen.

## OUTPUT
Deliver the final output only, in the structure named in TASK. No preamble. No restatement of the question. No "let me know if you need anything else" closer.
notes: The forced beats are where you'll need to either deepen character or change structure.

title: Scene revision - cut exposition, tighten beats
use_when: Have a scene that's flabby with backstory and over-explained motivation.
prompt:
Here is my scene:
---
[PASTE SCENE]
---

Revise it for punch. Specifically:
- Remove any sentence whose only job is to explain backstory the reader could infer
- Convert at least two interior monologue moments into action or dialogue
- Cut every adverb that describes a speech tag
- Find every sentence longer than 25 words and break or trim
- Identify exactly one detail to add that does work (image, gesture, object)

Output:
1. The revised scene
2. A bullet list of what you cut and why
3. The one detail you added and what it accomplishes
notes: Don't accept the revision wholesale - argue with the cuts. Some flab is voice.

title: Make two characters sound demonstrably different
use_when: My characters all sound like me - need real voice separation.
prompt:
I have two characters: [CHARACTER A: name, age, background in one line] and [CHARACTER B: name, age, background in one line].

For each character, give me:
- TYPICAL SENTENCE LENGTH (short clipped / long winding / mixed)
- VOCABULARY REGISTER (formal / vernacular / technical / regional)
- 3 WORDS OR PHRASES THEY USE that the other never would
- 2 WORDS OR PHRASES THEY AVOID (and why)
- HOW THEY HANDLE SILENCE (fill it / let it sit / cut it short)
- WHAT THEY DO WHEN ANGRY WITH LANGUAGE (escalate / withdraw / get precise / get vague)

Then write the same 4-line exchange twice: once where A speaks first, once where B speaks first. The exchanges should feel like completely different scenes.
notes: Tape the trait list above your draft. Reread dialogue against it.

title: Unblock a scene by surfacing the next beat
use_when: Stuck mid-scene and don't know what should happen next.
prompt:
Here is where I'm stuck:
---
[PASTE THE LAST 200-400 WORDS YOU WROTE]
---

The scene's purpose in the larger story is [WHAT THIS SCENE NEEDS TO ACCOMPLISH].

Don't continue writing the scene. Instead:
1. Diagnose why this is stuck (most likely: the scene already accomplished its purpose, OR a character is being too cooperative, OR you're avoiding a confrontation the scene wants)
2. Offer three different next beats:
   a) An escalation (someone pushes harder)
   b) An interruption (something external changes the stakes)
   c) A reveal (information shifts what we thought was happening)
3. For each, name what would have to be true earlier in the story for it to land
notes: If option (a) makes you uncomfortable, that's usually the right one.

title: Song lyrics in a specific genre and mood
use_when: Want lyrics for a specific kind of song, not generic 'song-shaped object.'
prompt:
Write lyrics for a song in the genre of [GENRE - be specific: alt-country, drill, shoegaze, math rock, etc.], in the mood of [MOOD: not happy/sad - try resigned, defiant, dissociated, vindicated, etc.].

Subject: [WHAT THE SONG IS ABOUT - concrete situation, not theme].

Structure: Verse 1 / Pre-chorus / Chorus / Verse 2 / Pre-chorus / Chorus / Bridge / Final chorus

Rules:
- The chorus must contain one image specific enough that nobody else could write it
- No rhyme of "fire/desire" or "heart/apart"
- The bridge must shift perspective, not just restate the chorus louder
- Verse 2 has to know something Verse 1 didn't
- One line in the song should be slightly grammatically wrong, in a way the speaker would actually talk

After the lyrics, name the image in the chorus and why it does the work.
notes: Sing it out loud. If you can't, the meter's off.

title: Children's book scene calibrated to a specific age group
use_when: Writing for kids and the tone is off - too babyish or too over their head.
prompt:
Write a scene from a children's book for age group [PICK: 2-4 picture book / 5-7 early reader / 8-10 middle grade / 11-13 upper middle grade].

Scene situation: [WHAT HAPPENS]
Main character: [NAME, AGE, ONE TRAIT]
The scene's emotional truth (what it's secretly about): [THEME - but never stated outright]

Calibrate to the age group:
- Sentence length and vocabulary appropriate (but never condescending)
- Emotional complexity present but accessible
- For picture book: leave room for the illustration to do half the work
- For middle grade: kids talk like kids that age actually talk, not adults' memory of kids
- No moralizing. No "and the lesson is..."

After the scene, in one line, name the emotional truth and how the scene carries it without saying it.
notes: Read it to an actual kid in that age range if you can. Their boredom is honest.

title: Personal essay opening that earns the reader's attention
use_when: Drafting a personal essay and the opening is all 'I have always been someone who...' - boring.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a personal essay opening that earns the reader's continued attention without leading on a thesis the rest of the essay won't deliver.

## AGENT
You are an essay editor at a magazine that gets 500 submissions a week.

## SITUATION
My essay is about [SUBJECT - the actual specific thing, not the theme]. The deeper question I'm circling is [QUESTION]. The personal stake is [WHY THIS SUBJECT WON'T LET ME GO].

## TASK
Write three different essay openings (150-200 words each). Each must:
- Begin with a specific scene, object, or moment - not a thesis
- Resist explaining itself for at least three sentences
- Imply the question without naming it
- Make the reader trust that the writer knows where they're going

For each opening, name in one line:
- The specific object / scene it leads with
- Why that's the right entry point for THIS essay

## THINK
Before producing the output: (a) name the obvious version that almost any writer would produce, (b) name what would make this output uniquely the user's instead of generic, (c) name the move you are most tempted to make that would be too safe. Use this to shape the output.

## RULES
- No "I have always..." sentences.
- No "Growing up, I..." sentences.
- No abstract opening sentences ("Memory is a strange thing").
- No throat-clearing about why you're writing this.
- No quoting a famous writer in the first paragraph.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Does this output have at least one specific detail or move that another writer with the same prompt would not have produced? (2) Did I avoid the obvious version of this? If either is no, sharpen.

## OUTPUT
Deliver the final output only, in the structure named in TASK. No preamble. No restatement of the question. No "let me know if you need anything else" closer.
notes: Pick the opening you're slightly embarrassed by - that's usually where the real essay is.

title: Letter from a fictional character to someone they can't say it to in person
use_when: Want to deepen a character by writing something they'd never say out loud.
prompt:
Write a letter from [CHARACTER - name, situation] to [RECIPIENT - who they are to the character], about [WHAT THE CHARACTER NEEDS TO SAY BUT WON'T IN PERSON].

Context: the letter will [NEVER BE SENT / BE SENT AND REGRETTED / BE FOUND POSTHUMOUSLY / BE THE THING THAT CHANGES EVERYTHING].

Constraints:
- The character's voice (rhythm, vocabulary, what they avoid) is consistent throughout
- The letter starts with something mundane and circles toward the real point
- At least one paragraph where the writer almost says it, then deflects
- At least one paragraph where they accidentally reveal more than they meant
- The ending lands on something specific, not a sweeping declaration

No "I just wanted to say." No "I know this is strange to write." No "by the time you read this."
notes: If the recipient is dead, the letter is for the writer. Let that be the engine.

title: Epistolary scene - story told through documents, not narration
use_when: Want to write a scene as artifacts (texts, emails, transcripts) instead of prose.
prompt:
Write a scene from a story told entirely through [PICK: text messages / emails / transcripts / Slack threads / voicemails / police reports / online reviews / app notifications]. The scene depicts [WHAT IS HAPPENING] and reveals [WHAT THE READER NEEDS TO REALIZE].

Constraints:
- Format must look real - timestamps, usernames, typos, autocorrect failures, sent/seen indicators
- The reader figures out what's happening from gaps, not exposition
- At least two voices, distinguishable from each other on format alone
- One artifact that contradicts the others (someone is lying, misremembering, or hiding)
- Ends on an artifact that recontextualizes the previous one
notes: The medium is the constraint. If a phone wouldn't show it that way, fix it.

title: Genre-mashup writing prompt generator
use_when: Stuck on what to write next - want a constrained mashup that forces something new.
prompt:
Generate five writing prompts that mash up [GENRE A] with [GENRE B] in non-obvious ways.

For each prompt, give me:
- A one-sentence premise
- The genre move from A it uses
- The genre move from B it uses
- The element that exists in neither genre on its own
- A word count target (flash / short story / novelette)

Rules:
- No "X but in space" or "X but with [supernatural creature]"
- The mashup must produce a problem neither genre alone could pose
- At least one prompt must invert the expected genre roles (the noir detective is the haunted one, the romance is the survival horror)
- Rank them from most to least promising and say why the top one is the top
notes: The top one is usually the one that scares you slightly.

title: Title brainstorm - 10 candidates ranked
use_when: Finished a piece and the working title is terrible.
prompt:
My piece is [GENRE / FORM - short story, essay, poem, etc.]. It's about [SUBJECT, 1 SENTENCE]. The emotional center is [THE FEELING OR QUESTION IT LEAVES THE READER WITH]. A key image or phrase from the piece is [IMAGE/PHRASE].

Give me 10 title candidates spanning these registers:
- 2 plain and concrete (one noun phrase)
- 2 borrowed from a line inside the piece
- 2 oblique / mysterious
- 2 that pose or answer a question
- 2 that lean into the piece's tone (funny, severe, tender, whatever it is)

Rank them 1-10, with one sentence per title on what it promises the reader and what it fails to deliver.

Then tell me which title #1 candidate I should NOT use, and why.
notes: The title you slightly hate at first is often the one. Sit with it overnight.

title: Weekly plan that survives one bad day without collapsing
use_when: Planning the week and need a plan that holds even when Tuesday gets eaten by something unexpected.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a weekly plan with built-in slack and explicit priority ordering, so when Tuesday gets eaten, Wednesday is not also lost.

## AGENT
You are a planning coach who has watched a thousand ambitious weekly plans die on Wednesday.

## SITUATION
My commitments this week are [LIST APPOINTMENTS, DEADLINES, NON-NEGOTIABLES with day/time]. My energy realistically peaks [WHEN - morning / late morning / etc.]. My known recurring drainers are [WHAT REGULARLY EATS TIME - meetings, kid pickup, commute, etc.]. The 2-3 things that MUST move forward this week are [LIST].

## TASK
Build a weekly plan that:
1. Identifies the WEEK'S ONE THING - if everything else goes wrong, this is what made the week count
2. Distributes the 2-3 must-move items into time slots that match my energy
3. Reserves Friday afternoon as buffer, not as more work
4. Names the THREE THINGS I AM EXPLICITLY NOT DOING THIS WEEK
5. Specifies the RECOVERY PROTOCOL if Tuesday or Wednesday goes sideways (what gets dropped, what gets moved, what stays)

## THINK
Before producing the plan: (a) identify the milestone most likely to fail and why, (b) identify the assumption that, if wrong, would invalidate the whole plan, (c) identify the work the user is most likely to underweight. Use this to shape the plan.

## RULES
- No plan that requires 5+ hours of deep work per day. That's a fantasy plan.
- Every block under 90 minutes or it splits.
- No back-to-back commitments without a 15-minute gap.
- If the plan has more than 35 hours of committed work for one person, force me to cut.
- Buffer time is not "free time to use." It's structural.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Does every milestone have a specific date and a named owner (not 'TBD' or 'us')? (2) Did I include the pre-mortem on the most fragile step? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Deliver the final output only, in the structure named in TASK. No preamble. No restatement of the question. No "let me know if you need anything else" closer.
notes: Re-read on Sunday night and Wednesday morning. Adjust Wednesday - don't pretend it's still Sunday's plan.

title: Daily plan with one anchor priority
use_when: Need a day plan that won't shatter the first time the day surprises me.
prompt:
Today's commitments: [LIST]. My energy right now: [HIGH / MIXED / LOW]. The week's one thing is [FROM WEEKLY PLAN].

Build today as:
- ONE ANCHOR PRIORITY - if I only do one work thing today, this is it. State it as a verb + concrete outcome (not "work on X").
- 2-3 SUPPORTING TASKS that move other things forward but don't compete with the anchor
- WHEN THE ANCHOR HAPPENS (specific time block, matched to my energy)
- WHAT GETS DROPPED FIRST if the day compresses
- ONE SMALL THING THAT'S NOT WORK that I'll actually do for me

No more than 6 items total. If a task is bigger than 90 minutes, name the first concrete subtask only.
notes: At end of day: did the anchor move? If yes, the day counted. If no, why not, and what changes tomorrow?

title: Two-week sprint plan for a small team
use_when: Running a sprint for a small team and want a plan that includes the unglamorous stuff people forget.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a two-week sprint plan that names the unglamorous work people forget (testing, deploys, comms), with capacity that reflects the team's actual availability.

## AGENT
You are an engineering manager who has run 100+ sprints and knows which ones quietly fail.

## SITUATION
Team size: [N people, roles]. Sprint length: [1 or 2 weeks]. Known capacity issues: [PTO, on-call, holidays, mid-sprint commitments]. Sprint goal candidate(s): [WHAT WE'RE TRYING TO ACHIEVE]. Carryover from last sprint: [WHAT'S NOT DONE].

## TASK
Produce a sprint plan with:
1. ONE SPRINT GOAL (single sentence, outcome-focused - not "ship X" but "users can do Y")
2. CAPACITY CALCULATION - actual person-days available after meetings, PTO, on-call, context switching tax (apply 25% overhead, not 0%)
3. COMMITTED WORK - items totaling no more than 70% of capacity, each with owner and acceptance criteria
4. STRETCH WORK - items only picked up if commit ships early
5. DEPENDENCY MAP - what blocks what, and who owns the unblock
6. SPRINT RISKS - top 3, with mitigation owner
7. WHAT WE ARE EXPLICITLY NOT DOING - items that will be requested mid-sprint that we will defer

## THINK
Before producing the plan: (a) identify the milestone most likely to fail and why, (b) identify the assumption that, if wrong, would invalidate the whole plan, (c) identify the work the user is most likely to underweight. Use this to shape the plan.

## RULES
- No sprint that commits more than 70% of nominal capacity.
- No story without a named owner.
- No "TBD" acceptance criteria.
- If a person is on-call, they get 50% capacity, not full.
- Carryover counts against this sprint's capacity, not as "free."

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Does every milestone have a specific date and a named owner (not 'TBD' or 'us')? (2) Did I include the pre-mortem on the most fragile step? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Deliver the final output only, in the structure named in TASK. No preamble. No restatement of the question. No "let me know if you need anything else" closer.
notes: Review the not-doing list with the team out loud. That's where alignment actually happens.

title: Quarterly OKR draft with measurable key results
use_when: Need to write OKRs that aren't just rebranded tasks.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a quarterly OKR draft where every key result is measurable, ambitious, and not a rebranded task - including one that would be a real stretch.

## AGENT
You are an OKR coach who has seen every flavor of bad OKR.

## SITUATION
My role / team: [DESCRIBE]. The quarter's strategic priorities from above: [WHAT LEADERSHIP CARES ABOUT]. What I/we own that matters: [SCOPE]. Last quarter's outcomes (what worked, what didn't): [NOTES].

## TASK
Draft 2-3 OBJECTIVES, each with 2-4 KEY RESULTS. For each:
- OBJECTIVE: a sentence describing a real change in state by quarter-end. Aspirational but bounded. Not a task list.
- KEY RESULTS: measurable, with a starting number, a target number, and a measurement method.
- HOW WE'D KNOW WE'RE OFF TRACK BY WEEK 6 (leading indicator)

Then audit your own draft:
- Which KR is actually just a project disguised as a metric?
- Which objective could be 100% achieved while the underlying problem gets worse?
- Which KR depends on something outside my control?

## THINK
Before producing the plan: (a) identify the milestone most likely to fail and why, (b) identify the assumption that, if wrong, would invalidate the whole plan, (c) identify the work the user is most likely to underweight. Use this to shape the plan.

## RULES
- No KR that uses "ensure," "improve," or "drive" without a number.
- No vanity metrics (followers, page views) unless tied to a downstream conversion.
- No more than 3 objectives. Pick.
- KRs must be measurable by someone who isn't me.
- "Launch X" is not a key result. "Have N users adopt X within 30 days of launch" might be.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Does every milestone have a specific date and a named owner (not 'TBD' or 'us')? (2) Did I include the pre-mortem on the most fragile step? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Deliver the final output only, in the structure named in TASK. No preamble. No restatement of the question. No "let me know if you need anything else" closer.
notes: Share the draft with one skeptic before committing. Their first objection is the real one.

title: Annual goals rooted in values, not aspirations
use_when: Setting goals for the year and want to avoid the usual 'read more / exercise more' list that dies by February.
prompt:
Before listing goals, answer these for me:

1. NAME 3-5 VALUES YOU ACTUALLY LIVE BY (not the ones you wish you did). Test: did you trade something for this value in the last 12 months?
2. For each value, name one area where your life is currently OUT OF ALIGNMENT with it (specific situation, not a feeling)
3. For each misalignment, propose ONE concrete change you could make this year - small enough that you'd actually do it, big enough that you'd notice if you didn't

Then give me 3-5 annual goals derived from #3, each in this form:
- THE CHANGE (specific, behavioral)
- THE VALUE IT SERVES
- HOW I'LL KNOW BY DECEMBER (observable, not "I'll feel better")
- THE FIRST SMALL ACTION (this week, under 2 hours)

Reject any goal that's just an aesthetic ("read 50 books") with no value attached.
notes: If you can't name what value a goal serves, it's someone else's goal.

title: Project plan with milestones and explicit decision points
use_when: Starting a multi-month project and want to know where the off-ramps and go/no-go moments are.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a multi-month project plan with explicit decision points and go/no-go criteria - so the project doesn't drift past the off-ramps unnoticed.

## AGENT
You are a project lead who knows that the question is never "will this go off track" but "where, and what will we do."

## SITUATION
Project: [DESCRIBE]. Goal / definition of done: [WHAT FINISHED LOOKS LIKE]. Timeline: [TOTAL DURATION]. Team / resources: [WHO, WHAT BUDGET]. Known unknowns: [WHAT MIGHT GO WRONG].

## TASK
Build a plan with:
1. PHASES (3-5, each with a name that describes the question the phase answers)
2. MILESTONES per phase - verifiable, dated, with owner
3. DECISION POINTS - explicit moments where we ask "continue / pivot / kill" based on stated criteria. At least one decision point per phase.
4. THE EARLIEST KILL CRITERION - what would tell us to stop before sinking more cost?
5. RISKS, ranked by likelihood × impact, with mitigation owner for the top 3
6. DEPENDENCIES on people outside the team - and how their slip changes our plan

## THINK
Before producing the plan: (a) identify the milestone most likely to fail and why, (b) identify the assumption that, if wrong, would invalidate the whole plan, (c) identify the work the user is most likely to underweight. Use this to shape the plan.

## RULES
- Every milestone is verifiable by someone who isn't the person doing the work.
- No decision point that says "we'll see how it's going." State actual criteria.
- The first decision point lands at 15-25% of timeline, not 80%.
- Buffer is 20% of the total, not zero, not 50%.
- If the plan has zero kill criteria, fail it back to me.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Does every milestone have a specific date and a named owner (not 'TBD' or 'us')? (2) Did I include the pre-mortem on the most fragile step? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Deliver the final output only, in the structure named in TASK. No preamble. No restatement of the question. No "let me know if you need anything else" closer.
notes: Make the kill criteria visible to the team. It's a gift to future-you when things are messy.

title: Pre-launch checklist for product, event, or content drop
use_when: About to launch something and want the unsexy list of things that always get missed.
prompt:
What I'm launching: [PRODUCT / EVENT / CONTENT - be specific]. Launch date: [DATE]. Audience: [WHO]. Channels: [WHERE]. The one thing I am most likely to forget historically: [WHAT].

Build a checklist with these sections:
- T-14 DAYS: foundations (technical, legal, content ready)
- T-7 DAYS: confirmations (stakeholders, partners, vendors notified)
- T-2 DAYS: dry run (test the actual experience end to end)
- LAUNCH DAY: who does what, in what order, with what fallback
- T+1 DAY: response plan (what we say if something breaks, who replies to what)
- T+7 DAYS: measurement and learning capture

For each section give me 4-8 specific items. Include the unglamorous stuff (DNS, accessibility checks, alt text, payment flow test with a real card, calendar invite for the launch call).

After the list, name the ONE THING in this checklist that, if skipped, will cause the worst day.
notes: Print it. Cross items off with a pen. Digital checkboxes don't have weight.

title: Post-launch retrospective that produces actual changes
use_when: Finished a launch and want a retro that doesn't just become 'great job everyone.'
prompt:
The launch: [WHAT, WHEN]. Outcome vs. expectation: [HOW IT WENT IN ONE SENTENCE]. People involved: [WHO SHOULD BE IN THE RETRO].

Design a 60-minute retro that:
- Opens with a 5-min individual silent write (so the loudest voice doesn't set the frame)
- Surfaces what worked, what didn't, what surprised us - with concrete examples, not vibes
- Forces every "we should X" item to have an owner, a date, and a definition of done
- Includes one round on "what almost went wrong but didn't" (near-misses, which we usually ignore)
- Ends with 3 commitments max - picked by the team, not by the loudest person

Give me:
- The exact agenda with timing
- 2-3 prompt questions per section
- The facilitator's script for the moments where people deflect or generalize ("can you give a specific example of when?")
- A template for the action items
notes: Send the action items the same day. Retros die in delay.

title: Travel itinerary with explicit tradeoffs between time, cost, and experience
use_when: Planning a trip and want to see the tradeoffs instead of optimizing one axis.
prompt:
Trip: [DESTINATION], [DURATION], [TRAVEL DATES]. Travelers: [WHO, AGES, MOBILITY/DIETARY NEEDS]. Budget range: [TOTAL OR PER DAY]. What this trip is for: [REST / EXPLORATION / SPECIFIC EVENT / TIME WITH SOMEONE].

Produce three itinerary versions:
- VERSION A: TIME-OPTIMIZED (see/do the most, accepting more spend and less rest)
- VERSION B: BUDGET-OPTIMIZED (lowest cost path, accepting fewer highlights or less convenient logistics)
- VERSION C: EXPERIENCE-OPTIMIZED (depth over breadth - fewer things, more presence)

For each version include:
- Daily structure (rough - not hour-by-hour)
- Estimated total cost with line items (flights, lodging, food, activities, ground transport)
- The 3 things you'd cut first if budget shrunk 20%
- The 1 thing that's worth paying extra for in this version
- A red flag - what about this version will most likely disappoint someone in the group

Then recommend a version and say why, given the trip's purpose.
notes: Show the group all three. Their reactions tell you what they actually want.

title: Weekly meal plan with dietary constraints and real-life sloppiness
use_when: Want a meal plan that survives a Wednesday where I don't want to cook.
prompt:
Constraints: [DIETARY - vegetarian / GF / allergies / kosher / etc.]. Household: [WHO EATS, AGES]. Skill level: [BEGINNER / OK / CONFIDENT]. Time budget for cooking on weeknights: [MAX MINUTES]. The cuisines I actually like: [LIST]. The vegetables I will eat: [LIST]. The vegetables I won't: [LIST].

Build a 7-day plan with:
- Monday-Thursday: dinners under [TIME BUDGET], at least 2 of them ideally batched on Sunday
- Friday: a "low-effort" night (leftovers, takeout, breakfast-for-dinner)
- Saturday: one slightly ambitious meal if I want
- Sunday: a batch-cook plan that produces 2 weekday meals' worth of leftovers
- LUNCHES: cover them or explicitly say "leftovers" - don't pretend they cook themselves
- A SHOPPING LIST grouped by store section
- A SUBSTITUTION COLUMN: for each meal, what to swap in if the main ingredient looks bad at the store

Include one "permission slip" night where the meal is literally cereal or eggs on toast and that's the plan.
notes: The permission slip is the load-bearing part. Don't cut it.

title: Home renovation phased over months/years with budget breathing room
use_when: Have a list of changes I want in the house but can't do them all at once.
prompt:
House: [TYPE, AGE, ROUGH SIZE]. Owner / renter: [OWNED]. Annual budget for home: [AMOUNT]. List of wants: [DUMP THEM - paint kitchen, replace floors, redo bathroom, fix yard, etc.]. Constraints: [TIMELINE PRESSURES - selling in 3 years / kid coming / etc.]. DIY appetite: [NONE / SOME / LOTS].

Phase the work across [12-36] months in 3-month blocks. For each phase:
- THE PROJECT(S) - what gets done
- WHY THIS NOW - what makes this phase's order correct (dependencies, seasonality, life events)
- ESTIMATED COST RANGE - low/mid/high with what drives the variance
- DIY vs HIRE call - and the reasoning
- "WAIT" CRITERIA - when to pause and re-plan

Then list:
- Three projects from my list that should be deferred or killed (with reasoning)
- The one project people always regret doing without thinking it through, given my situation
notes: Get one quote before you commit to any phase. Estimates here are starting points, not budgets.

title: Job search 30/60/90 with weekly anchor activities
use_when: Starting a job search and want structure, not just 'apply to things and hope.'
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a 30/60/90-day job-search plan with weekly anchor activities - so the user doesn't conflate 'applying to things' with 'making progress.'

## AGENT
You are a job search coach who has guided people out of decade-long careers.

## SITUATION
My current situation: [EMPLOYED LOOKING / UNEMPLOYED / TRANSITIONING FIELDS]. Target role(s): [BE SPECIFIC]. Target industries: [LIST]. Geography: [REMOTE / CITY/COUNTRY]. Realistic search runway: [WEEKS/MONTHS I CAN SUSTAIN]. Strongest assets: [LIST]. Biggest perceived gap: [WHAT YOU THINK].

## TASK
Build a 30/60/90 plan:
- DAYS 1-30: positioning, materials, network reactivation. Specific outputs: refreshed resume, 3 versions of pitch, LinkedIn updated, list of 50 target people / 20 target companies.
- DAYS 31-60: active outreach and pipeline. Weekly targets: N applications, N warm intros requested, N informational calls.
- DAYS 61-90: convert and decide. How to handle multiple offers / no offers / pivot signals.

For each phase specify:
- WEEKLY ANCHOR ACTIVITIES (the 3 things every week, regardless of mood)
- ENERGY MANAGEMENT (job searching is grief work - protect mornings)
- A METRIC THAT MATTERS (not "applications sent" - try "qualified conversations had")
- A KILL/PIVOT CRITERION at end of each 30-day block

## THINK
Before producing the plan: (a) identify the milestone most likely to fail and why, (b) identify the assumption that, if wrong, would invalidate the whole plan, (c) identify the work the user is most likely to underweight. Use this to shape the plan.

## RULES
- No "send 100 applications a week." Volume without targeting is theater.
- Networking activities must be specific people, not "do networking."
- The plan includes a financial runway check at day 60.
- One day per week is off. Burnout-search is worse than no-search.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Does every milestone have a specific date and a named owner (not 'TBD' or 'us')? (2) Did I include the pre-mortem on the most fragile step? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Deliver the final output only, in the structure named in TASK. No preamble. No restatement of the question. No "let me know if you need anything else" closer.
notes: The grief work is real. A search lasting 3+ months changes how you see yourself. Talk to people.

title: Career inflection - three viable next options compared
use_when: At a career crossroads and want to compare paths without falling for whichever sounds most exciting today.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a side-by-side comparison of three viable career options that surfaces the tradeoffs the user is most likely to underweight - so the choice isn't determined by which path sounds most exciting today.

## AGENT
You are a career strategist who treats career moves as portfolio decisions, not narratives.

## SITUATION
Current role: [TITLE, INDUSTRY, TENURE]. What's prompting the inflection: [PUSH FACTORS - burnout, ceiling, bad fit / PULL FACTORS - opportunity, learning, comp]. My non-negotiables: [LIST - geography, income floor, hours, identity stuff]. What I'd regret in 5 years if I didn't try: [WRITE IT].

## TASK
Lay out three distinct next-move options. For each:
1. THE MOVE (specific role, level, type of org)
2. WHAT IT BUILDS (skills, network, optionality, brand)
3. WHAT IT COSTS (compared to staying - income, stability, identity friction, family)
4. THE 24-MONTH OUTCOME if it goes well
5. THE 24-MONTH OUTCOME if it goes poorly (don't sugarcoat)
6. WHAT THIS OPTION CLOSES OFF (the doors it shuts)
7. THE FIRST STEP THAT WOULD TEST IT for 4 weeks before fully committing

End with:
- Which option I should fear most, and why
- Which option I'm already dismissing prematurely
- A question I haven't asked myself that I should

## THINK
Before producing the plan: (a) identify the milestone most likely to fail and why, (b) identify the assumption that, if wrong, would invalidate the whole plan, (c) identify the work the user is most likely to underweight. Use this to shape the plan.

## RULES
- No option that's just "stay and figure it out." That's a fourth, separate option if you want it on the list.
- Don't rank for me. Give me the tradeoffs.
- "Follow your passion" is banned as reasoning.
- Each option must be testable cheaply before full commitment.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Does every milestone have a specific date and a named owner (not 'TBD' or 'us')? (2) Did I include the pre-mortem on the most fragile step? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Deliver the final output only, in the structure named in TASK. No preamble. No restatement of the question. No "let me know if you need anything else" closer.
notes: Reread the 'fear most' answer in a week. If it still rings true, that's the one to look at hardest.

title: Monthly budget revision that accounts for the real you
use_when: Budget on paper keeps failing in practice - need one that includes the leaks I actually have.
prompt:
Current monthly income (after tax): [AMOUNT]. Fixed costs: [RENT/MORTGAGE, UTILITIES, INSURANCE, SUBSCRIPTIONS, DEBT PAYMENTS]. Variable necessities: [GROCERIES, TRANSPORT, AVERAGE]. Savings / investment goals this month: [AMOUNT, FOR WHAT]. Honest leaks (where money disappears): [DELIVERY, COFFEE, IMPULSE - be honest].

Build a budget that:
- Names the leaks and gives them a CAPPED amount instead of pretending they're zero
- Includes a "irregular but inevitable" line (annual subscriptions, car repairs, gifts) averaged monthly
- Has a buffer line of 5-10% for actual surprises
- Identifies the ONE category most worth defending and the ONE category most worth cutting this month
- Includes a "joy budget" - explicit money for things that aren't necessities, so they don't sabotage the rest

Then audit: where is this budget pretending I'm someone I'm not? Flag any line you don't believe.
notes: Track for two weeks before tightening. You can't budget against a fantasy of yourself.

title: Three-month reading plan on a topic
use_when: Want to actually learn a topic over 90 days through reading, not just collect books.
prompt:
Topic I want to understand: [TOPIC]. Why this topic, now: [REASON]. My current knowledge level: [BEGINNER / SOME EXPOSURE / INTERMEDIATE]. Reading time available per week: [REALISTIC HOURS]. Format preferences: [BOOKS / LONG-FORM ARTICLES / PAPERS / AUDIO].

Build a 3-month plan:
- MONTH 1: foundations. 1-2 sources that establish the lay of the land. Concrete: which book/articles, in what order.
- MONTH 2: depth in one sub-area. Pick one branch - go deep.
- MONTH 3: friction. Read something that disagrees with what month 1 established, OR a primary source that complicates the narrative.

For each source:
- Why this one (not a different one on the same topic)
- Time estimate
- What I should know how to articulate after finishing it
- One question to hold while reading

Include a once-a-week 30-minute synthesis block: write down what I now believe and where I'm uncertain.
notes: Skip the synthesis block and you've collected books, not learned. Don't skip it.

title: Deliberate practice schedule to acquire a skill
use_when: Trying to actually get good at something and want a plan that uses deliberate practice, not vibes.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a deliberate-practice schedule that includes the painful parts (working at the edge, isolating weaknesses, feedback loops) - not just a list of activities that resemble the skill.

## AGENT
You are a skill acquisition coach trained in deliberate practice principles.

## SITUATION
The skill: [SPECIFIC - not "be better at design," but "be able to design and ship a usable web form in 2 hours"]. Current level: [WHAT YOU CAN DO TODAY]. Target level at 12 weeks: [WHAT YOU WANT TO BE ABLE TO DO]. Weekly time available: [HOURS]. Access to feedback: [TEACHER / PEER / SELF-REVIEW ONLY].

## TASK
Build a 12-week plan structured around deliberate practice:
1. SUB-SKILLS - break the skill into 4-6 components. Each component is something you can practice in isolation.
2. WEEKLY STRUCTURE - for each week, name which sub-skill is in focus, what specific drill targets it, and what "good" looks like for the drill.
3. FEEDBACK LOOP - how you'll know if practice is producing improvement. If feedback isn't available externally, what self-review method (recording, output comparison, etc.)
4. INTEGRATION - week 8 and week 12 include integrated projects that combine sub-skills.
5. PLATEAU PROTOCOL - what to do when progress stalls (it will, around week 5-7).
6. THE THING YOU'LL BE TEMPTED TO SKIP - name it now (usually the boring drill that targets the actual weakness).

## THINK
Before producing the plan: (a) identify the milestone most likely to fail and why, (b) identify the assumption that, if wrong, would invalidate the whole plan, (c) identify the work the user is most likely to underweight. Use this to shape the plan.

## RULES
- No "spend 30 minutes a day on the skill." Practice must specify WHAT.
- Practice that doesn't strain you = play, not practice. Each week must include something that's just outside current ability.
- No drill without an output you could show someone.
- If feedback is self-only, build in monthly external review (recorded session, peer look, paid coach hour).

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Does every milestone have a specific date and a named owner (not 'TBD' or 'us')? (2) Did I include the pre-mortem on the most fragile step? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Deliver the final output only, in the structure named in TASK. No preamble. No restatement of the question. No "let me know if you need anything else" closer.
notes: The plateau protocol is the difference between people who get good and people who plateau permanently.

title: Savings timeline for a specific goal
use_when: Have a financial goal and want a realistic timeline with monthly targets.
prompt:
Goal: [WHAT - house down payment, sabbatical, surgery, car, wedding, etc.]. Target amount: [$]. Current saved: [$]. Hard deadline (if any): [DATE OR "FLEXIBLE"]. Monthly savings capacity (after necessities): [$]. Other claims on that capacity: [DEBT PAYDOWN, RETIREMENT, EMERGENCY FUND STATUS].

Build a plan:
- Minimum months to reach goal at current capacity
- Three scenarios: conservative (current capacity), stretched (current + cuts), aggressive (current + cuts + income side)
- For each scenario, the specific cuts or income additions that would make it real (not "spend less")
- Where to PARK the savings while accumulating (high-yield savings / CD / brokerage), with a one-line rationale
- A check-in cadence (monthly or quarterly) and what to do if you fall behind
- A "good problem" plan: what to do if you reach the target faster than expected

Reality-check: is this goal worth pausing a higher-priority financial move (emergency fund, employer 401k match)? Flag if yes.
notes: If you don't have 3 months of expenses saved, that comes first, even before this goal. Be honest.

title: Relocation logistics plan across cities or countries
use_when: Moving and want a plan that catches the boring bureaucratic stuff that ruins moves.
prompt:
Move type: [LOCAL / INTERSTATE / INTERNATIONAL]. From: [LOCATION]. To: [LOCATION]. Move date: [DATE]. Household: [WHO, KIDS, PETS]. Possessions: [SCALE - studio's worth / 2BR / full house]. Budget for the move itself: [$].

Build the plan in time-buckets:
- 12+ WEEKS OUT: research housing, visa/immigration if applicable, school enrollment timelines, license/registration requirements at destination
- 8 WEEKS OUT: book movers / rental truck, give notice to landlord/employer if needed, transfer or close memberships
- 4 WEEKS OUT: address change list (banks, doctors, IRS/equivalent, voter registration, subscriptions, employer HR, pharmacy, vet), set up utilities at destination, transfer prescriptions
- 2 WEEKS OUT: declutter and decide what's actually worth moving, separate immediate-arrival box, confirm move-day logistics
- MOVE WEEK: cleaning, final walkthrough, mail forwarding, immediate-needs box
- WEEK 1 AT DESTINATION: register essentials (license, voting, doctors), find grocery / pharmacy / gas station within walking distance

For each bucket, give me the items I will most likely forget about my specific kind of move. Pets and kids generate their own sub-lists.
notes: Take photos of every electronics setup before unplugging. You will not remember which cable goes where.

title: Wedding or large-event master planning list with phases
use_when: Planning a wedding or large event and want a master plan, not 47 tabs in a spreadsheet.
prompt:
Event: [WEDDING / MILESTONE BIRTHDAY / FUNDRAISER / ETC.]. Date: [DATE]. Guest count target: [N]. Budget: [$]. Style and feel I want: [DESCRIBE IN ONE LINE]. Non-negotiables: [LIST - venue type, specific people, dietary needs, accessibility, kid-friendly, etc.]. Stress points / what I'm worried about: [HONEST LIST].

Build a phased plan:
- PHASE 1 (12-9 months out): venue, date confirmed, guest list draft, big-budget lines locked (venue, food, photographer)
- PHASE 2 (9-6 months out): invitations, vendors locked, attire, accommodations for travelers
- PHASE 3 (6-3 months out): logistics (transportation, timeline, vendor coordination), RSVPs tracked, contingencies
- PHASE 4 (3-1 months out): final headcount, seating, day-of timeline, vendor reconfirmations
- PHASE 5 (final 30 days): pickups, deliveries, day-of point person briefing, self-care plan for week-of
- WEEK OF and DAY OF: minute-by-minute timeline for key transitions

Also give me:
- THE 3 LINE ITEMS PEOPLE OVERSPEND ON for this event type
- THE 3 THINGS PEOPLE UNDERSPEND ON AND REGRET (often: photo, comfort/shoes, day-of coordinator)
- ONE THING I SHOULD DELEGATE NOW, because doing it myself will burn me out by month 4
notes: Designate a day-of point person who is not a guest. This is the single best money you'll spend.

title: New-parent first-month plan focused on survival, not optimization
use_when: Having a baby soon and want a plan that protects sleep, sanity, and the relationship - not a Pinterest checklist.
prompt:
Situation: [FIRST KID / NOT FIRST]. Household: [PARTNERS / SOLO / EXTENDED FAMILY NEARBY]. Support available: [WHO CAN ACTUALLY HELP, WHAT THEY CAN DO]. Parental leave: [HOW MUCH, FOR WHOM]. Known constraints: [HEALTH FACTORS, OTHER KIDS, WORK PRESSURES].

Build a first-month plan organized around:
- SLEEP DEFENSE: how to structure nights so at least one adult gets a 4-hour block. Specific shift options based on feeding method.
- MEALS: meal-train coordination OR freezer-prep list OR delivery rotation. Specific plan, not "we'll figure it out."
- VISITORS: opening window, closing window, what to say to people who want to come and stay. The script.
- WORK BOUNDARIES (if non-leave partner exists): how to defend evenings and weekends from work creep specifically in this month.
- RELATIONSHIP MAINTENANCE: one 20-minute checkin per week between adult partners. Topics list. (Not "how are we doing." Try: what surprised you, what's hardest, what's one thing the other did this week I appreciated.)
- THE ONE THING TO DROP: identify a thing you usually do that gets dropped for 30 days without guilt (cooking, gym, social events, project work).
- HEALTH CHECK-INS: postpartum check-ins (mental and physical) for the birthing parent. Specific dates and who's tracking.

Mark any item that needs to be set up BEFORE the baby arrives, because you won't have the capacity after.
notes: The relationship checkin matters most. Don't skip it because you're tired. That's when you skip it.

title: Caregiving plan for an aging parent or family member
use_when: Stepping into a caregiving role and want a plan that doesn't leave it all on one person.
prompt:
The person being cared for: [RELATIONSHIP, AGE, GENERAL HEALTH STATUS]. Living situation: [INDEPENDENT / ASSISTED / WITH FAMILY]. Their wishes (known): [WHAT THEY'VE SAID ABOUT CARE PREFERENCES]. Family members involved: [WHO, HOW MUCH CAPACITY EACH HAS]. Geographic distance: [WHO IS CLOSE]. Financial picture: [STABLE / TIGHT / UNKNOWN]. Legal documents in place: [POA, ADVANCE DIRECTIVE, WILL - YES / NO / UNCLEAR].

Build a plan covering:
- IMMEDIATE LEGAL/ADMIN GAPS to close in next 60 days (POA, HIPAA releases, advance directive, beneficiary review)
- CARE TASK INVENTORY: medical appointments, medication management, daily activities, social/emotional check-ins, home maintenance, finances. For each, who does it now, who could share it.
- RESPONSIBILITY ALLOCATION: a draft of who does what, with one named primary and one named backup per area. The conversation script for proposing this to family.
- RESPITE PLAN: how the primary caregiver gets time off. Specific dates, not "as needed."
- ESCALATION TRIGGERS: what observable changes mean we revisit (falls, missed meds, weight loss, confusion increasing)
- COMMUNICATION RHYTHM: how the family stays aligned. Weekly text update? Monthly call? Shared document?
- MONEY TALK: who is paying for what now, and what's the threshold where it becomes a family conversation

Flag: the assumptions in this plan that need to be verified by an elder law attorney or a social worker in this jurisdiction.
notes: The respite plan is not optional. Caregiver collapse hurts everyone, including the person being cared for.

title: Leaving a job - transition plan over 2-6 weeks
use_when: Resigning and want to leave well - for my reputation, my team, and my own integrity.
prompt:
My role: [TITLE, TENURE]. Notice period: [WEEKS]. Reason for leaving (for my own clarity, not necessarily disclosed): [HONEST]. Manager relationship: [GOOD / NEUTRAL / STRAINED]. What I own that's not documented anywhere: [LIST].

Build a transition plan:
- RESIGNATION CONVERSATION: prep - when to do it, what to say, how to respond to counteroffer pressure or guilt, what to put in writing afterward
- WEEK 1 of notice: who I tell in what order (manager first, then team, then peers, then external), documentation kickoff
- WEEKS MIDDLE: knowledge transfer - list of artifacts to create (runbooks, contact lists, project status, passwords transferred properly via IT)
- FINAL WEEK: handoff completion, last 1:1s with key relationships, exit interview if applicable
- RELATIONSHIP MAINTENANCE: 5-10 people I want to stay connected with - what specific note do I send each before I leave?
- WHAT NOT TO DO: don't badmouth, don't take what isn't yours (files, IP), don't promise to stay in touch with everyone, don't bring up old grievances in the exit interview

Also give me:
- The script for the resignation conversation, including the boundary if counteroffered
- What to do if my manager reacts badly
notes: You will work in this industry again. The people in this room will be at your future companies. Act accordingly.

title: Side project from idea to launched in 30 days
use_when: Have a side-project idea I keep tweaking - need to ship something in a month, not perfect it.
prompt:
The idea: [ONE LINE]. The minimum viable version: [WHAT'S THE SMALLEST USEFUL THING]. Available time per week: [HONEST HOURS - not aspirational]. Skills I have: [LIST]. Skills I'd need to fake / buy / borrow: [LIST]. What "launched" means to me for this: [LIVE URL / 10 USERS / 1 PAYING USER / PUBLIC POST].

Plan it in 4 weeks:
- WEEK 1: scope ruthlessly. What's in the MVP. What's explicitly cut. The ugliest acceptable version of the experience.
- WEEK 2: build the core. The thing the user must be able to do.
- WEEK 3: build only what's needed for someone else to actually use it (auth or signup if needed, basic styling, error handling, deployment)
- WEEK 4: ship it to 5 real people who match the audience. Iterate based on what they actually do, not what they say.

For each week, give me:
- THE ONE DELIVERABLE that means the week succeeded
- THE THREE THINGS I'LL BE TEMPTED TO DO INSTEAD that I must defer
- WHAT TO CUT FIRST if the week compresses

End with: the version of this project after week 4 will look embarrassing. That's the goal. If it looks polished, you took too long.
notes: The deferred features list is a gift. Save it. You'll either build them in v2 or realize they didn't matter.

title: Replace one habit with another over 8 weeks
use_when: Want to swap a habit I want less of for one I want more of - not just willpower-suppress the old one.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce an 8-week plan that replaces one habit with a substitute that fills the same need - so the user isn't just willpower-suppressing the old one.

## AGENT
You are a behavior change coach who treats habits as systems, not character flaws.

## SITUATION
HABIT TO REPLACE: [SPECIFIC - "scroll Instagram in bed at night," not "phone use"]. CURRENT FREQUENCY / TRIGGER: [WHEN AND WHY IT HAPPENS]. WHAT IT GIVES ME: [the real payoff - relaxation, escape, dopamine, avoidance - be honest]. NEW HABIT: [WHAT I WANT TO DO INSTEAD]. WHY THE NEW HABIT: [WHAT IT'S MEANT TO SERVE].

## TASK
Build an 8-week replacement plan with:
1. TRIGGER MAPPING - the cue that fires the old habit (time, location, emotional state, preceding action). The new habit must be tied to the same cue, not a different one.
2. PAYOFF MATCH - the new habit must deliver something that addresses the same need. Be specific: if the old habit gave numbing, the new one needs to either provide numbing differently or address why I needed numbing.
3. WEEK-BY-WEEK PROGRESSION:
   - Weeks 1-2: insert the new habit on the cue, accept that the old habit may still happen sometimes
   - Weeks 3-4: friction the old habit (environment design - phone out of bedroom, app blockers, physical barriers)
   - Weeks 5-6: reinforcement - track what's working, identify the times of week when the old habit still wins
   - Weeks 7-8: stabilize - handle the situations where the old habit historically came back (travel, stress, weekends)
4. SLIP PROTOCOL - when (not if) I do the old habit, what's the recovery? (Specific, non-shaming.)
5. SIGNAL OF SUCCESS - what observable thing tells me the swap is sticking by week 8?

## THINK
Before producing the plan: (a) identify the milestone most likely to fail and why, (b) identify the assumption that, if wrong, would invalidate the whole plan, (c) identify the work the user is most likely to underweight. Use this to shape the plan.

## RULES
- No "just use willpower."
- No replacement that requires conditions to be perfect (a quiet house, free hands, the right mood).
- The new habit's first version is embarrassingly small. Make it smaller than I want.
- Tracking is binary (did it / didn't), not a streak. Streaks break and kill the habit.
- No self-punishment in the slip protocol. Shame creates more of the old behavior, not less.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Does every milestone have a specific date and a named owner (not 'TBD' or 'us')? (2) Did I include the pre-mortem on the most fragile step? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Deliver the final output only, in the structure named in TASK. No preamble. No restatement of the question. No "let me know if you need anything else" closer.
notes: Week 5-7 is the danger zone. The novelty's gone and the old habit's still nearby. Plan for it specifically.

title: Quick reframe: turn anxious rumination into one concrete next action
use_when: Looping on something unproductive and need to break the cycle into something doable.
prompt:
I am ruminating on [SITUATION]. The story I'm telling myself is [STORY].

Do three things:
1. Name the assumption in my story that's least likely to be true
2. Reframe the situation in one neutral sentence
3. Give me one concrete action I can take in the next 60 minutes

Be direct. No reassurance. No "it's understandable that you feel..."
notes: Works best when used immediately rather than after hours of looping.

title: End-of-day reflection in three questions, under five minutes
use_when: Want a daily wind-down that actually surfaces something, not a journal that becomes a chore.
prompt:
Ask me these three questions, one at a time, waiting for my answer before the next:

1. What's one specific thing from today I want to remember a week from now - and why?
2. What's one moment today I handled differently than I would have a year ago?
3. What's one thing I'm carrying into tomorrow that doesn't need to come with me?

After my three answers, give me ONE sentence that connects them - not a summary, but the pattern you see.

No "great job today." No emojis. No "you should be proud."
notes: If you skip night three in a row, the prompt isn't broken - you're avoiding question 3.

title: End-of-week reflection: what worked, what to change
use_when: Closing out the week and want a fast review that produces one change for next week.
prompt:
Ask me:
1. What were the 2-3 actual outcomes of this week - what moved, what got finished, what got decided?
2. What did I do this week that I want to do more of? Be specific (a behavior, not "be more productive").
3. What did I do this week that I want to do less of? Be specific.
4. What did I avoid this week that I'll keep avoiding next week if I don't decide otherwise?

After my answers, propose:
- ONE thing I'll deliberately do differently next week (verb + concrete trigger)
- ONE thing I'll stop pretending I'm going to do this month

Don't comfort me about #4. Push back if my "do differently" is vague.
notes: Do this Friday afternoon, not Sunday night. Sunday-night-you is too tired to be honest.

title: Monthly review using metrics that actually matter to me
use_when: End of the month and want a review that isn't just vibes or vanity numbers.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a monthly review that treats personal metrics as data rather than catharsis - surfacing one pattern and one behavioral intent for next month.

## AGENT
You are a reflective practice coach who treats monthly reviews as data, not catharsis.

## SITUATION
My personal metrics that actually matter to me right now are [LIST 3-5 - could be: hours of deep work, nights of decent sleep, weekly calls with a specific person, money saved, runs, days without [X], pages written]. The month's known events: [BIG THINGS THAT HAPPENED]. My intent at start of month was [WHAT I SAID I'D FOCUS ON].

## TASK
Walk me through the review in this order, asking one question at a time and waiting:

1. METRIC CHECK - for each metric, ask me what the number was and what it would have been if a "good" version of the month had unfolded. Note the gap.
2. INTENT CHECK - what did I say I'd focus on, and where did my actual attention go instead?
3. PATTERN CHECK - what kept happening that I noticed but didn't act on?
4. SURPRISE CHECK - what surprised me about myself this month?
5. ENERGY AUDIT - when did I feel most alive this month? Most depleted?

After all answers, give me:
- The ONE PATTERN that shows up in 3+ answers
- The ONE INTENT for next month that addresses that pattern (must be behavioral, not aspirational)
- What I should STOP tracking, if a metric is generating effort without insight

## THINK
Before producing the response: (a) check whether the user is asking for reassurance vs. real diagnosis, (b) identify the assumption they are most likely defending without realizing it, (c) name the response that would feel good but not be useful. Choose the useful one.

## RULES
- No reframing my answers into something positive. If a month was bad, it was bad.
- No suggestion that involves "just be kinder to yourself." Vague.
- Force me to be specific when I generalize ("I felt off" → "off how, in what situations, with whom").
- Don't let me list more than ONE intent for next month. Pick.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Is the answer something the user could not have written themselves on a good day, or am I echoing their setup back to them? (2) Did I push back where pushing back would help, or did I default to validation? If either, revise.

## OUTPUT
Deliver the final output only, in the structure named in TASK. No preamble. No restatement of the question. No "let me know if you need anything else" closer.
notes: If a metric hasn't changed your behavior in 3 months, retire it.

title: Annual review focused on what changed about me, not what I did
use_when: End of year and tired of the 'what I accomplished' lists - want to look at how I changed.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce an annual review focused on how the user changed (skills, beliefs, capacities, relationships) - not on a list of accomplishments.

## AGENT
You are a reflection guide who treats years as transformation, not productivity audits.

## SITUATION
The year that just ended: [YEAR]. Briefly, the biggest external things that happened (jobs, moves, relationships, losses, gains): [LIST].

## TASK
Walk me through these questions, one at a time, in order. Wait for my answer before continuing:

1. What did I believe in January that I don't believe in December?
2. What's something I used to do automatically that now requires effort, or vice versa?
3. Who became closer to me this year, and who became further away? What did I do (or fail to do) in each case?
4. What did I get wrong about myself this year?
5. What did I do that surprised me - that contradicted who I thought I was?
6. Where did I show up in a way I respect?
7. Where did I betray myself or someone else?
8. What changed about what I want?

After my answers, write me:
- A two-sentence portrait of who I was in January
- A two-sentence portrait of who I am now
- The single most important difference

## THINK
Before producing the response: (a) check whether the user is asking for reassurance vs. real diagnosis, (b) identify the assumption they are most likely defending without realizing it, (c) name the response that would feel good but not be useful. Choose the useful one.

## RULES
- Don't reassure me on #7. Sit with it.
- Don't moralize on #5.
- Don't summarize my year as a story arc. It probably wasn't one.
- No "you grew so much this year" closing. The portrait is the closing.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Is the answer something the user could not have written themselves on a good day, or am I echoing their setup back to them? (2) Did I push back where pushing back would help, or did I default to validation? If either, revise.

## OUTPUT
Deliver the final output only, in the structure named in TASK. No preamble. No restatement of the question. No "let me know if you need anything else" closer.
notes: Save the answer to #1 somewhere. Reread it next December.

title: Decision journal entry for a choice I just made
use_when: Just made a real decision and want to log it before hindsight rewrites my reasoning.
prompt:
I made the following decision: [DECISION]. The decision date is [TODAY]. The outcome will be visible by approximately [WHEN].

Help me capture this for future review. Ask me one question at a time:

1. What's the decision in one sentence?
2. What were the alternatives I considered?
3. What did I expect would happen if I picked this option? (Be specific about timeframe and indicators.)
4. What was the reasoning - both the rational case and what was driving me emotionally?
5. What was my confidence level (1-10) and what would change my confidence by 2 points in either direction?
6. What information was I missing that I wish I had?
7. If this decision turns out badly, what's the most likely failure mode?

After my answers, summarize the entry in a clean format I can revisit.

When the outcome becomes visible, I'll come back and compare to what I predicted. Don't comfort the future-me - show me where reasoning diverged from outcome.
notes: Reread these every 6 months. The point isn't to be right; it's to see your own patterns.

title: Identifying a recurring pattern I want to change
use_when: Sense I keep falling into the same trap and want to name it precisely.
prompt:
I've noticed I keep doing / experiencing [DESCRIBE THE PATTERN]. Recent instances: [LIST 3-4 SPECIFIC EXAMPLES, INCLUDING DATES IF POSSIBLE].

Ask me, one at a time:
1. What did each instance have in common - situation, people, emotional state, time of day, mental state?
2. What did each instance ACTUALLY give me, even if the outcome was bad? (Every recurring pattern serves something.)
3. What did I tell myself the cause was each time? Were those stories the same or different?
4. What did I try to change about it, and why didn't it stick?

After my answers, do this:
- Name the pattern in one sentence using the actual mechanism, not a label.
- Name the payoff. What I get from this, even when it hurts.
- Identify the intervention point - the moment in the sequence where intervention is easiest, before the pattern fully fires.

Don't tell me to "be more self-aware." Identify the specific 90-second window where the choice is still available.
notes: Most patterns survive because the payoff is real. You can't replace something with nothing.

title: Breaking a bad habit: root cause and replacement
use_when: Want to break a specific habit and skip the self-flagellation.
prompt:
The habit: [SPECIFIC - what I do, when, how often]. What I've tried before: [LIST]. Why it didn't stick: [HONEST GUESS]. What this habit gives me when it works: [THE REAL PAYOFF - escape, comfort, stimulation, identity, etc.].

Help me design a replacement, not a suppression. Ask me:
1. What's the trigger that fires this habit - situation, feeling, time, person?
2. What was happening 5 minutes BEFORE the trigger in recent instances?
3. What's the smallest possible alternative behavior that could fire on the same trigger and provide a related (not identical) payoff?

Then give me:
- The trigger I'm targeting (specific)
- The replacement behavior (small, doable in the same situation)
- An environmental change that makes the old habit slightly harder
- A non-shame slip protocol: when I do the old habit, here's what happens next

Don't tell me to "just have more discipline." Don't recommend cold showers, ice, or pain-based interventions. If the situation suggests deeper issues (compulsion, self-harm, dependency), say so directly and recommend professional support.
notes: Suppression alone almost always fails. Replacement that honors the payoff is the move.

title: Building a new habit using the minimum viable version
use_when: Want to start a new habit without setting a bar so high I quit by day four.
prompt:
The habit I want to build: [DESIRED HABIT]. Why I want it: [REAL REASON, not aspirational]. When in my day it could fit: [REALISTIC WINDOW]. What I've tried before with this habit: [HISTORY].

Help me design the MVH (minimum viable habit). Ask:
1. What's the smallest version of this habit that would still count?
2. What would the smallest version SMALLER THAN THAT look like - almost embarrassingly small?
3. What existing daily action could trigger this new habit (habit stacking)?
4. What's the failure mode of past attempts - did I overcommit and break, or under-commit and lose interest?

Then give me:
- The MVH (a version so small I can't fail)
- The trigger it stacks onto
- A "level up" criterion - when and how to grow it (only after N consecutive weeks of the MVH)
- The day-after-missing protocol: don't double up, just resume

The bar is doing the habit on the worst day of the week, not the best.
notes: The point isn't doing the habit big. It's becoming someone who does the habit at all.

title: Identifying the one thing I'm avoiding
use_when: Suspect there's something I'm dodging but can't quite name it.
prompt:
Help me surface what I'm avoiding. Ask me, one at a time:

1. What's the task or conversation that pops into my head and gets pushed away most often this week?
2. What am I checking my phone or doing busywork to avoid feeling?
3. If a wise friend looked at my calendar and to-do list, what would they notice is conspicuously missing?
4. What would I do this afternoon if I weren't allowed to do anything that felt productive but easy?
5. What's the worst-case outcome I'm imagining if I face the thing - and how likely is it really?

After my answers, do this:
- Name the thing in one sentence
- Identify whether it's avoidance of action, conversation, decision, or feeling
- Propose the smallest possible step toward it that I could take in the next 24 hours
- Identify the cost of continued avoidance - what does it become if I keep dodging it for another month?

Don't moralize about avoidance. Most avoidance is intelligent fear management - but only up to a point.
notes: You don't have to do the whole thing today. You have to stop pretending it's not there.

title: Procrastination diagnostic - why am I really stuck?
use_when: Procrastinating on a specific task and don't know why.
prompt:
The task I'm avoiding: [SPECIFIC TASK]. Deadline: [WHEN]. How long it should take if I just did it: [ESTIMATE]. What I've been doing instead: [HONEST].

Ask me, one at a time:
1. Is it unclear what done looks like? If so, what would clarify it?
2. Is it boring? If so, is it boring because it's beneath my skill, or because it's tedious in a way that's just real?
3. Is it threatening - does completing it expose me to judgment, feedback, or failure?
4. Is it too big? Where would I start if I had only 25 minutes?
5. Is it not actually mine - am I doing this because someone else's standards say I should?
6. Am I tired in a way that no amount of "just start" will fix?

Based on the answers, tell me:
- The likely actual cause (probably one or two of the above)
- The matching intervention (not a generic "just start," but the one that fits the cause)
- For threat-based avoidance specifically: what's the cost of doing it badly versus not doing it?

If the answer to #6 is yes, the intervention is rest, not productivity advice.
notes: Procrastination is information. It's telling you something specific about the task or yourself.

title: Energy audit - what gives vs drains
use_when: Tired in a non-physical way and want to see where my energy is actually going.
prompt:
Walk me through an energy audit. Ask me:

1. List 5 things from the last two weeks that ENERGIZED me - specific moments, not categories. What was happening, who was there, what was I doing?
2. List 5 things from the last two weeks that DRAINED me. Same level of specificity.
3. What pattern shows up across the energizing list?
4. What pattern shows up across the draining list?
5. Which drain item is recurring and which was a one-time situation?
6. Which energizing item could I add more of in the next two weeks?
7. Which drain item could I subtract, delegate, restructure, or set a boundary around?

Then give me:
- The top 1-2 recurring drains worth addressing
- The top 1-2 energizing patterns worth amplifying
- One specific change to make in the next week (add one thing OR subtract one thing - not both at once)

Don't suggest I "just say no more." Identify the specific situation and the specific words.
notes: Boring fact: the drain is usually a specific person or a specific recurring meeting. Look there first.

title: Relationship audit - who to deepen, who to prune
use_when: Sense my relationship landscape needs review - some connections matter more than the time I give them, others get more than they deserve.
prompt:
List the 10-15 most active relationships in my life right now (family, friends, colleagues, past relationships still in contact). For each, I'll note: how often we connect, who initiates, how I feel after, and what we exchange (support, fun, obligation, etc.).

Ask me to fill in the list. Then walk me through:

1. Which relationships am I undernourishing - people who matter but who I haven't reached out to in 3+ months?
2. Which relationships am I overgiving to - people who consume more than they return, where I leave feeling depleted?
3. Which relationships do I keep going out of guilt or history, that no longer match who I am?
4. Which relationship would I most regret losing in 5 years if I kept current trajectory?

For each finding, propose:
- For undernourished: one specific reach-out this week (the exact message or call to make)
- For overgiving: where to put a small boundary (a less-frequent meet, a topic to stop bringing up, a request to stop accepting)
- For obligation-only: whether to actively close or just let dwindle, and what closure (if any) it deserves
- For high-stakes future regret: the next investment to make

Don't recommend cutting people off impulsively. Closure is usually a process, not a moment.
notes: Most relationship work is in the small acts - a text on the right day, leaving a conversation 15 minutes earlier.

title: Identifying what I'm performing vs what I actually want
use_when: Suspect some of my goals are inherited or performed for others, not actually mine.
prompt:
Help me separate the goals I'm actually pursuing from the ones I'm performing. Ask me:

1. List 5 things I'm currently working toward (career, lifestyle, relationship, body, status).
2. For each, who would notice or be impressed if I achieved it? Who would care? (Be specific - name actual people or audiences.)
3. For each, would I still want this if no one would ever know I achieved it?
4. For each, what is the actual moment-to-moment activity required - and do I enjoy or tolerate that activity?
5. Which of these goals did I want 5 years ago, and which are recent? What shifted?

Then tell me:
- Which goals appear to be performance for a specific audience (name it)
- Which goals survive the "no one knows" test
- Which goal I'm spending the most energy on that ranks lowest on the "actually want" axis
- One question to sit with this week: what would change if I dropped the lowest-ranking goal?

Don't tell me to "live authentically." Identify the specific goal and the specific audience.
notes: You don't have to drop it. You have to know it's a performance, so you can decide whether to keep performing.

title: Defining 'enough' for a goal I keep moving the goalposts on
use_when: Achieving things but the bar keeps moving - want to define a stopping point.
prompt:
The goal I keep moving the bar on: [WHAT - income, body, achievements, followers, productivity]. Original version of the goal years ago: [WHAT IT WAS]. Current version: [WHAT IT IS NOW]. Whether I'm closer or further from satisfaction: [HONEST].

Ask me:
1. The last time I hit a milestone in this domain, how long until I set a new, higher one?
2. What am I really chasing - the achievement, the feeling, the identity, the comparison?
3. Who am I measuring against, and is that person's situation actually comparable to mine?
4. What would "enough" look like as a specific, observable state - not a feeling, but a concrete description?
5. If I hit that "enough," what would I do with the energy currently spent climbing?

Then:
- Propose a defined "enough" with concrete criteria
- Propose a check-in rhythm to revisit it (quarterly?) so it's not unchangeable, but not drifting upward by default
- Identify the underlying need that the goalpost-moving was managing - what would actually address THAT need?

Don't recommend complacency. "Enough" is a chosen line, not a surrender.
notes: Write down the 'enough' definition. When you hit it, you owe yourself the moment of acknowledgment.

title: Imposter syndrome reality check with evidence
use_when: Feeling like a fraud at work / in a role and want to evaluate the feeling against actual data.
prompt:
The role / context: [WHERE I FEEL LIKE A FRAUD]. The specific belief: [I'M NOT [QUALIFIED / EXPERIENCED / SMART] ENOUGH BECAUSE...]. How long I've held this role: [DURATION].

Help me evaluate this against evidence. Ask me:
1. What specific outcomes have I delivered in this role? (Concrete results, not feelings.)
2. What's the feedback I've gotten - positive and critical - from people whose judgment I trust?
3. Where am I comparing myself to: peers, mentors, or an imagined ideal version of the role?
4. What does the actual gap look like - what skill or knowledge would I have to demonstrably acquire to "qualify"? Is that gap real?
5. What would I tell a friend with my actual record who described themselves the way I'm describing myself?

Then tell me:
- The strongest evidence FOR the fraud belief (steelman it honestly)
- The strongest evidence AGAINST
- Whether the feeling is calibration (a real signal I'm underprepared somewhere specific) or contamination (a generalized doubt that's poorly aimed)
- If calibration: what specific gap to close. If contamination: what specific reframe.

Don't tell me "everyone feels this way." Look at the actual evidence.
notes: Calibration is useful. Contamination is just static. The difference is whether there's a specific gap to close.

title: Comparison-mindset reframe when someone else's wins sting
use_when: Saw someone's news / post / achievement and it landed sideways - want to handle the comparison cleanly.
prompt:
I'm feeling [JEALOUSY / RESENTMENT / INADEQUACY] about [PERSON'S ACHIEVEMENT OR SITUATION]. What I told myself in the moment: [STORY].

Ask me:
1. What specifically about their situation am I comparing myself to - the achievement, the recognition, the apparent ease, the timing?
2. What do I actually know about how they got there - versus what I'm assuming?
3. What would my situation look like through the same lens (curated for an audience)?
4. If I subtract them from the picture, am I dissatisfied with my own trajectory - or only when I see theirs?
5. Is this comparison pointing at something I genuinely want, or at a status I think I'm supposed to want?

Then:
- Name what the comparison is actually about (often: timing, recognition, or trajectory - not the specific thing)
- Separate the signal (this matters to me) from the noise (their version isn't my version)
- One concrete action if there's a signal worth acting on
- One specific reframe if it's mostly noise

Don't tell me "comparison is the thief of joy." That's a poster. Identify what's actually happening.
notes: The sting is information. Sometimes it's about them; usually it's about something you've been quiet about wanting.

title: Honest conversation with my future self, 5 years out
use_when: Stuck on a current decision or pattern and want to consult the version of me down the road.
prompt:
## MISSION
Run a simulated conversation with the user's future self that surfaces what they're overestimating, underestimating, and avoiding - without flattering them or predicting events.

## AGENT
You will simulate a conversation with my future self, 5 years from today. Future-me has been through everything I'm currently in the middle of. Future-me is honest, slightly impatient with my current evasions, but not cruel.

## SITUATION
My current situation: [DESCRIBE]. The decision, pattern, or question I'm wrestling with: [BE SPECIFIC]. What I'm afraid of: [HONEST]. What I'm hoping for: [HONEST].

## TASK
Run the conversation in this structure:
1. I ask future-me a specific question.
2. Future-me answers with the perspective of having lived the next 5 years - not certainty about what happened, but the wisdom of seeing this same situation in retrospect.
3. I follow up. Future-me follows up.
4. Continue for 6-8 exchanges.

Then, in a final step, future-me names:
- The ONE thing I'm currently overestimating the importance of
- The ONE thing I'm currently underestimating
- The advice they wish I'd take but suspect I won't, and why

## THINK
Before producing the response: (a) check whether the user is asking for reassurance vs. real diagnosis, (b) identify the assumption they are most likely defending without realizing it, (c) name the response that would feel good but not be useful. Choose the useful one.

## RULES
- Future-me does not flatter me.
- Future-me does not predict specific events - they can't see the future. They can see what mattered and what didn't.
- Future-me speaks in plain English, not aphorisms.
- Future-me is allowed to say "I don't know" or "you'll find out."
- No "everything will work out." Future-me has seen real costs.
- Future-me does not give answers I haven't earned in the conversation.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Is the answer something the user could not have written themselves on a good day, or am I echoing their setup back to them? (2) Did I push back where pushing back would help, or did I default to validation? If either, revise.

## OUTPUT
Deliver the final output only, in the structure named in TASK. No preamble. No restatement of the question. No "let me know if you need anything else" closer.
notes: This works because it gives you permission to say what you already half-know. The trick isn't predicting; it's listening.

title: Examining a strongly held belief - what would change my mind?
use_when: Want to test a belief I hold with conviction, before I confuse certainty for truth.
prompt:
The belief I want to examine: [STATE IT IN ONE SENTENCE]. How long I've held it: [DURATION]. What it lets me do or feel: [WHAT THIS BELIEF GIVES ME - direction, identity, justification, comfort].

Ask me, one at a time:
1. What evidence would I need to see to update this belief by 10%?
2. What evidence would I need to see to update it by 50%?
3. What evidence would I never accept, even if it appeared - and why?
4. Where did I acquire this belief - was it derived from my own experience, or inherited from a person, group, or culture?
