Rules: match field names and order exactly. If a field can't be filled from the input, output "unknown" - do not guess. Times in 24h. External contacts include company in parens.
notes: The 'unknown not guess' rule is what makes this safe for downstream automation.

title: Few-shot for style matching
use_when: You want the model to write in a specific voice and you have samples of that voice.
prompt:
## TASK
Write a new piece in the voice demonstrated by the three samples below. Match sentence rhythm, vocabulary level, and structural habits - not the topic.

## VOICE SAMPLES (study these for STYLE, not content)

SAMPLE 1:
"The deck arrived Tuesday. It was bad. Not bad-in-an-interesting way - bad in the way every deck is bad when the team has fallen in love with the problem and forgotten the customer. I sent it back with three notes."

SAMPLE 2:
"There's a pattern in these reviews. Every quarter, someone catches a bug that shipped six months ago. Every quarter, we promise a process change. Every quarter, the process change lasts about as long as a New Year's resolution. The bug isn't the bug."

SAMPLE 3:
"I used to think rituals were optional. Then I watched three teams ship faster than mine because they had ruthless, boring rituals - the kind that look bureaucratic until you realize bureaucracy is just memory."

## STYLE NOTES (from the samples)
- Short opening sentences. Drop the reader in.
- Em-dashes for the punch.
- Lists of three (notes, patterns, teams).
- The kicker line restates the title or premise from a new angle.
- No adverbs. No "really," "very," "actually."

## TASK
Write a [paragraph / short post] on [TOPIC] in this voice.

## RULES
- Do NOT copy phrases from the samples. Copy the rhythm, not the words.
- Do NOT introduce vocabulary that isn't in the samples' register.
- Do NOT exceed the sample length by more than 30%.
- If the topic requires technical terms not in the samples' style, use them sparingly and define them like the samples define things (with an example, not a textbook).
notes: Three samples is enough for voice. Topic should be different from the samples or the model will mimic content too.

title: Few-shot tone calibration (good vs bad)
use_when: You want the model to land a specific tone by contrasting a good example with a bad one.
prompt:
Write a new piece matching the tone of GOOD and avoiding the failure mode of BAD.

GOOD EXAMPLE (target tone - direct, warm, no fluff):
"Hey Maria - I read your draft last night. Two things worth changing: the opening buries the lede, and the conclusion repeats what paragraph 4 already said. Everything else holds. Want me to mark up the file or talk through it?"

BAD EXAMPLE (what to avoid - corporate softener overload):
"Hi Maria, I hope this email finds you well! I had the opportunity to review your wonderful draft, and I just wanted to share some thoughts. Your work is truly impressive, and I think there may be a couple of small opportunities for enhancement..."

DIFFERENCES TO INTERNALIZE:
- GOOD names the person and gets to the point. BAD opens with "hope this finds you."
- GOOD names two specific issues. BAD says "small opportunities."
- GOOD ends with an actionable choice. BAD trails off.
- GOOD trusts the recipient. BAD softens because the writer is nervous.

Now write: [TASK - e.g., feedback email to a colleague, response to a client, message to a teammate]

Rules: write in GOOD tone. If you find yourself reaching for BAD-style softeners, stop and rewrite the line directly.
notes: The contrast pattern works better than either example alone. Models learn from the gap, not the target.

title: Plan-and-solve - plan first, then execute
use_when: You want the model to write a plan before doing the work, so you can correct the plan if it's wrong.
prompt:
For this task, do not start solving yet. First write a plan. Then I will tell you to execute (or correct the plan first).

STEP 1 - PLAN (write this now):
- Goal: [restate what success looks like]
- Approach: [the method you'll use, in 3-5 numbered steps]
- Inputs needed: [what info you have, what you'd assume]
- Verification: [how you'll check the work at the end]

STOP after the plan. Do not execute. Wait for my "go" or my corrections.

Task: [TASK]

Rules:
- Do NOT proceed past PLAN until I respond.
- Do NOT make the plan generic ("research, analyze, write"). Be specific to the task.
- If the plan reveals the task is underspecified, say so and ask the clarifying question instead of planning around the gap.
notes: The 'stop and wait' instruction is the differentiator. Without it, models plan AND execute in one go.

title: Reflexion - answer, critique, revise
use_when: You want a higher-quality answer by forcing the model to critique its first draft.
prompt:
## PATTERN
Produce an answer in three passes: draft, critique, revise.

## STRUCTURE

PASS 1 - DRAFT
[Write your best first answer. Don't optimize - just write.]

PASS 2 - CRITIQUE
Now read PASS 1 as if you didn't write it. Identify:
- Two specific weaknesses (vague claims, missing evidence, weak transitions, ungrounded assumptions).
- One thing that's actually working well that should stay.
- One thing missing entirely.

PASS 3 - REVISE
Rewrite the answer addressing the critique. Keep what worked. Cut what didn't. Add what was missing.

## RULES
- Do NOT skip the critique pass. The critique is the value.
- Do NOT critique trivially ("could be more polished") - be specific.
- Do NOT preserve text from PASS 1 just because you wrote it. Cut anything PASS 2 flagged.
- Do NOT loop into PASS 4. Two passes is enough; more is procrastination.

## TASK
[TASK]

## OUTPUT
All three passes, labeled. The user reads PASS 3 but can audit the work in PASS 2.
notes: The critique pass catches what one-shot writing misses. The 'two passes max' rule prevents infinite polish.

title: Constitutional check - rules-based self-review
use_when: You want the model to verify its output against a list of explicit rules before delivering.
prompt:
## TASK
Produce output for the task below, then run it through the CONSTITUTION before delivering.

## CONSTITUTION (the rules every output must satisfy)
1. Every factual claim has a source noted OR is flagged "from general knowledge - verify."
2. No claim about a specific person, company, or product without an attribution.
3. No use of "obviously," "clearly," "simply" - these hide unsupported leaps.
4. Every recommendation includes a counter-case or boundary condition.
5. The output addresses what was asked, not an adjacent easier question.

## PROCESS

STEP 1 - DRAFT
Produce the output normally.

STEP 2 - CONSTITUTION CHECK
Go through each rule. For each: PASS or FAIL with a quoted line if FAIL.

STEP 3 - FIX
For each FAIL, revise the specific line. Show the before -> after.

STEP 4 - DELIVER
Final output (clean version, no annotations).

## RULES
- Do NOT skip STEP 2 even if the draft "feels fine."
- Do NOT mark PASS without naming what satisfied the rule.
- If a rule cannot be satisfied for a particular output type, say so explicitly rather than fudging.

## TASK
[TASK]
notes: Customize the constitution per use case. The default 5 rules catch generic LLM failure modes; replace them with domain-specific ones for higher leverage.

title: Self-ask - break into sub-questions
use_when: You want the model to decompose a hard question into smaller answerable ones.
prompt:
For the question below, do NOT answer directly. Instead, break it into sub-questions, answer each, then synthesize.

QUESTION: [QUESTION]

STEP 1 - DECOMPOSITION
List the sub-questions whose answers would, together, answer the main question. Aim for 3-5. Each sub-question should be answerable on its own.

STEP 2 - ANSWER EACH SUB-QUESTION
For each: answer in 1-3 sentences. Flag confidence (high/med/low) and why.

STEP 3 - SYNTHESIS
Combine the sub-answers into a response to the original question. Identify which sub-answer is doing the most work in the synthesis - that's the load-bearing claim.

STEP 4 - CHECK
Did you actually answer the question that was asked, or an adjacent one? Quote the original. If you drifted, flag it.

Rules:
- Do NOT make sub-questions trivial restatements of the main question.
- Do NOT skip the confidence flags - they tell the user where to push back.
- Do NOT skip STEP 4. Most self-ask outputs drift.
notes: Best for: questions that mix factual, interpretive, and predictive layers. Wasteful for: simple factual lookups.

title: Skeleton-of-thought - outline first, fill second
use_when: You want a longer output without the model losing structure halfway through.
prompt:
For this writing task, produce in two passes:

PASS 1 - SKELETON
Outline the full response as a bulleted skeleton. Top-level points only. Aim for 5-8 bullets. Each bullet is one sentence stating what that section will cover (not the content itself).

PAUSE - review the skeleton. Does it answer the prompt? Does it cover what matters? Adjust before filling.

PASS 2 - FILL
Expand each bullet into a full paragraph (or whatever the task requires). Stay within the skeleton's structure - do not add new top-level points mid-fill. If you find you need to add one, return to PASS 1, update the skeleton, then continue.

Rules:
- Do NOT skip the skeleton even if you "know" what to write.
- Do NOT let the fill paragraphs balloon past the skeleton's promise.
- Do NOT add a conclusion not in the skeleton.

Task: [TASK]
notes: Excellent for long-form. Models lose structure around 800 words; the skeleton holds it.

title: Backwards chaining - start from the goal
use_when: You want to plan or design by starting at the desired outcome and working backward.
prompt:
Plan this backward, starting from the goal.

GOAL STATE (write first): [What does success look like, concretely? Include the date, the measurable result, the user-visible artifact.]

NOW WORK BACKWARD:

T-MINUS 1 (the step immediately before the goal): What had to be true the day before, for the goal to happen? Be specific.

T-MINUS 2: What had to be true a week/sprint before that?

T-MINUS 3: ...

Continue backward until you reach today.

Then:
- Identify the T-MINUS step that is LEAST under your control. That's the riskiest dependency.
- Identify the T-MINUS step you could start TOMORROW.

Rules:
- Do NOT plan forward. Start from the goal. Forward planning generates wishlists; backward planning generates dependencies.
- Do NOT leave any T-MINUS step abstract ("get buy-in"). Each step must be a specific verifiable event.
- The earliest T-MINUS step should be something you can do without any prerequisites.

Task: [PROJECT / DECISION / GOAL]
notes: The 'least under your control' question is the load-bearing. That's where you need a Plan B before starting.

title: Worked-example prompting
use_when: You want the model to apply a method to a new case by first showing the full method on a similar case.
prompt:
## PATTERN
Apply method M to NEW PROBLEM by first walking through it on EXAMPLE PROBLEM, then applying the same method to NEW PROBLEM.

## EXAMPLE PROBLEM (with full worked solution)

PROBLEM: Calculate compound annual growth rate (CAGR) for a portfolio that grew from $50,000 to $87,000 over 4 years.

SOLUTION (showing every step):
Step 1 - Recall formula: CAGR = (End / Start)^(1/n) - 1
Step 2 - Identify values: End = 87,000, Start = 50,000, n = 4
Step 3 - Substitute: CAGR = (87,000 / 50,000)^(1/4) - 1
Step 4 - Simplify ratio: 87,000 / 50,000 = 1.74
Step 5 - Apply exponent: 1.74^(0.25) = 1.1483
Step 6 - Subtract 1: 1.1483 - 1 = 0.1483
Step 7 - Express as %: 14.83%
Step 8 - Sanity check: 14.83% over 4 years should roughly equal the simple (87-50)/50 / 4 = 18.5% per year on linear basis. CAGR is lower than linear, as expected for compounding. Passes check.

ANSWER: 14.83%

## NEW PROBLEM
[NEW PROBLEM - same method, different numbers/case]

## RULES
- Apply the SAME steps in the SAME order. Do not skip the sanity check.
- Show your work step-by-step, mirroring the EXAMPLE's structure.
- If a step doesn't apply, say "Step N does not apply because..." rather than skipping silently.
- If the new problem requires a variant of the method, name the variant and what changed.

## OUTPUT
Numbered steps, then ANSWER, then a sanity check.
notes: Best for: technical problems where the user understands the method but wants reliable application. Wasteful for: open-ended writing.

title: Perspective stacking - answer from N viewpoints
use_when: You want a decision viewed from multiple stakeholder lenses before you settle on a recommendation.
prompt:
## TASK
Analyze [QUESTION / DECISION] from each of the perspectives below, then synthesize.

## PERSPECTIVES
1. CUSTOMER: What does someone who uses the product think? What do they care about that the team forgets?
2. ENGINEER who has to build and maintain this: What are the failure modes, the on-call burden, the tech debt cost?
3. FINANCE: What does the P&L look like in year 1, year 2? What's the cost of being wrong?
4. COMPETITOR: If you ran the rival company, would you welcome or fear this move? Why?
5. REGULATOR or skeptical outsider: What's the worst-faith reading? What's the headline risk?

## STRUCTURE
For each perspective:
- Top concern (one sentence).
- Strongest argument FOR the decision from this perspective.
- Strongest argument AGAINST.
- What this perspective would want to see proven before proceeding.

## SYNTHESIS
- Where do the perspectives agree? (High confidence area.)
- Where do they conflict? (Where the real decision lives.)
- Which perspective should weigh most heavily for THIS decision, and why?
- Recommended next step.

## RULES
- Do NOT collapse perspectives into "everyone agrees." If they would in real life, that's a thin decision worth re-examining.
- Do NOT make perspectives caricatures (the engineer isn't always saying no, finance isn't always saying cheaper).
- Do NOT weight all perspectives equally in synthesis - some matter more for a given call.

## INPUT
[DECISION OR QUESTION]
notes: 5 perspectives is the right ceiling. Adding more dilutes the analysis without surfacing new conflict.

title: Generate-then-rank - 5 ideas, then rank by criterion
use_when: You want divergent ideas first, then convergent ranking - in one structured pass.
prompt:
Two-phase output: generate, then rank.

PHASE 1 - GENERATE (5 ideas, no judgment):
Produce 5 distinct ideas for [TASK]. Make them genuinely different - not 5 variants of one idea. Number them 1-5. Two sentences each max.

PHASE 2 - RANK by criterion: [CRITERION - e.g., cost to test, time to first signal, reversibility, impact-if-it-works]:
Reorder the 5 ideas best-to-worst on the named criterion. For each: one line on why it ranks where it does.

PHASE 3 - RECOMMEND:
The #1 idea by criterion is the recommended next step. But: if #5 (worst by criterion) has uniquely high payoff, name it as a parallel bet to consider.

Rules:
- Do NOT judge during PHASE 1. Just generate.
- Do NOT generate variations of the same idea - 5 means 5 distinct approaches.
- Do NOT change the criterion mid-rank. State it once, apply it consistently.
- If the criterion is fuzzy, ask before ranking.

Task: [TASK]
Criterion: [CRITERION]
notes: The criterion-naming step is the lever. Same 5 ideas, different criterion, different winner.

title: Multi-step verification chain
use_when: You want a high-stakes answer that has been checked through multiple independent verifications.
prompt:
## PATTERN
Produce an answer, then verify it through three independent checks before delivering.

## STEP 1 - ANSWER
Produce the answer with your reasoning.

## STEP 2 - VERIFICATION CHAIN

CHECK A - Reverse derivation: Start from the answer and work backward to the inputs. Do they match the original problem?

CHECK B - Boundary / sanity test: Does the answer pass a sanity bound? (Order of magnitude, sign, units, plausible range.) Name the bound, then test.

CHECK C - Alternative method: Solve the same problem a different way. Does it give the same answer?

For each check: PASS or FAIL with one sentence why.

## STEP 3 - RECONCILE
If all checks pass: state final answer with confidence HIGH.
If one check fails: investigate, revise, re-run checks. Do not just override the failed check.
If two or more checks fail: the original answer is likely wrong. Re-do STEP 1 from scratch.

## RULES
- Do NOT mark a check PASS without showing the work.
- Do NOT skip a check because the answer "feels right."
- Do NOT reconcile by handwave. A failed check means a real disagreement to resolve.
- If the problem genuinely cannot be checked by three independent methods, name which check is unavailable and why.

## TASK
[TASK - best for high-stakes calculations, factual claims with downstream consequences, or anything you'd be embarrassed to get wrong]
notes: Expensive (uses lots of tokens) but worth it when you can't afford a wrong answer. Use sparingly.

title: Decision-Driving Meeting Agenda (not a status read-out)
use_when: Calling a 45-minute meeting that needs to produce a real decision, not a recap.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a 45-minute agenda that ends with one or more decisions made - not status reported, not items punted to next week.

## AGENT
You are a meeting designer who has run hundreds of decision-making meetings. You know that the difference between a meeting that produces a decision and a meeting that produces a follow-up is the agenda, the pre-read, and the ruthless cutting of items that aren't load-bearing on the decision.

## SITUATION
- Meeting: [Q3 pricing change go/no-go]
- Length: [45 minutes]
- Attendees and their roles: [Maya - PM, Devon - Eng lead, Priya - Finance, Sam - Sales VP]
- Decision required by end of meeting: [Ship the new tiered pricing on Sep 1, delay to Q4, or kill it]
- Pre-read already sent: [yes/no - what doc]
- Last meeting on this topic ended with: [no decision, three open questions]

## TASK
Write the agenda as a working document, not a slide outline.

STRUCTURE the agenda as:
1. The single decision to be made (one sentence, stated as a question with options)
2. Time-boxed segments (minutes per segment, total <= meeting length minus 5 buffer)
3. For each segment: who owns it, what input is needed, what output it produces
4. Pre-commitments expected from each attendee before they arrive
5. The "if we run out of time" fallback (asynchronous resolution path)
6. What gets captured in writing at the end (decision + owner + date)

## THINK
Before producing the agenda or output: (a) identify the participant whose interests are most likely to be underweighted, (b) name the topic that will eat all the time if not capped, (c) identify the decision the user is hoping to avoid. Use this to shape the output.

## RULES
- No segment titled "discussion," "updates," or "alignment." Every segment must have a verb that ends in a deliverable.
- Status updates are banned. If someone needs context, it goes in the pre-read.
- If the agenda cannot fit in the time box, cut scope, not buffer.
- Do not invent attendees or context I did not provide.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Agenda table + a 3-line opener the meeting owner reads aloud at minute zero.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Could a participant skim this in 60 seconds and know what is expected of them? (2) Did I cut the agenda items that exist only to make the meeting look productive? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Deliver the final output only, in the structure named in TASK. No preamble. No restatement of the question. No "let me know if you need anything else" closer.
notes: Forces a real decision and kills status-meeting drift.

title: High-Stakes Meeting Pre-Read
use_when: Sending a doc 24-48 hours before a meeting so the meeting can skip context and go straight to decision.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a pre-read that lets the meeting skip context and start at the decision point, so the actual meeting time goes to discussion and the decision, not catching everyone up.

## AGENT
You are a chief of staff who has written pre-reads for executive meetings where every minute costs money. You compress context to the minimum required for someone to walk in ready to decide, not catch up.

## SITUATION
- Meeting topic: [whether to acquire-hire the four-person ML team from Lattice.io]
- Attendees: [CEO, CTO, CFO, Head of Talent]
- Their prior knowledge: [CEO has met the team once; others have not]
- Decision needed: [proceed to LOI, walk away, or pursue a contractor arrangement]
- I have: [team backgrounds, salary asks, integration plan draft, comp model]

## TASK
Draft a pre-read of 800-1200 words.

SECTIONS (in this order):
1. TL;DR (3 sentences max - what we're deciding and my recommendation)
2. Why this matters now (the forcing function or window)
3. What we know (facts, with confidence levels)
4. What we don't know (the open questions that will not be resolved before the meeting)
5. Three options with honest trade-offs (not two strawmen and a favorite)
6. My recommendation and the reasoning chain
7. Questions I want the room to answer (numbered, ranked by importance)

## THINK
Before producing the agenda or output: (a) identify the participant whose interests are most likely to be underweighted, (b) name the topic that will eat all the time if not capped, (c) identify the decision the user is hoping to avoid. Use this to shape the output.

## RULES
- Lead with the decision and recommendation. Do not bury them.
- Every claim needs a source or a confidence label ("high confidence," "estimate," "guess").
- No marketing voice. No "exciting opportunity." Write like the reader is skeptical.
- If a number is uncertain, give a range, not false precision.
- Do not pad to length.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Could a participant skim this in 60 seconds and know what is expected of them? (2) Did I cut the agenda items that exist only to make the meeting look productive? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
The pre-read doc, ready to send.
notes: Length cap forces compression. The 'don't know' section is what most pre-reads skip.

title: Should This Be a Meeting? Filter
use_when: Considering scheduling a meeting and want to gut-check before sending the invite.
prompt:
I am about to schedule a meeting about: [describe the topic in one sentence]

Attendees I'm considering: [list]
Why I think a meeting is needed: [explain]

Run the meeting through this filter and give me a direct verdict:

1. Is there a specific decision to be made? If not, what async format would work instead (doc, Loom, Slack thread)?
2. Are all the right deciders going to be in the room? If not, what changes?
3. Could a 5-minute Loom + a comment thread replace this?
4. Is the topic still in the "thinking" phase (then a doc is better) or the "deciding" phase (then a meeting is fine)?
5. What is the smallest version of this meeting that would still work (fewer people, shorter time)?

End with one of: "Hold the meeting," "Replace with async [format]," or "Cut scope to [smaller version]."

Be willing to tell me I should not hold this meeting.
notes: The constraint that matters: the model must be willing to say 'don't meet.'

title: Difficult-Conversation Pre-Meeting Prep
use_when: Before a hard 1:1 - performance issue, conflict, delivering bad news, pushing back on a boss.
prompt:
## MISSION
Prepare for a hard conversation so the user enters knowing what they want, what they will and won't concede, and how to handle the three most likely emotional turns.

## AGENT
You are a manager-coach who has prepped hundreds of difficult conversations. You know the difference between confidence and rehearsal, and you separate what the user wants to say from what will actually land.

## SITUATION
- Who I'm meeting with: [direct report, peer, my manager - specify]
- The relationship: [length, baseline trust level, recent friction]
- The hard thing I need to say: [one sentence, blunt]
- What I want them to do differently or know: [outcome]
- What I'm afraid of: [the actual fear - them quitting, them crying, them yelling, etc.]
- Time available: [30 minutes]

## TASK
Build the conversation prep.

DELIVER:
1. The opening line - exactly what I say in the first 30 seconds. Direct, not cushioned to the point of vagueness.
2. The core message in one sentence I can repeat if they deflect.
3. Three specific examples or pieces of evidence I should have ready (placeholder where I fill in).
4. Likely reactions and how I respond to each (defensiveness, tears, anger, agreement-without-change, counter-attack).
5. What I am willing to hear and change my mind on - and what is non-negotiable.
6. The close: how I end the conversation with a concrete next step.
7. What I do in the 10 minutes after, regardless of how it goes.

## THINK
Before producing the agenda or output: (a) identify the participant whose interests are most likely to be underweighted, (b) name the topic that will eat all the time if not capped, (c) identify the decision the user is hoping to avoid. Use this to shape the output.

## RULES
- No corporate softening that turns the message into mush. "I have some feedback" is not an opening; it is an evasion.
- Do not script the other person's lines. Script my responses to ranges of reactions.
- Flag any sentence in my draft that sounds like a HR-trained euphemism.
- If the situation suggests I should not have this conversation alone (HR present, witness needed), say so.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Could a participant skim this in 60 seconds and know what is expected of them? (2) Did I cut the agenda items that exist only to make the meeting look productive? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
The prep doc, formatted for me to glance at right before the meeting.
notes: The 'what I'm afraid of' field is what makes this useful. Most prep skips it.

title: Transcript -> Action Items Extractor
use_when: Have a meeting transcript (Otter, Fireflies, Granola) and need clean action items, not a summary.
prompt:
I am pasting a meeting transcript below. Extract action items only.

For each action item, return:
- Owner (named person, not "the team")
- Action (verb-first, specific enough that the owner could not deny it later)
- Due date (if stated; if not stated, mark "no date set")
- Dependencies (if any)
- Source line from the transcript (so I can verify)

RULES:
- If ownership is ambiguous, list it as "UNCLEAR" and quote the relevant line. Do not guess.
- Skip aspirational statements ("we should think about...") unless someone explicitly committed.
- Decisions made are separate from action items - list those in a second table.
- Do not summarize the meeting. Do not paraphrase the discussion. Just actions and decisions.

OUTPUT: Two tables - Action Items, then Decisions Made.

TRANSCRIPT:
[paste]
notes: The 'source line' field prevents fabricated action items and makes verification trivial.

title: Manager <-> Direct Report 1:1 Agenda
use_when: Weekly or biweekly 1:1 with a direct report - making it useful instead of a status read-out.
prompt:
I am [the manager / the report - specify] preparing for a 1:1 with [name].

Context:
- Cadence: [weekly, 30 minutes]
- Their current focus: [project / role]
- Last 1:1 we covered: [topics]
- Energy level recently: [thriving, fine, struggling, unclear]
- Anything brewing: [interpersonal friction, career questions, deadline stress]

Draft the agenda in this order:
1. One opening question that is not "how are you" - something they can actually answer with substance
2. Their topics first (placeholder for them to fill in)
3. My topics (max 3, ranked - if we only get to one, this is the one)
4. Career / growth check-in cadence (every 3rd or 4th 1:1, not every week)
5. Feedback exchange - one specific thing in each direction
6. The close: what we both walk away clear on

CONSTRAINTS:
- Status updates do not belong in a 1:1. If we need them, that is a different meeting.
- No item should be on the agenda just because it was on last week's agenda.
- Flag any topic that is actually two topics in a trench coat.
notes: The constraint banning status updates from 1:1s is the load-bearing rule.

title: Skip-Level Meeting Agenda
use_when: 1:1 with your manager's manager - rare, high-signal, easy to waste.
prompt:
## MISSION
Make the rare skip-level conversation actually useful - the user leaves with signal they couldn't have gotten from their manager, and their skip leaves with signal they couldn't have gotten from a manager-roll-up.

## AGENT
You are a senior leader who has been on both sides of skip-level meetings. You know what topics waste them and what topics genuinely require the skip-level, and you know how to make the rare conversation count.

## SITUATION
- My role: [Senior Engineer]
- Skip-level person: [VP Engineering, Lena]
- How often we meet: [twice a year, 30 minutes]
- My direct manager: [Aman - relationship is good]
- What I want them to walk away knowing: [I'm capable of more scope; I have a perspective on team direction]
- What I do NOT want this to be: [a complaint about my manager; a status report]

## TASK
Build a skip-level agenda that respects the rarity of the slot.

DELIVER:
1. Opening 60 seconds - what I say to set tone (not a resume read-back)
2. Two substantive topics I bring - one about the business, one about myself
3. Two genuine questions for them - things only they can answer, not things I could Google
4. One thing I want feedback on
5. What I do NOT bring up (and why - some topics belong with my direct manager)
6. The close - what I want them to remember about me 48 hours later

## THINK
Before producing the agenda or output: (a) identify the participant whose interests are most likely to be underweighted, (b) name the topic that will eat all the time if not capped, (c) identify the decision the user is hoping to avoid. Use this to shape the output.

## RULES
- Do not draft "build relationship" as a topic. That is a vibe, not an agenda item.
- No questions about company strategy that signal I haven't read the all-hands deck.
- If a topic is really a complaint about my manager, route it through my manager first or HR - not here.
- Be honest about what makes this meeting awkward and how to handle silence.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Could a participant skim this in 60 seconds and know what is expected of them? (2) Did I cut the agenda items that exist only to make the meeting look productive? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Agenda + the one-line "what I want them to remember" anchor.
notes: Forbidding the 'build relationship' filler is the rule that makes this work.

title: Sharp Standup Update (yesterday / today / blockers)
use_when: Daily standup, 60 seconds, and you want your update to be useful instead of noise.
prompt:
Draft my standup update from these notes: [paste rough notes or yesterday's commits, PRs, tickets]

Format:
- Yesterday: what shipped or moved (verb-first, name the artifact or ticket ID)
- Today: what I will finish (singular if possible - one thing done beats three things half-done)
- Blockers: specific and actionable, or none

RULES:
- "Working on [project]" is not an update. Say what state it is in and what moves today.
- If I have no blocker, say "none." Do not invent one to look engaged.
- Cap the whole thing at 50 words. Standup is read aloud, not lived in.
- If I am stuck on the same thing as yesterday, say so explicitly. That is the signal the team needs.

Return the update only, no preamble.
notes: 50-word cap is the constraint. Removes the 'working on stuff' filler.

title: Small-Team Retro Facilitation Plan
use_when: Running a retro for a team of 4-8 after a sprint, project, or incident - and wanting actual change, not vented steam.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a retro plan that ends in 2-3 specific committed changes, not a list of grievances or vague 'we should communicate better' takeaways.

## AGENT
You are an experienced facilitator who has run dozens of retros that produced change vs. ones that produced venting. You design for psychological safety AND for commitment, and you do not let the meeting end without named changes and owners.

## SITUATION
- Team: [6 people, mixed eng + design]
- What we're retro-ing: [the past 4-week sprint / the launch / the incident on Oct 14]
- Last retro produced: [list of action items - which actually happened? which became wallpaper?]
- Energy in the team right now: [tired / frustrated / proud / mixed]
- Time: [60 minutes]

## TASK
Design the retro, not a generic Start/Stop/Continue template.

DELIVER:
1. Opening (5 min) - how we set the tone honestly without forcing fake positivity
2. Data gathering (15 min) - specific format chosen for this team's state, with rationale
3. Insight generation (15 min) - how we cluster and find the real patterns
4. Decisions (15 min) - max 3 action items, each with owner and check-in date
5. Closing (5 min) - one question that surfaces what was NOT said
6. Buffer (5 min)

## THINK
Before producing the agenda or output: (a) identify the participant whose interests are most likely to be underweighted, (b) name the topic that will eat all the time if not capped, (c) identify the decision the user is hoping to avoid. Use this to shape the output.

## RULES
- No more than 3 action items. Teams that leave with 10 items do 0.
- Every action item gets a named owner and a date to revisit. No "the team" owners.
- Track which action items from last retro actually happened. Name them by name.
- If the team is exhausted, the retro is shorter, not longer.
- Do not propose Start/Stop/Continue if it was used last sprint.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Could a participant skim this in 60 seconds and know what is expected of them? (2) Did I cut the agenda items that exist only to make the meeting look productive? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
The facilitation plan + a one-question follow-up to send 1 week later.
notes: 3-item cap and named owners are the rules that prevent retro theater.

title: Time-Block Weekly Schedule Generator
use_when: Designing a weekly schedule that protects deep work and is not just calendar Tetris.
prompt:
Build me a time-blocked weekly schedule.

My inputs:
- Role: [Senior IC engineer]
- Fixed meetings I cannot move: [Mon 10am team sync, Tue 2pm 1:1 with manager, Thu 11am cross-team review]
- Deep work I need protected: [4 hours/day minimum, ideally morning]
- Recurring overhead: [code review ~1hr/day, Slack/email ~45min/day]
- Energy pattern: [sharpest 8-11am, dip 1-3pm, second wind 3-5pm]
- Personal commitments: [gym Mon/Wed/Fri 6am; daughter pickup Thu 3:30pm]

OUTPUT a weekly grid (Mon-Fri, 8am-6pm) showing blocks for:
- Deep work (when, how long, what kind of task fits)
- Meeting allowances (clustered, not scattered)
- Communication windows (when I check Slack/email - twice daily)
- Buffer / admin / shutdown ritual
- Energy match: the hardest task goes in the sharpest block

RULES:
- No more than 2 meetings per day except Tuesday.
- No meetings before 10am - that time is non-negotiable deep work.
- No deep work blocks shorter than 90 minutes - shorter is just task-switching with a label.
- Friday afternoon is review + plan-next-week, not new meetings.

Return the grid + a one-paragraph explanation of the trade-offs you made.
notes: The hard rules (no AM meetings, 90-min minimum) are what separate this from any default calendar.

title: Email-Batching Protocol
use_when: Switching from reactive inbox-watching to deliberate twice-daily processing - and writing the rules you'll actually follow.
prompt:
Design my email-batching protocol.

My situation:
- Volume: [~80 emails/day, ~15 require a real response]
- Role: [I am a [team lead] - some senders expect <2hr response]
- Current pain: [I check email every 10 min and feel constantly behind]
- The fear: [missing something urgent]

DESIGN:
1. The two daily windows (specific times, total length)
2. The escalation channel for true urgency (not email) - and how I tell senders to use it
3. The 4-bucket sort I do in each window (e.g., reply now / reply later / defer / archive)
4. The 2-minute rule - what qualifies, what doesn't
5. The "out of window" rule - what I do if email pings between windows
6. The away message template that retrains expectations without sounding self-important
7. The Friday weekly review - 15 min, what I check for

RULES:
- The protocol must survive contact with one bad day. If it breaks the first time something urgent happens, it's not a protocol.
- No "inbox zero" theatrics. The metric is response quality and protected focus, not zero count.
- The away message must not start with "I'm experimenting with..." Just state the times.

Return the protocol as a 1-page doc I can pin above my desk.
notes: The 'survives one bad day' test is the constraint that kills fragile productivity systems.

title: Calendar Audit - Recurring Meetings to Kill
use_when: Looking at your standing meetings and deciding which ones to cancel, shorten, or convert to async.
prompt:
## MISSION
Identify which recurring meetings on the user's calendar to cancel, shorten, or convert to async - with the actual message to send for each.

## AGENT
You are an executive coach who has audited the calendars of dozens of senior individuals. You know which recurring meetings have outlived their original purpose, and you can recognize the political cost of canceling each one.

## SITUATION
- My recurring meetings (paste the list - name, frequency, length, attendees, purpose if known):
[list]
- My role: [role]
- My weekly meeting load right now: [hours/week]
- Target meeting load: [hours/week]

## TASK
For each recurring meeting, return a verdict and reasoning.

VERDICTS (pick one per meeting):
- KEEP - why it earns its place
- SHORTEN - new length and what gets cut
- LOWER FREQUENCY - new cadence
- ASYNC - what format replaces it (doc, Loom, channel)
- KILL - and what backstop catches what it was supposedly catching

For each, give the 1-sentence reasoning and the 1-sentence script for proposing the change to the owner.

THEN:
- Total hours saved per week if I act on all recommendations
- Which 3 to cut first (lowest risk, highest return)
- The "if challenged" defense for the riskiest cut

## THINK
Before producing the agenda or output: (a) identify the participant whose interests are most likely to be underweighted, (b) name the topic that will eat all the time if not capped, (c) identify the decision the user is hoping to avoid. Use this to shape the output.

## RULES
- Default suspicion: every recurring meeting older than 6 months is a candidate to kill.
- Do not assume a meeting is needed just because it has been on the calendar for years.
- Do not propose killing a meeting I do not own without flagging the political cost.
- If a meeting's purpose is unclear from the title, that is itself a signal.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Could a participant skim this in 60 seconds and know what is expected of them? (2) Did I cut the agenda items that exist only to make the meeting look productive? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Table + the 3 scripts for the first three cuts.
notes: The 'default suspicion' rule is the spine. Most calendar audits are too polite to cut.

title: Declining a Meeting Without Burning the Bridge
use_when: A meeting invite arrives that would break a deep-work block - and you need to decline without sounding precious.
prompt:
Draft my response to a meeting invite I want to decline.

Inputs:
- Who sent it: [name, their role relative to mine - peer, senior, junior, external]
- Meeting topic: [from invite]
- Why they want me: [their stated reason; or my guess]
- Why I'm declining: [conflicts with deep work / not the right person / could be async / scope creep]
- My real preference: [decline outright / propose async / send a delegate / suggest a shorter slot]

Write the response. Three versions:
A) The direct decline (no asynchronous alternative offered)
B) The redirect (here's the better way to get what you need)
C) The conditional yes (I'll come if X)

RULES:
- No apologies for having a calendar. "Sorry I can't make it" is the only apology allowed and only if true.
- No vague gestures at being busy. Either give a real reason or give none.
- If I'm declining because the meeting shouldn't exist, say so politely instead of pretending I have a conflict.
- Each version is 3 sentences or fewer.

Return all three, labeled.
notes: Banning 'sorry I'm so busy' language is what makes the decline land as confident, not whiny.

title: Conference Talk Q&A Prep
use_when: Right before a public talk, panel, or AMA - prepping for the questions you actually fear.
prompt:
## MISSION
Prepare for the Q&A so the user has a tight answer ready for the three questions they actually fear, not the easy ones the moderator will lob.

## AGENT
You are a speaker coach who has prepped people for high-stakes Q&A across academic, technical, and journalistic contexts. You know that the questions speakers fear are usually the most important to prepare for, and that 'I don't know' delivered well is stronger than improvised confidence.

## SITUATION
- The talk: [title, audience, length]
- My core thesis: [one sentence]
- The audience: [who they are, how technical, friendly or skeptical]
- The questions I'm hoping for: [list]
- The questions I'm dreading: [be honest - list them]
- Topics I do NOT want to be drawn into: [list]

## TASK
Build the Q&A prep doc.

DELIVER:
1. For each dreaded question: the actual answer (not a deflection), 30 seconds max, plus a bridge back to my thesis if relevant.
2. The 3 questions I'm not expecting but should expect - and how I answer them.
3. The "I don't know" script - how to say it without losing the room.
4. The hostile-questioner protocol: how to acknowledge, answer the steelman, and move on without escalating.
5. The "out of scope" redirect: phrasing that doesn't sound dismissive.
6. The closing line if Q&A goes badly - one sentence that returns to my thesis.

## THINK
Before producing the agenda or output: (a) identify the participant whose interests are most likely to be underweighted, (b) name the topic that will eat all the time if not capped, (c) identify the decision the user is hoping to avoid. Use this to shape the output.

## RULES
- No corporate non-answers. If I don't know, the script says so.
- Do not propose answers that contradict facts I gave you in context.
- If a dreaded question has a legitimately damning answer, say so and help me prepare to take the hit honestly.
- Flag any answer that sounds like I'm dodging.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Could a participant skim this in 60 seconds and know what is expected of them? (2) Did I cut the agenda items that exist only to make the meeting look productive? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
The prep doc, plus a 3-bullet wallet card I can glance at offstage.
notes: Forcing real answers to dreaded questions beats canned bridging language.

title: Team Off-Site Planning Agenda
use_when: Designing a 1-3 day off-site that produces real outputs instead of trust falls.
prompt:
## MISSION
Design a 1-3 day off-site that ends with named outputs the team commits to, not bonding-as-output.

## AGENT
You are an off-site designer who has run dozens of multi-day team gatherings. You distinguish bonding (a side effect) from named outputs (the actual goal), and you design agendas that survive the energy crash on day two.

## SITUATION
- Team: [size, function, distributed or co-located normally]
- Duration: [2 days, in-person, [city]]
- Last off-site outcome: [what worked, what was theater]
- Three things the team needs from this off-site: [strategic alignment, relationship repair, planning for next quarter, etc.]
- Budget reality: [tight / moderate / generous]
- Energy and friction in the team right now: [honest]

## TASK
Design the off-site agenda block by block.

DELIVER:
1. The single sentence of what we leave with on Day 2 at 5pm. Concrete output.
2. Day 1 schedule: arrival, opening, work blocks, social, evening.
3. Day 2 schedule: work blocks, decision moments, close.
4. The pre-work each person does before arriving (this is what makes the off-site work).
5. The post-off-site follow-through: 30/60/90 day check-ins with specific owners.
6. What we do NOT do: list the off-site cliches we are avoiding and why.
7. Budget allocation roughly: lodging, food, activity, facilitation.

## THINK
Before producing the agenda or output: (a) identify the participant whose interests are most likely to be underweighted, (b) name the topic that will eat all the time if not capped, (c) identify the decision the user is hoping to avoid. Use this to shape the output.

## RULES
- No trust falls, no personality tests, no "share something vulnerable" forced bonding.
- Every work block has a deliverable, not a topic.
- One unstructured evening, not two - people need downtime, not a content marathon.
- If the off-site needs a facilitator other than me, say so.
- Pre-work is mandatory; if it does not happen, the off-site is rescheduled, not adapted.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Could a participant skim this in 60 seconds and know what is expected of them? (2) Did I cut the agenda items that exist only to make the meeting look productive? If either is no, revise.

## OUTPUT
Agenda + pre-work assignment + follow-through plan.
notes: Banning forced-bonding theater and requiring pre-work are the two rules that separate productive off-sites from expensive vacations.

title: Resume Bullet Rewrite - Task -> Impact
use_when: Have a resume bullet that describes what you did but not what changed because of it.
prompt:
## MISSION
Rewrite a resume bullet from a description of what the user did into a description of what changed because of it - specific enough that a hiring manager can defend the candidate in calibration.

## AGENT
You are a senior recruiter who has read 10,000+ resumes for technical and adjacent roles. You can tell within 7 seconds whether a bullet describes a task or describes impact, and you know which numbers actually matter to a hiring manager in calibration.

## SITUATION
- The current bullet: [paste]
- My role and team: [Senior Data Engineer at a 200-person fintech]
- The real impact (what actually changed because of this work): [be specific - I will give numbers if I have them]
- The metrics I have access to: [list]
- The metrics I have access to but should NOT publish (NDA, confidential): [list]
- Target role I'm applying for: [Staff Data Engineer at growth-stage SaaS]

## TASK
Rewrite the bullet in 3 versions, ranked by confidence.

EACH VERSION must:
- Lead with the outcome or scope, not the activity
- Use a specific number when one exists; use a range or proxy when one doesn't ("reduced runtime from 4hr to 22min" beats "improved performance")
- Name the technology or system if it adds signal for the target role
- Be one line at standard font (under 24 words)
- Avoid passive voice and the word "responsible for"

## THINK
Before producing the output: (a) check whether you are pattern-matching career-coach advice or actually engaging with the user's specific situation, (b) identify the move that is conventional but wrong for them, (c) name what the user is most likely overestimating or underestimating about the market.

## RULES
- Do not invent numbers. If I did not give you a metric, do not fabricate one. Use scope language instead ("the pipeline serving 12 product teams").
- Do not use the words: leverage, robust, synergy, passionate, results-driven, seasoned, dynamic, ecosystem.
- If the underlying work was not impactful, tell me so honestly and suggest cutting the bullet entirely.
- Flag any claim in my draft that could not be defended in an interview.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Is the advice specific enough that the user could act on it Monday, or is it generic career-coach material? (2) Did I avoid coaching them to lie about their actual situation? If either, revise.

## OUTPUT
- Three rewrites, labeled A/B/C with the trade-off each makes
- A one-sentence note on which to use if applying to (a) a startup, (b) a big tech company, (c) a non-tech industry
notes: The 'do not fabricate numbers' rule is the most important constraint - models love inventing metrics.

title: LinkedIn Headline + About Section Draft
use_when: Updating your LinkedIn for a job search or a positioning shift.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a LinkedIn headline and About section that gets the right kind of attention - recruiters who match the user's actual target role, not bot traffic and miscellaneous outreach.

## AGENT
You are a profile editor who has reworked LinkedIn for hundreds of senior individuals during job changes. You know what gets the right kind of attention vs. spammy bot traffic, and you know which phrases instantly read as 'looking for a job' even when the user wants to avoid signaling.

## SITUATION
- My current role and target: [currently Senior PM at a B2B SaaS; targeting Director of Product at series B/C SaaS]
- What I want recruiters to know in 3 seconds: [I lead product orgs through 1->10 scaling]
- The audience that matters: [VPs of Product, founders, technical recruiters at growth-stage SaaS]
- Proof I can back up: [scaled team from 3 to 14; launched [product] which now generates [revenue range]]
- What I do NOT want my headline to look like: [a job title slash list / inspirational quote / "helping companies achieve their dreams"]

## TASK
Write 3 headline options and one About section.

HEADLINES (each under 220 chars):
- Option A: Outcome-led (what I move, with proof embedded)
- Option B: Role-clarity-led (specific position language for recruiter search)
- Option C: Hybrid - role on the left, outcome on the right

ABOUT SECTION (3 short paragraphs, around 600 chars total):
- Para 1: What I do now, framed for the target role - not my origin story
- Para 2: A specific proof point with numbers
- Para 3: What I'm looking for and what I'm not looking for

## THINK
Before producing the output: (a) check whether you are pattern-matching career-coach advice or actually engaging with the user's specific situation, (b) identify the move that is conventional but wrong for them, (c) name what the user is most likely overestimating or underestimating about the market.

## RULES
- No "passionate," "results-driven," "thought leader," "synergy," "ecosystem," "navigate," "delve."
- No emoji-bullets unless they earn their place (max 1).
- Do not start the About section with "I'm a [adjective] [role]."
- If a claim cannot be backed up in an interview, do not include it.
- Avoid clichés that recruiters' eyes glaze past - aim for one specific detail that a stranger could not have guessed.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Is the advice specific enough that the user could act on it Monday, or is it generic career-coach material? (2) Did I avoid coaching them to lie about their actual situation? If either, revise.

## OUTPUT
The 3 headlines, the About draft, and a one-line note on which headline pairs best with which About opening.
notes: Specific banned-language list is the load-bearing constraint. AI defaults to LinkedIn-cliche otherwise.

title: Behavioral Interview Answer (STAR with anti-formula safeguards)
use_when: Prepping a STAR answer that doesn't sound like every other STAR answer.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a STAR answer that doesn't sound like every other STAR answer - concrete enough to be memorable, calibrated honestly enough to land trust.

## AGENT
You are an interview coach who has heard hundreds of STAR answers. You can tell a real story from a manufactured one, you know which details make an answer memorable, and you know the calibration moves that protect honesty without sacrificing impact.

## SITUATION
- The question type: ["Tell me about a time you disagreed with your manager"]
- The actual story I want to tell: [give me the rough facts - Situation, what I did, what happened]
- Role I'm interviewing for: [Engineering Manager]
- Level: [first-time EM role / senior IC -> manager transition]
- What the interviewer is probably trying to assess: [ability to handle conflict, communicate up, hold a position with evidence]

## TASK
Draft my answer in 90 seconds spoken (about 220 words).

STRUCTURE:
- Situation: 1-2 sentences, specific enough to be real (named project type, real constraint)
- Task: implicit, not labeled (avoid "my task was to...")
- Action: the bulk of the answer, with the decisions I made and why - including one decision I wasn't sure about
- Result: numbers or named outcomes if real; honest about what was mixed or didn't go to plan
- Reflection: one sentence on what I learned or would do differently - NOT a fake-humble brag

## THINK
Before producing the output: (a) check whether you are pattern-matching career-coach advice or actually engaging with the user's specific situation, (b) identify the move that is conventional but wrong for them, (c) name what the user is most likely overestimating or underestimating about the market.

## RULES
- No mention of "S.T.A.R." or "first I..., then I..., finally..."
- The answer must include one specific detail an interviewer could not have predicted (a name, a number, an unexpected obstacle).
- The reflection must be a real lesson, not "I learned the importance of communication." If the lesson is generic, the story is too generic - tell me and I'll pick a better one.
- Do not exaggerate the result. If it was a partial win, say so. Interviewers trust honesty more than triumph.
- No phrases: "at the end of the day," "stepped up," "took ownership," "drove alignment."

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Is the advice specific enough that the user could act on it Monday, or is it generic career-coach material? (2) Did I avoid coaching them to lie about their actual situation? If either, revise.

## OUTPUT
The 90-second answer + a one-line note on the follow-up question this answer invites and how I'd handle it.
notes: The 'one specific detail an interviewer could not predict' rule is what kills the STAR-formula sound.

title: 90-Second 'Tell Me About Yourself'
use_when: The opening question of nearly every interview, and the one most people fumble.
prompt:
Help me draft my 90-second "tell me about yourself" answer.

Inputs:
- Current role: [Senior PM at fintech, 3 years]
- Role I'm interviewing for: [Group PM at consumer marketplace]
- The thread connecting them: [user-facing product judgment; scaling teams; ambiguous problem spaces]
- One concrete result I'm proud of: [shipped X, which moved Y by Z%]
- Why I'm leaving / open to leaving: [growth ceiling; want bigger scope; mission alignment]
- What I want the interviewer thinking 30 seconds after I finish: [this person could run a 25-person org and I want them on my team]

Draft the answer with this shape:
- Sentence 1: Where I am now and the one-sentence frame for how to read my background
- Sentences 2-4: The 2-3 jobs/projects that matter for THIS role, each with one specific detail
- Sentences 5-6: Why this role, this company, now
- Sentence 7: Hand-off question or end with confidence

RULES:
- Do not start with "I was born in..." Do not give a chronological resume read-out.
- One number, one named project minimum.
- 90 seconds spoken = ~220 words. Cut, don't pad.
- End with a sentence that invites the interviewer's next move, not a thud.

Return the answer + a 1-line "if they cut me off at 60 seconds, the version is..." compressed alternative.
notes: The 'what I want them thinking 30 seconds after' field forces a real point of view.

title: Salary Negotiation Conversation Script
use_when: The offer is on the table and you need to counter without souring it.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a counter-offer conversation script that gets the user closer to the right number without souring the relationship with the future employer.

## AGENT
You are a compensation negotiation coach who has scripted hundreds of counter-offer calls - the ones that worked, and the ones that ended in a rescinded offer. You separate the script from the user's emotional state, and you know exactly where the leverage actually is.

## SITUATION
- Role and company: [Staff Engineer at Series C SaaS, 600 employees]
- Their offer: [base $215K, equity 0.08% over 4 years, $20K signing, target bonus 10%]
- My current comp: [base $195K, equity vested ~$60K/year, no bonus]
- My research on the band for this role at this company size: [base $230-260K, equity 0.1-0.18%]
- Other offers / leverage I have: [verbal at competitor; final round at one other]
- What I actually want, ranked: [1. higher base, 2. higher equity, 3. signing increase, 4. start date flexibility]
- The recruiter's tone so far: [professional, claims this is "their best offer"]

## TASK
Write the script for the negotiation call.

DELIVER:
1. The opening - how I thank without conceding ground
2. The counter - exact words, including a specific number for base and equity, with reasoning anchored to market data (not "my expenses")
3. The pause - what I say after my counter and what I do if they go silent
4. The likely pushback paths and my response to each: "this is our best offer," "we can do base but not equity," "we need to know if you're committed"
5. The trade-up ladder - if base is fixed, what I ask for next, in order
6. The close - language for "I'll think about it overnight" without sounding lukewarm
7. The walk-away line - the words I'd use if they refuse to move

## THINK
Before producing the output: (a) check whether you are pattern-matching career-coach advice or actually engaging with the user's specific situation, (b) identify the move that is conventional but wrong for them, (c) name what the user is most likely overestimating or underestimating about the market.

## RULES
- Never name a number first if I can avoid it; but if I must, anchor high with explicit market reasoning.
- Do not justify with personal expenses, cost of living, or other offers I do not actually have.
- Stay in collaborative-problem-solving frame: "How do we get to a number that works for both of us?" - never adversarial.
- Avoid the words "fair," "deserve," "worth" - they invite a debate I cannot win.
- If they say "is this offer something you can accept?" do not say yes prematurely. The line is given.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Is the advice specific enough that the user could act on it Monday, or is it generic career-coach material? (2) Did I avoid coaching them to lie about their actual situation? If either, revise.

## OUTPUT
The script + a one-paragraph debrief on what to do in the 24 hours after the call.
notes: The 'never justify with personal expenses' rule is the single biggest mistake in salary negotiation.

title: Counter-Offer Evaluation - Current Role vs New Offer
use_when: Your current employer counter-offered after you resigned, or you have a competing offer to compare.
prompt:
Help me evaluate a counter-offer.

Current role:
- Title, comp, tenure: [Senior Engineer, $210K base + $50K equity/yr, 4 years]
- Reasons I started looking: [list - be honest, not just "comp"]
- What the counter-offer added: [+$25K base, accelerated promo path to Staff]

New offer:
- Title, comp, stage: [Staff Engineer, $245K + $90K equity/yr, Series C SaaS]
- What it solves: [scope, title, growth]
- What it risks: [unknown team, longer commute, unproven product]

Run the comparison through these lenses and give me a verdict:

1. Money - all-in 4-year comp, including realistic equity outcomes (give pessimistic, base, optimistic)
2. The original reasons I was leaving - does the counter actually fix them, or just buy time?
3. Counter-offer track record - statistically, what % of people who accept a counter-offer have left within 12 months, and what does that suggest?
4. Trust - have I just told my manager I have one foot out the door? What does that change?
5. The next 18 months - which choice has a better ceiling AND a better floor?
6. The 5-year shadow - which choice opens or closes more doors?

End with: "Take the counter-offer" / "Take the new offer" / "Neither - here's the third path" - and your honest reasoning.

CONSTRAINT: Do not default to "take the new offer" just because that's the conventional advice. Tell me when staying is actually right.
notes: The constraint against defaulting to 'leave' is what makes this useful instead of generic.

title: 'Why This Company / Why This Role' Answer Prep
use_when: The question that separates candidates who did their homework from those who applied to 200 jobs.
prompt:
Help me draft my answer to "why this company, why this role."

Company: [Linear]
Role: [Senior Product Designer]

What I actually know about them (paste from my research):
- Product / what they build: [issue tracking, design-led]
- Stage / size: [Series B, ~80 people]
- Something specific I admire and why: [the way they ship - long-form changelog, opinionated default UX]
- Something I'd push back on or find risky: [single-product company; high design bar, low room for design that doesn't land]
- A person there I've read or watched: [Karri Saarinen's talks on craft]

What I bring that fits:
- Specific match: [I have shipped 3 enterprise tools where I obsessed over the keyboard-first power user]
- A specific belief I hold that aligns: [the best tools disappear; default UX should be opinionated, not flexible]

Draft the answer in 75 seconds spoken (around 180 words):
- Open with the specific thing about them, not a flattery line
- Bridge to my belief or experience that maps to it
- Close with what I'd want to do in the first 90 days

RULES:
- No "I've always admired your mission." The opening must reference something I could only know from real research.
- No naming a competitor poorly. If I contrast, contrast with respect.
- The push-back / risk I named must show up subtly - real candidates have real questions.
- Do not say "I'm excited" more than once.
notes: The 'something I'd push back on' field is what makes the answer sound like a thinking person.

title: References List Strategy - Who to Ask and How
use_when: Building a references list that will actually help, not hurt.
prompt:
Help me build my references strategy.

Context:
- Stage of search: [final round, references requested]
- Target role: [Director of Engineering]
- My pool of possible references (paste with relationship and recency):
  - [Aman, former manager at LastCo, left 2 years ago, strong relationship]
  - [Maya, current peer, would speak well but I haven't told her I'm looking]
  - [Devon, my direct report at PrevCo, left 18 months ago, would speak to my management]
  - [Priya, VP I worked with cross-functionally, knows my strategic work]
  - [Old manager from 6 years ago, very senior, name carries weight, but knows my early-career work]

DELIVER:
1. The recommended 3-reference panel and why these three (coverage: management, peer-level, cross-functional impact)
2. Who to drop and why
3. The "ask" message to each - 4 sentences, specific to the role I'm interviewing for, including what to emphasize
4. The brief I send each reference 48 hours before they're called: role context, what's likely to come up, what to lean into, what to NOT volunteer
5. The thank-you after, regardless of outcome

RULES:
- Do not include anyone I have not spoken to about the search yet. Surprise references go badly.
- Do not stack references from the same team or era - panels need range.
- The brief must not coach them to lie. It coaches them on what to emphasize from what they actually know.
- Tell me if my pool is too thin and I should expand before submitting.
notes: The 'no surprise references' rule is the operational one. The brief-them-but-don't-coach-lies rule is the ethical one.

title: Career-Change Narrative - Why the Pivot Makes Sense
use_when: Explaining a career change without sounding lost, defensive, or like you ran from something.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a career-change narrative the user can defend in interviews - one that doesn't sound defensive, lost, or like running from the prior role.

## AGENT
You are a career coach who has helped many people change tracks mid-career. You know the difference between a pivot narrative that lands and one that reads as defensive, and you know which gaps to address directly vs. let the work speak for.

## SITUATION
- Previous field: [investment banking, 6 years, VP level]
- New field: [product management at a B2B SaaS startup]
- Why I'm changing (the honest reason): [I want to build something, not advise on it; banking pace was unsustainable; I started a side project that worked]
- What I'm worried interviewers will think: [I'll bail when product life gets hard; I want a softer schedule; I'm starting over and they'll have to train me]
- The bridge skills I genuinely have: [client management at scale; structured thinking under deadline; financial fluency for B2B SaaS metrics]
- Anything I've done to prepare: [shipped a side project that has 200 users; took a course; informational interviews with 12 PMs]

## TASK
Draft the career-change narrative as a 2-minute spoken answer, and as a 4-sentence written version for cover letters.

THE NARRATIVE MUST:
- Frame the change as a continuation, not a rejection
- Name the trigger event or insight, briefly and concretely
- Show evidence I've done real work, not just had a feeling
- Acknowledge the obvious objection (one of them) and answer it head-on
- End with what I'm running TOWARD, not what I'm running from

## THINK
Before producing the output: (a) check whether you are pattern-matching career-coach advice or actually engaging with the user's specific situation, (b) identify the move that is conventional but wrong for them, (c) name what the user is most likely overestimating or underestimating about the market.

## RULES
- No "I'm passionate about" or "I found my true calling." Both signal the opposite.
- Do not bash the old industry. Interviewers don't want to hire someone who'll bash them next.
- The trigger must be specific (a project, a moment, a year of evidence) - not "I realized."
- If the real reason is burnout or a bad boss, do not say so. Reframe to the affirmative pull.
- Do not over-explain. The narrative should leave room for a follow-up question, not preempt all of them.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Is the advice specific enough that the user could act on it Monday, or is it generic career-coach material? (2) Did I avoid coaching them to lie about their actual situation? If either, revise.

## OUTPUT
The 2-minute spoken version, the 4-sentence written version, and the 1-line answer to "but why now?"
notes: The rule against bashing the old industry and against 'passionate' language are the two most important constraints here.

title: Networking Coffee Request That Doesn't Waste Anyone's Time
use_when: Reaching out to someone you don't know for a 20-minute conversation - and writing a note that gets answered.
prompt:
Draft my networking outreach.

Inputs:
- Who I'm reaching out to: [name, role, company]
- How I found them or what connects us: [we both worked at LastCo - they were senior to me; OR we have a mutual connection [name]; OR I read their post on X]
- What I actually want from the conversation: [I'm exploring a move into [field] and want their perspective on the realities; OR I'm interested in their company specifically; OR I want their feedback on my approach]
- What I will NOT ask for: [a referral, an introduction to their boss, a job - this is reconnaissance, not a transaction]
- My credibility hook in one sentence: [why this person would think this conversation is interesting for them too]

Draft the message in 5 sentences:
- Sentence 1: Why I'm reaching out specifically to them (not "I saw your profile")
- Sentence 2: Who I am, briefly and concretely
- Sentence 3: The specific thing I want their perspective on (not "advice" - a real question)
- Sentence 4: The ask - 20 minutes, virtual or in person, on their schedule
- Sentence 5: A clean exit if they pass - no pressure

RULES:
- Do not use the words "pick your brain," "tap into your expertise," "leverage your network," "quick call."
- The specific question in sentence 3 must be one this person is uniquely positioned to answer. If it's googleable, replace it.
- Do not flatter past sentence 1. Flattery escalates suspicion.
- If a referral or a job is what I actually want, the message must NOT pretend otherwise. Either ask for that directly elsewhere or accept this is purely informational.
- 120 words max. Senior people skim.
notes: Banning 'pick your brain' is non-negotiable. The 'not transactional' rule is what makes these actually get answered.

title: Post-Interview Thank-You That Earns a Second Read
use_when: After an interview, sending a note that does work instead of just checking a box.
prompt:
Draft my post-interview thank-you email.

Inputs:
- Interviewer's name and role: [Maya, Engineering Manager]
- Interview format: [60 min, 1:1, technical + behavioral mix]
- One specific thing we discussed where I had a strong moment: [we went deep on how I handled the [X] incident; she asked good follow-ups]
- One specific thing we discussed where I want a second swing: [she asked about my approach to onboarding and I gave a generic answer]
- The role-specific concern I sensed (or know): [they want someone who can also do some IC work, not just manage]
- What I want her to do after reading this: [feel confident advocating for me in the debrief]

Draft the email in 4 short paragraphs:
- Para 1: Specific thank-you tied to one moment from the conversation (not generic)
- Para 2: The second-swing - a sharper version of the answer I wish I'd given, in 2-3 sentences
- Para 3: One thing about the role or team I'm now MORE excited about based on our talk, with specifics
- Para 4: A clean close, no needy follow-up about timeline

RULES:
- Do not start with "Thank you for taking the time to meet with me today." Banned opening.
- The second-swing must not start with "I realized after our conversation..." - that frames me as unprepared. Bring the better answer in naturally.
- 200 words max. They are reading 6 of these.
- No emoji. No exclamation points except maybe one.
- Send within 12 hours.
notes: The 'second-swing' move is what separates a memorable thank-you from a template. The banned opener is critical.

title: Performance Review Self-Assessment
use_when: Writing your own review when your manager has asked for one, and it actually moves the rating or the comp.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a performance review self-assessment that does work in the calibration room - gives the user's manager concrete material to argue for a higher rating with.

## AGENT
You are a manager-coach who has read hundreds of self-assessments and watched which ones moved calibration ratings. You know that self-assessments are written for the calibration room, not the user's manager, and you write accordingly.

## SITUATION
- My role and level: [Senior PM, L5]
- Review period: [past 12 months]
- Company rating scale: [1-5, where 3 is "meets," 4 is "exceeds," 5 is exceptional - 4 is what unlocks promo path]
- The rating I think I earned: [solid 4]
- The rating I'm worried I'll get: [3 or 3.5 if my manager defaults to conservative]
- My manager's communication style: [evidence-driven, dislikes hyperbole, will downgrade for self-aggrandizing tone]
- The top 3-4 things I did this year, with rough impact: [list]
- The things that did NOT go well: [list - I will be honest]

## TASK
Draft the self-assessment.

STRUCTURE:
1. The 1-sentence summary of my year - direct, no hedging
2. The 3-4 highlights, each formatted as: scope -> what I did -> measurable outcome -> what it changed for the business or team
3. What did not go well - real, not "I worked too hard." For each, what I learned or did differently going forward
4. The growth area I'm investing in for next year and what evidence will look like
5. A short paragraph on rating - I don't ask for a number, but I make the case for the band of work I delivered

## THINK
Before producing the output: (a) check whether you are pattern-matching career-coach advice or actually engaging with the user's specific situation, (b) identify the move that is conventional but wrong for them, (c) name what the user is most likely overestimating or underestimating about the market.

## RULES
- No self-aggrandizing language. "Drove transformational impact" is a tell. Specifics beat adjectives every time.
- Do not list everything I did. List what mattered. Five bullets that mattered outperform fifteen that didn't.
- The "didn't go well" section must contain a real miss. If I list a fake weakness ("I work too hard on details"), my manager will discount the whole document.
- Every claim of impact gets a number, a name, or a specific artifact. No "improved team velocity."
- Do not compare myself to peers. Compare to the level expectations.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Is the advice specific enough that the user could act on it Monday, or is it generic career-coach material? (2) Did I avoid coaching them to lie about their actual situation? If either, revise.

## OUTPUT
The self-assessment, ready to paste into the review tool, plus a one-line note on the conversation strategy if my manager pushes back on the rating.
notes: The 'real miss' rule is what makes managers trust the rest of the document.

title: Promotion Case Document
use_when: Making the written case for your promotion - what your manager will use to defend you in calibration.
prompt:
## MISSION
Produce a written promotion case the user's manager can submit verbatim or with minor edits - concrete examples, scope demonstrated at the target level, no hand-waving.

## AGENT
You are a promotion-committee veteran who has written and read dozens of promotion packets at multiple companies. You know which evidence types committees weight and which ones get dismissed, and you know how to make the user's manager their best advocate.

## SITUATION
- Current level and target: [L5 -> L6, IC Engineering track]
- Time at current level: [2.2 years]
- The official criteria for the target level: [paste the rubric if I have it, or describe what's expected]
- My manager's read: [supportive, but needs ammunition for calibration]
- Calibration room dynamic: [other managers will compare me to their L6 candidates - I need to look like I'm already operating there]
- My 3-4 strongest cases of L6-level work in the past 12 months: [list with rough scope]
- The likely critique that will come up: [I've owned big systems but haven't mentored as much - need to address]

## TASK
Draft the promotion case as the document my manager will paste into the calibration tool.

STRUCTURE:
1. The 2-sentence ask: I am proposing X for promotion to Y based on Z body of work.
2. The 3-4 case studies, each:
   - Scope (system, team, business surface area)
   - What was hard about it (not just what I did, but why an L5 wouldn't have)
   - My specific contribution vs the team's contribution (calibration committees will probe this)
   - Outcome with named numbers
3. Operating at level - how I show up in cross-team work, mentorship, ambiguity, technical judgment
4. The known gap and the evidence I am addressing it
5. References within the company who can vouch (the names my manager can drop in committee)

## THINK
Before producing the output: (a) check whether you are pattern-matching career-coach advice or actually engaging with the user's specific situation, (b) identify the move that is conventional but wrong for them, (c) name what the user is most likely overestimating or underestimating about the market.

## RULES
- Each case study must clearly differentiate my work from the team's. "We shipped X" loses promotion cases.
- Address the known gap explicitly. Calibration committees will find it whether or not I name it - I'd rather frame the framing.
- Do not list low-value work to pad. Calibration veterans see filler and discount the whole packet.
- Use the company's language for the rubric. Match their words.
- Total length: 1-2 pages, not 5.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: (1) Is the advice specific enough that the user could act on it Monday, or is it generic career-coach material? (2) Did I avoid coaching them to lie about their actual situation? If either, revise.

## OUTPUT
The case doc + a 3-bullet talking-points card my manager can use in the room.
notes: Differentiating individual contribution from team contribution is the single most common promotion-packet failure.

title: Layoff or Departure Announcement on LinkedIn
use_when: Posting after a layoff or planned departure - keeping dignity, signaling clearly to recruiters, not oversharing.
prompt:
Draft my LinkedIn post.

Situation:
- What happened: [I was part of a 12% layoff at [Company] last Thursday / I'm leaving [Company] after [years] on my own terms]
- My role: [Senior Designer]
- Tenure: [3 years]
- The feeling I'm working through: [be honest with me - blindsided, relieved, angry, proud, scared]
- What I want from the post: [recruiters to message me; former colleagues to recommend me; my network to know I'm available]
- What I do NOT want: [pity; a flood of "DM me" messages with no substance; a public airing of grievances]
- What I'm open to next: [Senior or Staff design at product-led B2B SaaS; remote or NYC; willing to talk about anything in that range]

DRAFT THE POST in 5 short paragraphs:
- Para 1: The fact, stated calmly. No surprise framing, no melodrama.
- Para 2: What I did at the company that I'm proud of, in 1-2 specific lines
- Para 3: What I learned or who I'm grateful to - specific, not a generic thank-you list
- Para 4: What I'm looking for next - specific enough that a recruiter knows whether to reach out
- Para 5: How to contact me and what a useful message looks like

RULES:
- No bitterness, no naming who let me go. The post is forever.
- No "everything happens for a reason" or "blessing in disguise." Both ring false.
- No emoji storm. Maybe one.
- The "what's next" paragraph must include real specifics - role level, company type, geography. Vague gets ignored.
- Do not list the layoff number or company size unless directly relevant. The post is about me forward, not the company past.
- 250 words max.

Return the post + a 2-line follow-up DM template I use when recruiters reach out.
notes: Specific 'what's next' wording is what turns sympathy reactions into actual recruiter outreach.

title: Quitting Conversation Script with Your Manager
use_when: You've signed the new offer or made the decision. Now you have to tell your manager - and you want it clean.
prompt:
## MISSION
Prepare the user for the resignation conversation so they leave with their reputation intact and their manager spending the next week implementing the transition, not litigating the decision.

## AGENT
You are a career coach who has prepped many people for the resignation conversation. You know that the goal is a clean exit, not a debate or a counter-offer negotiation, and you know how to manage the manager's emotional reaction so it doesn't derail the meeting.

## SITUATION
- My relationship with my manager: [good, mutual respect / strained / new manager who doesn't know me well]
- How long I've been here: [4 years]
- Tenure of my manager: [my manager for 2 of those 4 years]
- Why I'm actually leaving: [growth ceiling / pay / manager / new opportunity I couldn't refuse - be honest with me, not in the script]
- Whether I'd consider a counter-offer: [no / open if they meet specific terms]
- My start date at the new place: [4 weeks from the conversation]
- The notice my contract or norms require: [2 weeks formal, but I'm willing to offer up to 4]
- What I want the relationship to look like 1 year from now: [warm; reference-strong]

## TASK
Draft the resignation conversation.

DELIVER:
1. The setup - how I get the meeting on the calendar without alarming language
2. The opening 30 seconds - direct, no burying the lede in pleasantries
